revision.comment: adding map -- southern and northern capitals?
revision.text:
The {{nihongo|'''Southern Court'''|南_|''Nanchō''}}, also known as the '''Ashikaga Pretenders''' or '''Northern Pretenders''', were a set of six [[pretender]]s to the throne of [[Japan]] during the [[Nanboku-chō period]] (1336 to 1392). [[Image:Nanbokucho-capitals.svg|thumb|left|The Imperial seats during the ''Nanboku-chō'' period were in relatively close proximity, but geographically distinct. They were conventionally identified as identifiable:
  • Northern capital : [[Kyoto]]
  • Southern capital : [[Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]].]] The origins of the Northern Court go back to [[Emperor Go-Saga]], reigning from 1242 to 1246. Go-Saga was succeeded in turn by two of his sons, [[Emperor Go-Fukakusa]] and [[Emperor Kameyama]]. The descendants of these two competed with each other for the throne. Go-Fukakusa's descendants were referred to as the {{nihongo|Jimyōin-tō|持明院統}} while Kameyama's descendants were known as the {{nihongo|Daikakuji-tō|大覚寺統}}. In 1333, when [[Emperor Go-Daigo]] (from the Daikakuji-tō) staged the [[Kemmu Restoration]] and revolted against the [[Kamakura shogunate]], the [[Shogun|Shōgun]] responded by declaring [[Emperor Kōgon]], Go-Daigo's [[second cousin once removed]] and the son of an earlier emperor, [[Emperor Go-Fushimi]] of the Jimyōin-tō, as the new emperor. After the destruction of the Kamakura shogunate in 1333, Kōgon lost his claim, but his brother, [[Emperor Kōmyō]], and two of his sons were supported by the new [[Ashikaga shogunate|Ashikaga shoguns]] as the rightful claimants to the throne. Kōgon's family thus comprised an Imperial Court in Kyoto; and this came to be called the Northern Court because its seat was in a location north of its rival. ==Southern Court== The Imperial Court supported by the Ashikaga shoguns was rivalled by the [[Southern Court]] (''Nanchō'') of Go-Daigo and his descendants. This came to be called the Southern Court because its seat was in a location south of its rival. Although the location did change, it was often identified as [[Yoshino]]. In 1392, [[Emperor Go-Kameyama]] of the Southern Court was defeated and abdicated in favor of Kōgon's great-grandson, [[Emperor Go-Komatsu]], thus ending the divide. ==Re-unification of Imperial Courts== Go-Kameyama signed an agreement with Go-Komatsu to return to the old alternations on a ten-year plan. However, Go-Komatsu broke this promise, not only ruling for 20 years, but being succeeded by his own son, rather than by one from the former Southern Court. The ancestors of all the subsequent emperors, the Northern Court was for centuries afterward officially described as the true Imperial Family. However, since 1911, the Japanese government has declared the southern claimants were actually the rightful emperors because they retained possession of the [[Imperial Regalia of Japan|three sacred treasures]], making these six officially pretenders, though they were still members of the imperial family. ===Northern Pretenders=== These are the ''Hokuchō'' or Northern Court emperors: *[[Emperor Kogon|Emperor Kōgon]] 1332-1333 *''No Northern Court'' *[[Emperor Kōmyō]] 1336-1348 *[[Emperor Sukō]] 1348-1351 *''Brief interregnum'' *[[Emperor Go-Kogon|Emperor Go-Kōgon]] 1352-1371 *[[Emperor Go-En'yu|Emperor Go-En'yū]] 1371-1382 *[[Emperor Go-Komatsu]] 1382-1392 (then went on to reign as legitimate emperor 1392-1412) ===Southern Court Emperors=== These are the ''Nanchō'' or Southern Court emperors: *[[Emperor Go-Daigo]] 1336-1339 *[[Emperor Go-Murakami]] 1339-1368 *[[Emperor Chokei|Emperor Chōkei]] 1368-1383 *[[Emperor Go-Kameyama]] 1383-1392 ==See also== * [[Nanboku-cho]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== {{refbegin}} * Mehl, Margaret. (1997). ''History and the State in Nineteenth-Century Japan.'' New York: [[St Martin's Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-312-21160-0; 13-ISBN 978-0-312-21160-8; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/419870136?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 419870136] * Nussbaum, Louis Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2005). ''Japan Encyclopedia.'' Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-674-01753-6; 13-ISBN 978-0-674-01753-5; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/48943301/editions?editionsView=true&referer=br OCLC 48943301] * Thomas, Julia Adeney. (2001). ''Reconfiguring Modernity: Concepts of Nature in Japanese Political Ideology.'' Berkeley: [[University of California Press]]. 10-ISBN 0520228545; 13-ISBN 9780520228542; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/47916285 OCLC 47916285] * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh]], Isaac, ed. (1834). [Siyun-sai Rin-siyo/[[Hayashi Gahō]], 1652], ''[[Nipon o daï itsi ran]]; ou, [http://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran Annales des empereurs du Japon.''] Paris: [[Royal Asiatic Society|Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland]]. {{refend}} [[Category:Feudal Japan]] [[Category:Japanese nobility]] [[Category:Former countries in Japanese history]] [[es:Corte del Norte (Japón)]] [[eu:Iparraldeko Gortea (Japonia)]] [[fr:Cour du Nord]] [[ja:北朝 (日本)]] [[pl:Dwór północny i południowy]]

revision.comment: citation support for first paragraph
revision.text:
The {{nihongo|'''Southern Court'''|南朝|''Nanchō''}} were a set of four emperors whose legitimate claims were forcefully challenged during the [[Nanboku-chō period]] from 1336 through 1392.Nussbaum, Louis Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2005). ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 251; n.b., Louis-Frédéric is pseudonym of Louis-Frédéric Nussbaum, ''see'' [http://dispatch.opac.ddb.de/DB=4.1/PPN?PPN=128842709 Deutsche Nationalbibliothek Authority File]. ==Nanboku-chō overview== [[Image:Nanbokucho-capitals.svg|thumb|right|160px|The Imperial seats during the ''Nanboku-chō'' period were in relatively close proximity, but geographically distinct. They were conventionally identified as identifiable:
  • Northern capital : [[Kyoto]]
  • Southern capital : [[Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]].]] The genesis of the Northern Court go back to [[Emperor Go-Saga]], who reigned from 1242 through 1246.Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). ''Annales des empereurs du japon,'' pp. 245-247. Go-Saga was succeeded in turn by two of his sons, [[Emperor Go-Fukakusa]]Titsingh, pp. 248-255. and [[Emperor Kameyama]].Titsingh, pp. 255-261. On his death bed in 1272, Go-Saga insisted that his sons adopt a plan in which future emperors from the two fraternal lines would ascend the throne in alternating succession.Titsingh, p. 261. This plan proved to be unworkable, resulting in rival factions and rival claimants to the throne. Go-Fukakusa's descendants were referred to as the {{nihongo|Jimyōin-tō|持明院統}} while Kameyama's descendants were known as the {{nihongo|Daikakuji-tō|大覚寺統}}. During the [[Meiji period]], an Imperial decree dated March 3, 1911 established that the legitimate reigning monarchs of this period were the direct descendants of [[Emperor Go-Daigo]] through [[Emperor Go-Murakami]], whose {{nihongo|Southern Court|南朝|''nanchō''}} had been established in exile in [[Yoshino District|Yoshino]], near Nara.Thomas, Julia Adeney. (2001). [http://books.google.com/books?id=Re4djF3oaTMC&pg=RA1-PA199&dq=1911+texbook+controversy#v=onepage&q=&f=false ''Reconfiguring modernity: concepts of nature in Japanese political ideology,'' p. 199 n57,] citing Mehl, Margaret. (1997). ''History and the State in Nineteenth-Century Japan.'' p. 140-147. Until the end of the [[Edo period]], the militarily superior pretender-Emperors supported by the [[Ashikaga shogunate]] had been mistakenly incorporated in Imperial chronologies despite the undisputed fact that the [[Imperial Regalia of Japan|Imperial Regalia]] were not in their possession.. This illegitimate {{nihongo|[[Northern Court]]|北朝|''hokuchō''}} had been established in [[Kyoto]] by [[Ashikaga Takauji]]. ===Northern Pretenders=== These are the ''Hokuchō'' or Northern Court emperors: *[[Emperor Kogon|Emperor Kōgon]] 1332-1333.Titsingh, pp. 286-289. * – *[[Emperor Kōmyō]] 1336-1348.Titsingh, pp. 294-298. *[[Emperor Sukō]] 1348-1351.Titsingh, pp. 298-301. *– *[[Emperor Go-Kogon|Emperor Go-Kōgon]] 1352-1371.Titsingh, pp. 302-309. *[[Emperor Go-En'yu|Emperor Go-En'yū]] 1371-1382.Titsingh, pp. 310-316, 320. *[[Emperor Go-Komatsu]] 1382-1392 (then went on to reign as legitimate emperor 1392-1412)Titsingh, pp. 317-327. ===Southern Court Emperors=== These are the ''Nanchō'' or Southern Court emperors: *[[Emperor Go-Daigo]] 1336-1339.Titsingh, pp. 281-295; Varley, H. Paul. (1980). ''Jinnō Shōtōki,'' pp. 241-269. *[[Emperor Go-Murakami]] 1339-1368.Titsingh, pp. 295-308; Varley, pp. 269-270. *[[Emperor Chokei|Emperor Chōkei]] 1368-1383.Titsingh, p. 308; Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?client=firefox-a&id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&q=chokei#search_anchor ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 158.] *[[Emperor Go-Kameyama]] 1383-1392.Titsingh, p. 320. ==See also== * [[Nanboku-cho]] == Notes == {{reflist|2}} ==References == {{refbegin}} * Mehl, Margaret. (1997). ''History and the State in Nineteenth-Century Japan.'' New York: [[St Martin's Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-312-21160-0; 13-ISBN 978-0-312-21160-8; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/419870136?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 419870136] * Nussbaum, Louis Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2005). ''Japan Encyclopedia.'' Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-674-01753-6; 13-ISBN 978-0-674-01753-5; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/48943301/editions?editionsView=true&referer=br OCLC 48943301] * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * Thomas, Julia Adeney. (2001). ''Reconfiguring Modernity: Concepts of Nature in Japanese Political Ideology.'' Berkeley: [[University of California Press]]. 10-ISBN 0520228545; 13-ISBN 9780520228542; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/47916285 OCLC 47916285] * {{fr icon}} [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh]], Isaac, ed. (1834). ''[[Nipon o daï itsi ran]]''; ou, [http://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon.''] (écrit par Hayashi Gahō en 1652). Paris: Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/251800045 OCLC 251800045] * Varley, H. Paul , ed. (1980). ''A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns: [[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'' (écrit par [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]] en 1359). New York: Columbia University Press. 10-ISBN 0-231-04940-4; 13-ISBN 978-0-231-04940-5; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/311157159?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 311157159] {{refend}} [[Category:Feudal Japan]] [[Category:Japanese nobility]] [[Category:Former countries in Japanese history]]

The {{nihongo|'''Southern Court'''|南朝|''Nanchō''}} were a set of four emperors whose legitimate claims were forcefully challenged during the [[Nanboku-chō period]] from 1336 through 1392.Nussbaum, Louis Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2005). ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 251; n.b., Louis-Frédéric is a pseudonym of Louis-Frédéric Nussbaum, ''see'' [http://dispatch.opac.ddb.de/DB=4.1/PPN?PPN=128842709 Deutsche Nationalbibliothek Authority File]. In spite of the legitimacy of its claims to the throne, the Southern Court line was permanently replaced in 1392 by the illegitimate [[Northern Court]]. ==Nanboku-chō overview== [[Image:Nanbokucho-capitals.svg|thumb|right|The Imperial seats during the ''Nanboku-chō'' period were in relatively close proximity, but geographically distinct. They were conventionally identified as:

On his death bed in 1272, Go-Saga insisted that his sons adopt a plan in which future emperors from the two fraternal lines would ascend the throne in alternating succession.Titsingh, p. 261. This plan proved to be unworkable, resulting in rival factions and rival claimants to the throne. ''Annales des empereurs du japon,'' pp. 245-247. Go-Saga was succeeded in turn by two of his sons, [[Emperor Go-Fukakusa]]Titsingh, pp. 248-255. and [[Emperor Kameyama]].Titsingh, pp. 255-261. On his death bed in 1272, Go-Saga insisted that his sons adopt a plan in which future emperors from the two fraternal lines would ascend the throne in alternating succession.Titsingh, p. 261. This plan proved to be unworkable, resulting in rival factions and rival claimants to the throne. Go-Fukakusa's descendants were referred to as the {{nihongo|Jimyōin-tō|持明院統}} while Kameyama's descendants were known as the {{nihongo|Daikakuji-tō|大覚寺統}}. During the [[Meiji period]], an Imperial decree dated March 3, 1911 established that the legitimate reigning monarchs of this period were the direct descendants of [[Emperor Go-Daigo]] through [[Emperor Go-Murakami]], whose {{nihongo|Southern Court|南朝|''nanchō''}} had been established in exile in [[Yoshino District|Yoshino]], near Nara.Thomas, Julia Adeney. (2001). [http://books.google.com/books?id=Re4djF3oaTMC&pg=RA1-PA199&dq=1911+texbook+controversy#v=onepage&q=&f=false ''Reconfiguring modernity: concepts of nature in Japanese political ideology,'' p. 199 n57,] citing Mehl, Margaret. (1997). ''History and the State in Nineteenth-Century Japan.'' p. 140-147. This illegitimate {{nihongo|[[Northern Court]]|北朝|''hokuchō''}} had been established in [[Kyoto]] by [[Ashikaga Takauji]]. ==Northern Court== In 1333, when [[Emperor Go-Daigo]] (from the Daikakuji-tō) staged the [[Kemmu Restoration]] and revolted against the [[Kamakura shogunate]], the [[Shogun|Shōgun]] responded by declaring [[Emperor Kōgon]], Go-Daigo's [[second cousin once removed]] and the son of an earlier emperor, [[Emperor Go-Fushimi]] of the Jimyōin-tō, as the new emperor. After the destruction of the Kamakura shogunate in 1333, Kōgon lost his claim, but his brother, [[Emperor Kōmyō]], and two of his sons were supported by the new [[Ashikaga shogunate|Ashikaga shoguns]] as the rightful claimants to the throne. Kōgon's family thus formed an alternate Imperial Court in Kyoto, which came to be called the Northern Court because its seat was in a location north of its rival. ==Southern Court== The Imperial Court supported by the Ashikaga shoguns was rivaled by the {{nihongo|[[Southern Court]]|南朝|''nanchō''}} of Go-Daigo and his descendants. This came to be called the Southern Court because its seat was in a location south of its rival. Although the precise location of the emperors' seat did change, it was often identified as simply [[Yoshino]]. In 1392, [[Emperor Go-Kameyama]] of the Southern Court was defeated and abdicated in favor of Kōgon's great-grandson, [[Emperor Go-Komatsu]], thus ending the divide. ==Re-unification of Imperial Courts== Go-Kameyama reached an agreement with Go-Komatsu to return to the old alternations on a ten-year plan. However, Go-Komatsu broke this promise, not only ruling for 20 years, but being succeeded by his own son, rather than by one from the former Southern Court. During the [[Meiji period]], an Imperial decree dated March 3, 1911 established that the legitimate reigning monarchs of this period were the direct descendants of [[Emperor Go-Daigo]] through [[Emperor Go-Murakami]], whose {{nihongo|Southern Court|南朝|''nanchō''}} had been established in exile in [[Yoshino District|Yoshino]], near Nara.Thomas, Julia Adeney. (2001). [http://books.google.com/books?id=Re4djF3oaTMC&pg=RA1-PA199&dq=1911+texbook+controversy#v=onepage&q=&f=false ''Reconfiguring modernity: concepts of nature in Japanese political ideology,'' p. 199 n57,] citing Mehl, Margaret. (1997). ''History and the State in Nineteenth-Century Japan.'' p. 140-147. ===Southern Court Emperors=== These are the ''Nanchō'' or Southern Court emperors: *[[Emperor Go-Daigo]] 1336-1339.Titsingh, pp. 281-295; Varley, H. Paul. (1980). ''Jinnō Shōtōki,'' pp. 241-269. *[[Emperor Go-Murakami]] 1339-1368.Titsingh, pp. 295-308; Varley, pp. 269-270. *[[Emperor Chokei|Emperor Chōkei]] 1368-1383.Titsingh, p. 308; Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?client=firefox-a&id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&q=chokei#search_anchor ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 158.] *[[Emperor Go-Kameyama]] 1383-1392.Titsingh, p. 320. ===Northern Pretenders=== These are the ''Hokuchō'' or Northern Court emperors: *[[Emperor Kogon|Emperor Kōgon]] 1332-1333.Titsingh, pp. 286-289. * – *[[Emperor Kōmyō]] 1336-1348.Titsingh, pp. 294-298. *[[Emperor Sukō]] 1348-1351.Titsingh, pp. 298-301. *– *[[Emperor Go-Kogon|Emperor Go-Kōgon]] 1352-1371.Titsingh, pp. 302-309. *[[Emperor Go-En'yu|Emperor Go-En'yū]] 1371-1382.Titsingh, pp. 310-316, 320. *[[Emperor Go-Komatsu]] 1382-1392 (then went on to reign as legitimate emperor 1392-1412)Titsingh, pp. 317-327. ==See also== * [[Nanboku-cho]] == Notes == {{reflist|2}} ==References == {{refbegin}} * Mehl, Margaret. (1997). ''History and the State in Nineteenth-Century Japan.'' New York: [[St Martin's Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-312-21160-0; 13-ISBN 978-0-312-21160-8; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/419870136?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 419870136] * Nussbaum, Louis Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2005). ''Japan Encyclopedia.'' Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-674-01753-6; 13-ISBN 978-0-674-01753-5; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/48943301/editions?editionsView=true&referer=br OCLC 48943301] * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * Thomas, Julia Adeney. (2001). ''Reconfiguring Modernity: Concepts of Nature in Japanese Political Ideology.'' Berkeley: [[University of California Press]]. 10-ISBN 0520228545; 13-ISBN 9780520228542; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/47916285 OCLC 47916285] * {{fr icon}} [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh]], Isaac, ed. (1834). ''[[Nipon o daï itsi ran]]''; ou, [http://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon.''] (écrit par Hayashi Gahō en 1652). Paris: Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/251800045 OCLC 251800045] * Varley, H. Paul , ed. (1980). ''A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns: [[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'' (écrit par [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]] en 1359). New York: Columbia University Press. 10-ISBN 0-231-04940-4; 13-ISBN 978-0-231-04940-5; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/311157159?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 311157159] {{refend}} [[Category:Feudal Japan]] [[Category:Japanese nobility]] [[Category:Former countries in Japanese history]] [[ja:南朝 (日本)]]

revision.comment: /* Re-unification of Imperial Courts */ Deleted duplicate sentenceù
revision.text:
The {{nihongo|'''Southern Court'''|南朝|''Nanchō''}} were a set of four emperors whose legitimate claims were forcefully challenged during the [[Nanboku-chō period]] from 1336 through 1392.Nussbaum, Louis Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2005). ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 251; n.b., Louis-Frédéric is a pseudonym of Louis-Frédéric Nussbaum, ''see'' [http://dispatch.opac.ddb.de/DB=4.1/PPN?PPN=128842709 Deutsche Nationalbibliothek Authority File]. In spite of the legitimacy of its claims to the throne, the Southern Court line was permanently replaced in 1392 by the illegitimate [[Northern Court]]. ==Nanboku-chō overview== [[Image:Nanbokucho-capitals.svg|thumb|right|The Imperial seats during the ''Nanboku-chō'' period were in relatively close proximity, but geographically distinct. They were conventionally identified as:
  • Northern capital : [[Kyoto]]
  • Southern capital : [[Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]].]] The genesis of the Northern Court go back to [[Emperor Go-Saga]], who reigned from 1242 through 1246.Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). ''Annales des empereurs du japon,'' pp. 245-247. Go-Saga was succeeded in turn by two of his sons, [[Emperor Go-Fukakusa]]Titsingh, pp. 248-255. and [[Emperor Kameyama]].Titsingh, pp. 255-261. On his death bed in 1272, Go-Saga insisted that his sons adopt a plan in which future emperors from the two fraternal lines would ascend the throne in alternating succession.Titsingh, p. 261. This plan proved to be unworkable, resulting in rival factions and rival claimants to the throne. Go-Fukakusa's descendants were referred to as the {{nihongo|Jimyōin-tō|持明院統}} while Kameyama's descendants were known as the {{nihongo|Daikakuji-tō|大覚寺統}}. During the [[Meiji period]], an Imperial decree dated March 3, 1911 established that the legitimate reigning monarchs of this period were the direct descendants of [[Emperor Go-Daigo]] through [[Emperor Go-Murakami]], whose {{nihongo|Southern Court|南朝|''nanchō''}} had been established in exile in [[Yoshino District|Yoshino]], near Nara.Thomas, Julia Adeney. (2001). [http://books.google.com/books?id=Re4djF3oaTMC&pg=RA1-PA199&dq=1911+texbook+controversy#v=onepage&q=&f=false ''Reconfiguring modernity: concepts of nature in Japanese political ideology,'' p. 199 n57,] citing Mehl, Margaret. (1997). ''History and the State in Nineteenth-Century Japan.'' p. 140-147. After the destruction of the Kamakura shogunate in 1333, Kōgon lost his claim, but his brother, [[Emperor Kōmyō]], and two of his sons were supported by the new [[Ashikaga shogunate|Ashikaga shoguns]] as the rightful claimants to the throne. Kōgon's family thus formed an alternate Imperial Court in Kyoto, which came to be called the Northern Court because its seat was in a location north of its rival. ==Southern Court== The Imperial Court supported by the Ashikaga shoguns was rivaled by the {{nihongo|[[Southern Court]]|南朝|''nanchō''}} of Go-Daigo and his descendants. This came to be called the Southern Court because its seat was in a location south of its rival. Although the precise location of the emperors' seat did change, it was often identified as simply [[Yoshino]]. In 1392, [[Emperor Go-Kameyama]] of the Southern Court was defeated and abdicated in favor of Kōgon's great-grandson, [[Emperor Go-Komatsu]], thus ending the divide. ==Re-unification of Imperial Courts== Go-Kameyama reached an agreement with Go-Komatsu to return to the old alternations on a ten-year plan. However, Go-Komatsu broke this promise, not only ruling for 20 years, but being succeeded by his own son, rather than by one from the former Southern Court. ===Southern Court Emperors=== These are the ''Nanchō'' or Southern Court emperors: *[[Emperor Go-Daigo]] 1336-1339.Titsingh, pp. 281-295; Varley, H. Paul. (1980). ''Jinnō Shōtōki,'' pp. 241-269. *[[Emperor Go-Murakami]] 1339-1368.Titsingh, pp. 295-308; Varley, pp. 269-270. *[[Emperor Chokei|Emperor Chōkei]] 1368-1383.Titsingh, p. 308; Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?client=firefox-a&id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&q=chokei#search_anchor ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 158.] *[[Emperor Go-Kameyama]] 1383-1392.Titsingh, p. 320. ===Northern Pretenders=== These are the ''Hokuchō'' or Northern Court emperors: *[[Emperor Kogon|Emperor Kōgon]] 1332-1333.Titsingh, pp. 286-289. * – *[[Emperor Kōmyō]] 1336-1348.Titsingh, pp. 294-298. *[[Emperor Sukō]] 1348-1351.Titsingh, pp. 298-301. *– *[[Emperor Go-Kogon|Emperor Go-Kōgon]] 1352-1371.Titsingh, pp. 302-309. *[[Emperor Go-En'yu|Emperor Go-En'yū]] 1371-1382.Titsingh, pp. 310-316, 320. *[[Emperor Go-Komatsu]] 1382-1392 (then went on to reign as legitimate emperor 1392-1412)Titsingh, pp. 317-327. ==See also== * [[Nanboku-cho]] == Notes == {{reflist|2}} ==References == {{refbegin}} * Mehl, Margaret. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * Thomas, Julia Adeney. (2001). ''Reconfiguring Modernity: Concepts of Nature in Japanese Political Ideology.'' ''A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns: [[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'' (écrit par [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]] en 1359). New York: Columbia University Press. 10-ISBN 0-231-04940-4; 13-ISBN 978-0-231-04940-5; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/311157159?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 311157159] {{refend}} [[Category:Feudal Japan]] [[Category:Japanese nobility]] [[Category:Former countries in Japanese history]] [[ja:南朝 (日本)]]

(1997). ''History and the State in Nineteenth-Century Japan.'' p. 140-147. The {{nihongo|[[Northern Court]]|北朝|''hokuchō''}} established in [[Kyoto]] by [[Ashikaga Takauji]] is therefore considered illegitimate. ===Southern Court=== The Imperial Court supported by the Ashikaga shoguns was rivaled by the {{nihongo|[[Southern Court]]|南朝|''nanchō''}} of Go-Daigo and his descendants. This came to be called the Southern Court because its seat was in a location south of its rival. Although the precise location of the emperors' seat did change, it was often identified as simply [[Yoshino]]. In 1392, [[Emperor Go-Kameyama]] of the Southern Court was defeated and abdicated in favor of Kōgon's great-grandson, [[Emperor Go-Komatsu]], thus ending the divide. ==Re-unification of Imperial Courts== Go-Kameyama reached an agreement with Go-Komatsu to return to the old alternations on a ten-year plan. However, Go-Komatsu broke this promise, not only ruling for 20 years, but being succeeded by his own son, rather than by one from the former Southern Court. ===Southern Court Emperors=== These are the ''Nanchō'' or Southern Court emperors: *[[Emperor Go-Daigo]] 1336-1339.Titsingh, pp. 281-295; Varley, H. Paul. (1980). ''Jinnō Shōtōki,'' pp. 241-269. *[[Emperor Go-Murakami]] 1339-1368.Titsingh, pp. 295-308; Varley, pp. 269-270. *[[Emperor Chokei|Emperor Chōkei]] 1368-1383.Titsingh, p. 308; Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?client=firefox-a&id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&q=chokei#search_anchor ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 158.] *[[Emperor Go-Kameyama]] 1383-1392.Titsingh, p. 320. ===Northern Pretenders=== These are the ''Hokuchō'' or Northern Court emperors: *[[Emperor Kogon|Emperor Kōgon]] 1332-1333.Titsingh, pp. 286-289. * – *[[Emperor Kōmyō]] 1336-1348.Titsingh, pp. 294-298. *[[Emperor Sukō]] 1348-1351.Titsingh, pp. 298-301. *– *[[Emperor Go-Kogon|Emperor Go-Kōgon]] 1352-1371.Titsingh, pp. 302-309. *[[Emperor Go-En'yu|Emperor Go-En'yū]] 1371-1382.Titsingh, pp. 310-316, 320. *[[Emperor Go-Komatsu]] 1382-1392 (then went on to reign as legitimate emperor 1392-1412)Titsingh, pp. 317-327. ==See also== * [[Nanboku-cho]] == Notes == {{reflist|2}} ==References == {{refbegin}} * Mehl, Margaret. (1997). ''History and the State in Nineteenth-Century Japan.'' New York: [[St Martin's Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-312-21160-0; 13-ISBN 978-0-312-21160-8; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/419870136?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 419870136] * Nussbaum, Louis Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2005). ''Japan Encyclopedia.'' Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-674-01753-6; 13-ISBN 978-0-674-01753-5; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/48943301/editions?editionsView=true&referer=br OCLC 48943301] * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * Thomas, Julia Adeney. (2001). ''Reconfiguring Modernity: Concepts of Nature in Japanese Political Ideology.'' Berkeley: [[University of California Press]]. 10-ISBN 0520228545; 13-ISBN 9780520228542; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/47916285 OCLC 47916285] * {{fr icon}} [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh]], Isaac, ed. (1834). ''[[Nipon o daï itsi ran]]''; ou, [http://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon.''] (écrit par Hayashi Gahō en 1652). Paris: Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/251800045 OCLC 251800045] * Varley, H. Paul , ed. (1980). ''A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns: [[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'' (écrit par [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]] en 1359). New York: Columbia University Press. 10-ISBN 0-231-04940-4; 13-ISBN 978-0-231-04940-5; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/311157159?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 311157159] {{refend}} [[Category:Feudal Japan]] [[Category:Japanese nobility]] [[Category:Former countries in Japanese history]] [[ja:南朝 (日本)]]

revision.comment: Some formal changes
revision.text:
The {{nihongo|'''Southern Court'''|南朝|''Nanchō''}} were a set of four emperors whose legitimate claims were usurped during the [[Nanboku-chō period]] spanning from 1336 through 1392.Nussbaum, Louis Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2005). ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 251; n.b., Louis-Frédéric is a pseudonym of Louis-Frédéric Nussbaum, ''see'' [http://dispatch.opac.ddb.de/DB=4.1/PPN?PPN=128842709 Deutsche Nationalbibliothek Authority File]. In spite of the legitimacy of its claims to the throne, the Southern Court was permanently replaced in 1392 by the illegitimate [[Northern Court]]. ==Nanboku-chō overview== [[Image:Nanbokucho-capitals.svg|thumb|right|The Imperial seats during the ''Nanboku-chō'' period were in relatively close proximity, but geographically distinct. They were conventionally identified as:
  • Northern capital : [[Kyoto]]
  • Southern capital : [[Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]].]] The genesis of the Northern Court go back to [[Emperor Go-Saga]], who reigned from 1242 through 1246.Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). ''Annales des empereurs du japon,'' pp. 245-247. Go-Saga was succeeded by two of his sons, [[Emperor Go-Fukakusa]]Titsingh, pp. 248-255. and [[Emperor Kameyama]], who took turns on the throne.Titsingh, pp. 255-261. This because on his death bed in 1272, Go-Saga had insisted that his sons adopt a plan in which future emperors from the two fraternal lines would ascend the throne in alternating succession.Titsingh, p. 261. This plan proved to be unworkable, resulting in rival factions and rival claimants to the throne. Go-Fukakusa's descendants were referred to as the {{nihongo|''Jimyōin-tō''|持明院統}} while Kameyama's descendants were known as the {{nihongo|''Daikakuji-tō''|大覚寺統}}. ==Northern Court== In 1333, when [[Emperor Go-Daigo]] (from the Daikakuji-tō) staged the [[Kemmu Restoration]] and revolted against the [[Kamakura shogunate]], the [[Shogun|Shōgun]] responded by declaring [[Emperor Kōgon]], Go-Daigo's [[second cousin once removed]] and the son of an earlier emperor, [[Emperor Go-Fushimi]] of the ''Jimyōin-tō'', as the new emperor. After the destruction of the Kamakura shogunate in 1333, Kōgon lost his claim, but his brother, [[Emperor Kōmyō]], and two of his sons were supported by the new [[Ashikaga shogunate|Ashikaga shoguns]] as the rightful claimants to the throne. Kōgon's family thus formed an alternate Imperial Court in Kyoto, which came to be called the Northern Court because its seat was in a location north of its rival. During the [[Meiji period]], an Imperial decree dated March 3, 1911 established that the legitimate reigning monarchs of this period were the direct descendants of [[Emperor Go-Daigo]] through [[Emperor Go-Murakami]], whose {{nihongo|Southern Court|南朝|''nanchō''}} had been established in exile in [[Yoshino District|Yoshino]], near Nara.Thomas, Julia Adeney. (2001). [http://books.google.com/books?id=Re4djF3oaTMC&pg=RA1-PA199&dq=1911+texbook+controversy#v=onepage&q=&f=false ''Reconfiguring modernity: concepts of nature in Japanese political ideology,'' p. 199 n57,] citing Mehl, Margaret. (1997). ''History and the State in Nineteenth-Century Japan.'' p. 140-147. The {{nihongo|[[Northern Court]]|北朝|''hokuchō''}} established in [[Kyoto]] by [[Ashikaga Takauji]] is therefore considered illegitimate. ===Northern Pretenders=== These are the ''Hokuchō'' or Northern Court emperors: *[[Emperor Kogon|Emperor Kōgon]] 1332-1333.Titsingh, pp. 286-289. * – *[[Emperor Kōmyō]] 1336-1348.Titsingh, pp. 294-298. *[[Emperor Sukō]] 1348-1351.Titsingh, pp. 298-301. *– *[[Emperor Go-Kogon|Emperor Go-Kōgon]] 1352-1371.Titsingh, pp. 302-309. *[[Emperor Go-En'yu|Emperor Go-En'yū]] 1371-1382.Titsingh, pp. 310-316, 320. *[[Emperor Go-Komatsu]] 1382-1392 (then went on to reign as legitimate emperor 1392-1412)Titsingh, pp. 317-327. ==Southern Court== The Imperial Court supported by the Ashikaga shoguns was rivaled by the {{nihongo|[[Southern Court]]|南朝|''nanchō''}} of Go-Daigo and his descendants. This came to be called the Southern Court because its seat was in a location south of its rival. Although the precise location of the emperors' seat did change, it was often identified as simply [[Yoshino]]. In 1392, [[Emperor Go-Kameyama]] of the Southern Court was defeated and abdicated in favor of Kōgon's great-grandson, [[Emperor Go-Komatsu]], thus ending the divide. ===Southern Court Emperors=== These are the ''Nanchō'' or Southern Court emperors: *[[Emperor Go-Daigo]] 1336-1339.Titsingh, pp. 281-295; Varley, H. Paul. (1980). ''Jinnō Shōtōki,'' pp. 241-269. *[[Emperor Go-Murakami]] 1339-1368.Titsingh, pp. 295-308; Varley, pp. 269-270. *[[Emperor Chokei|Emperor Chōkei]] 1368-1383.Titsingh, p. 308; Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?client=firefox-a&id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&q=chokei#search_anchor ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 158.] *[[Emperor Go-Kameyama]] 1383-1392.Titsingh, p. 320. ==Re-unification of Imperial Courts== Go-Kameyama reached an agreement with Go-Komatsu to return to the old alternations on a ten-year plan. However, Go-Komatsu broke this promise, not only ruling for 20 years, but being succeeded by his own son, rather than by one from the former Southern Court. ==See also== * [[Nanboku-cho period]] == Notes == {{reflist|2}} ==References == {{refbegin}} * Mehl, Margaret. (1997). ''History and the State in Nineteenth-Century Japan.'' New York: [[St Martin's Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-312-21160-0; 13-ISBN 978-0-312-21160-8; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/419870136?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 419870136] * Nussbaum, Louis Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2005). ''Japan Encyclopedia.'' Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-674-01753-6; 13-ISBN 978-0-674-01753-5; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/48943301/editions?editionsView=true&referer=br OCLC 48943301] * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * Thomas, Julia Adeney. (2001). ''Reconfiguring Modernity: Concepts of Nature in Japanese Political Ideology.'' Berkeley: [[University of California Press]]. 10-ISBN 0520228545; 13-ISBN 9780520228542; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/47916285 OCLC 47916285] * {{fr icon}} [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh]], Isaac, ed. (1834). ''[[Nipon o daï itsi ran]]''; ou, [http://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon.''] (écrit par Hayashi Gahō en 1652). Paris: Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/251800045 OCLC 251800045] * Varley, H. Paul , ed. (1980). ''A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns: [[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'' (écrit par [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]] en 1359). New York: Columbia University Press. 10-ISBN 0-231-04940-4; 13-ISBN 978-0-231-04940-5; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/311157159?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 311157159] {{refend}} [[Category:Feudal Japan]] [[Category:Japanese nobility]] [[Category:Former countries in Japanese history]] [[ja:南朝 (日本)]]

revision.text:
The {{nihongo|'''Southern Court'''|南朝|''Nanchō''}} were a set of four emperors whose legitimate claims were usurped during the [[Nanboku-chō period]] spanning from 1336 through 1392.Nussbaum, Louis Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2005). ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 251; n.b., Louis-Frédéric is a pseudonym of Louis-Frédéric Nussbaum, ''see'' [http://dispatch.opac.ddb.de/DB=4.1/PPN?PPN=128842709 Deutsche Nationalbibliothek Authority File]. They were conventionally identified as:
  • Northern capital : [[Kyoto]]
  • Southern capital : [[Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]].]] The genesis of the Northern Court go back to [[Emperor Go-Saga]], who reigned from 1242 through 1246.Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). ''Annales des empereurs du japon,'' pp. 245-247. Go-Saga was succeeded by two of his sons, [[Emperor Go-Fukakusa]]Titsingh, pp. 248-255. and [[Emperor Kameyama]], who took turns on the throne.Titsingh, pp. 255-261. This because on his death bed in 1272, Go-Saga had insisted that his sons adopt a plan in which future emperors from the two fraternal lines would ascend the throne in alternating succession.Titsingh, p. 261. This plan proved to be unworkable, resulting in rival factions and rival claimants to the throne. Go-Fukakusa's descendants were referred to as the {{nihongo|''Jimyōin-tō''|持明院統}} while Kameyama's descendants were known as the {{nihongo|''Daikakuji-tō''|大覚寺統}}. ==Northern Court== In 1333, when [[Emperor Go-Daigo]] (from the Daikakuji-tō) staged the [[Kemmu Restoration]] and revolted against the [[Kamakura shogunate]], the [[Shogun|Shōgun]] responded by declaring [[Emperor Kōgon]], Go-Daigo's [[second cousin once removed]] and the son of an earlier emperor, [[Emperor Go-Fushimi]] of the ''Jimyōin-tō'', as the new emperor. After the destruction of the Kamakura shogunate in 1333, Kōgon lost his claim, but his brother, [[Emperor Kōmyō]], and two of his sons were supported by the new [[Ashikaga shogunate|Ashikaga shoguns]] as the rightful claimants to the throne. Kōgon's family thus formed an alternate Imperial Court in Kyoto, which came to be called the Northern Court because its seat was in a location north of its rival. During the [[Meiji period]], an Imperial decree dated March 3, 1911 established that the legitimate reigning monarchs of this period were the direct descendants of [[Emperor Go-Daigo]] through [[Emperor Go-Murakami]], whose {{nihongo|Southern Court|南朝|''nanchō''}} had been established in exile in [[Yoshino District|Yoshino]], near Nara.Thomas, Julia Adeney. (2001). [http://books.google.com/books?id=Re4djF3oaTMC&pg=RA1-PA199&dq=1911+texbook+controversy#v=onepage&q=&f=false ''Reconfiguring modernity: concepts of nature in Japanese political ideology,'' p. 199 n57,] citing Mehl, Margaret. (1997). ''History and the State in Nineteenth-Century Japan.'' p. 140-147. The {{nihongo|[[Northern Court]]|北朝|''hokuchō''}} established in [[Kyoto]] by [[Ashikaga Takauji]] is therefore considered illegitimate. ===Northern Pretenders=== These are the ''Hokuchō'' or Northern Court emperors: *[[Emperor Kogon|Emperor Kōgon]] 1332-1333.Titsingh, pp. 286-289. * – *[[Emperor Kōmyō]] 1336-1348.Titsingh, pp. 294-298. *[[Emperor Sukō]] 1348-1351.Titsingh, pp. 298-301. *– *[[Emperor Go-Kogon|Emperor Go-Kōgon]] 1352-1371.Titsingh, pp. 302-309. *[[Emperor Go-En'yu|Emperor Go-En'yū]] 1371-1382.Titsingh, pp. 310-316, 320. *[[Emperor Go-Komatsu]] 1382-1392 (then went on to reign as legitimate emperor 1392-1412)Titsingh, pp. 317-327. ==Southern Court== The Imperial Court supported by the Ashikaga shoguns was rivaled by the {{nihongo|[[Southern Court]]|南朝|''nanchō''}} of Go-Daigo and his descendants. This came to be called the Southern Court because its seat was in a location south of its rival. Although the precise location of the emperors' seat did change, it was often identified as simply [[Yoshino]]. In 1392, [[Emperor Go-Kameyama]] of the Southern Court was defeated and abdicated in favor of Kōgon's great-grandson, [[Emperor Go-Komatsu]], thus ending the divide. The Southern Court of the Japanese Imperial Line, which to this day is seen as legitimate. In fact, as already mentioned, Northern Court members are officially called [[pretenders]]. One Southern Court descendant, [[Kumazawa Hiromichi]], proclaimed himself Japan's Emperor after World War 2, calling Emperor [[Hirohito]] a fraud, as Hirohito's entire line is descended from the Northern Court. Despite this, he was not arrested for [[lèse majesté]], even when donning the Imperial Crest, because he had a [[koseki]] detailing his bloodline back to Go-Daigo in [[Yoshino]], but has been unsuccessful at creating any political change other than sympathy. ===Southern Court Emperors=== These are the ''Nanchō'' or Southern Court emperors: *[[Emperor Go-Daigo]] 1336-1339.Titsingh, pp. 281-295; Varley, H. Paul. (1980). ''Jinnō Shōtōki,'' pp. 241-269. *[[Emperor Go-Murakami]] 1339-1368.Titsingh, pp. 295-308; Varley, pp. 269-270. *[[Emperor Chokei|Emperor Chōkei]] 1368-1383.Titsingh, p. 308; Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?client=firefox-a&id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&q=chokei#search_anchor ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 158.] *[[Emperor Go-Kameyama]] 1383-1392.Titsingh, p. 320. ==Re-unification of Imperial Courts== Go-Kameyama reached an agreement with Go-Komatsu to return to the old alternations on a ten-year plan. However, Go-Komatsu broke this promise, not only ruling for 20 years, but being succeeded by his own son, rather than by one from the former Southern Court. == Notes == {{reflist|2}} ==References == {{refbegin}} * Mehl, Margaret. (1997). ''History and the State in Nineteenth-Century Japan.'' revision.comment: /* Southern Court */ Change
revision.text:
The {{nihongo|'''Southern Court'''|南朝|''Nanchō''}} were a set of four emperors whose legitimate claims were usurped during the [[Nanboku-chō period]] spanning from 1336 through 1392.Nussbaum, Louis Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2005). ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 251; n.b., Louis-Frédéric is a pseudonym of Louis-Frédéric Nussbaum, ''see'' [http://dispatch.opac.ddb.de/DB=4.1/PPN?PPN=128842709 Deutsche Nationalbibliothek Authority File]. In spite of the legitimacy of its claims to the throne, the Southern Court was permanently replaced in 1392 by the illegitimate [[Northern Court]]. ==Nanboku-chō overview== [[Image:Nanbokucho-capitals.svg|thumb|right|The Imperial seats during the ''Nanboku-chō'' period were in relatively close proximity, but geographically distinct. They were conventionally identified as:
  • Northern capital : [[Kyoto]]
  • Southern capital : [[Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]].]] The genesis of the Northern Court go back to [[Emperor Go-Saga]], who reigned from 1242 through 1246.Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). ''Annales des empereurs du japon,'' pp. 245-247. Go-Saga was succeeded by two of his sons, [[Emperor Go-Fukakusa]]Titsingh, pp. 248-255. and [[Emperor Kameyama]], who took turns on the throne.Titsingh, pp. 255-261. This because on his death bed in 1272, Go-Saga had insisted that his sons adopt a plan in which future emperors from the two fraternal lines would ascend the throne in alternating succession.Titsingh, p. 261. This plan proved to be unworkable, resulting in rival factions and rival claimants to the throne. Go-Fukakusa's descendants were referred to as the {{nihongo|''Jimyōin-tō''|持明院統}} while Kameyama's descendants were known as the {{nihongo|''Daikakuji-tō''|大覚寺統}}. ==Northern Court== In 1333, when [[Emperor Go-Daigo]] (from the Daikakuji-tō) staged the [[Kemmu Restoration]] and revolted against the [[Kamakura shogunate]], the [[Shogun|Shōgun]] responded by declaring [[Emperor Kōgon]], Go-Daigo's [[second cousin once removed]] and the son of an earlier emperor, [[Emperor Go-Fushimi]] of the ''Jimyōin-tō'', as the new emperor. After the destruction of the Kamakura shogunate in 1333, Kōgon lost his claim, but his brother, [[Emperor Kōmyō]], and two of his sons were supported by the new [[Ashikaga shogunate|Ashikaga shoguns]] as the rightful claimants to the throne. Kōgon's family thus formed an alternate Imperial Court in Kyoto, which came to be called the Northern Court because its seat was in a location north of its rival. During the [[Meiji period]], an Imperial decree dated March 3, 1911 established that the legitimate reigning monarchs of this period were the direct descendants of [[Emperor Go-Daigo]] through [[Emperor Go-Murakami]], whose {{nihongo|Southern Court|南朝|''nanchō''}} had been established in exile in [[Yoshino District|Yoshino]], near Nara.Thomas, Julia Adeney. (2001). [http://books.google.com/books?id=Re4djF3oaTMC&pg=RA1-PA199&dq=1911+texbook+controversy#v=onepage&q=&f=false ''Reconfiguring modernity: concepts of nature in Japanese political ideology,'' p. 199 n57,] citing Mehl, Margaret. (1997). ''History and the State in Nineteenth-Century Japan.'' p. 140-147. The {{nihongo|[[Northern Court]]|北朝|''hokuchō''}} established in [[Kyoto]] by [[Ashikaga Takauji]] is therefore considered illegitimate. ===Northern Pretenders=== These are the ''Hokuchō'' or Northern Court emperors: *[[Emperor Kogon|Emperor Kōgon]] 1332-1333.Titsingh, pp. 286-289. * – *[[Emperor Kōmyō]] 1336-1348.Titsingh, pp. 294-298. *[[Emperor Sukō]] 1348-1351.Titsingh, pp. 298-301. *– *[[Emperor Go-Kogon|Emperor Go-Kōgon]] 1352-1371.Titsingh, pp. 302-309. *[[Emperor Go-En'yu|Emperor Go-En'yū]] 1371-1382.Titsingh, pp. 310-316, 320. *[[Emperor Go-Komatsu]] 1382-1392 (then went on to reign as legitimate emperor 1392-1412)Titsingh, pp. 317-327. ==Southern Court== The Imperial Court supported by the Ashikaga shoguns was rivaled by the {{nihongo|[[Southern Court]]|南朝|''nanchō''}} of Go-Daigo and his descendants. This came to be called the Southern Court because its seat was in a location south of its rival. Although the precise location of the emperors' seat did change, it was often identified as simply [[Yoshino]]. In 1392, [[Emperor Go-Kameyama]] of the Southern Court was defeated and abdicated in favor of Kōgon's great-grandson, [[Emperor Go-Komatsu]], thus ending the divide. The Southern Court of the Japanese Imperial Line is nonetheless to this day seen as legitimate. In fact, as already mentioned, Northern Court members are officially called [[pretenders]]. One Southern Court descendant, [[Kumazawa Hiromichi]], proclaimed himself Japan's Emperor after World War 2, calling Emperor [[Hirohito]] a fraud, as Hirohito's entire line is descended from the Northern Court. Despite this, he was not arrested for [[lèse majesté]], even when donning the Imperial Crest, because he had a [[koseki]] detailing his bloodline back to Go-Daigo in [[Yoshino]], but has been unsuccessful at creating any political change other than sympathy. ===Southern Court Emperors=== These are the ''Nanchō'' or Southern Court emperors: *[[Emperor Go-Daigo]] 1336-1339.Titsingh, pp. 281-295; Varley, H. Paul. (1980). ''Jinnō Shōtōki,'' pp. 241-269. *[[Emperor Go-Murakami]] 1339-1368.Titsingh, pp. 295-308; Varley, pp. 269-270. *[[Emperor Chokei|Emperor Chōkei]] 1368-1383.Titsingh, p. 308; Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?client=firefox-a&id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&q=chokei#search_anchor ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 158.] *[[Emperor Go-Kameyama]] 1383-1392.Titsingh, p. 320. ==Re-unification of Imperial Courts== Go-Kameyama reached an agreement with Go-Komatsu to return to the old alternations on a ten-year plan. However, Go-Komatsu broke this promise, not only ruling for 20 years, but being succeeded by his own son, rather than by one from the former Southern Court. == Notes == {{reflist|2}} ==References == {{refbegin}} * Mehl, Margaret. (1997). ''History and the State in Nineteenth-Century Japan.'' New York: [[St Martin's Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-312-21160-0; 13-ISBN 978-0-312-21160-8; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/419870136?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 419870136] * Nussbaum, Louis Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2005). ''Japan Encyclopedia.'' Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-674-01753-6; 13-ISBN 978-0-674-01753-5; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/48943301/editions?editionsView=true&referer=br OCLC 48943301] * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * Thomas, Julia Adeney. (2001). ''Reconfiguring Modernity: Concepts of Nature in Japanese Political Ideology.'' Berkeley: [[University of California Press]]. 10-ISBN 0520228545; 13-ISBN 9780520228542; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/47916285 OCLC 47916285] * {{fr icon}} [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh]], Isaac, ed. (1834). ''[[Nipon o daï itsi ran]]''; ou, [http://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon.''] (écrit par Hayashi Gahō en 1652). Paris: Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/251800045 OCLC 251800045] * Varley, H. Paul , ed. (1980). ''A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns: [[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'' (écrit par [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]] en 1359). New York: Columbia University Press. 10-ISBN 0-231-04940-4; 13-ISBN 978-0-231-04940-5; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/311157159?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 311157159] {{refend}} [[Category:Feudal Japan]] [[Category:Japanese nobility]] [[Category:Former countries in Japanese history]] [[ja:南朝 (日本)]]

(2001). [http://books.google.com/books?id=Re4djF3oaTMC&pg=RA1-PA199&dq=1911+texbook+controversy#v=onepage&q=&f=false ''Reconfiguring modernity: concepts of nature in Japanese political ideology,'' p. 199 n57,] citing Mehl, Margaret. The {{nihongo|[[Northern Court]]|北朝|''hokuchō''}} established in [[Kyoto]] by [[Ashikaga Takauji]] is therefore considered illegitimate. ===Northern Pretenders=== These are the ''Hokuchō'' or Northern Court emperors: *[[Emperor Kogon|Emperor Kōgon]] 1332-1333.Titsingh, pp. 286-289. * – *[[Emperor Kōmyō]] 1336-1348.Titsingh, pp. 294-298. *[[Emperor Sukō]] 1348-1351.Titsingh, pp. 298-301. *– *[[Emperor Go-Kogon|Emperor Go-Kōgon]] 1352-1371.Titsingh, pp. 302-309. *[[Emperor Go-En'yu|Emperor Go-En'yū]] 1371-1382.Titsingh, pp. 310-316, 320. *[[Emperor Go-Komatsu]] 1382-1392 (then went on to reign as legitimate emperor 1392-1412)Titsingh, pp. 317-327. ==Southern Court== The Imperial Court supported by the Ashikaga shoguns was rivaled by the {{nihongo|[[Southern Court]]|南朝|''nanchō''}} of Go-Daigo and his descendants. The Southern Court of the Japanese Imperial Line is nonetheless seen as legitimate. In fact, Northern Court members are officially called [[pretenders]]. One Southern Court descendant, [[Kumazawa Hiromichi]], declared himself to be Japan's rightful Emperor in the days after the end of the Pacific War. Paul. (1980). ''Jinnō Shōtōki,'' pp. 241-269. *[[Emperor Go-Murakami]] 1339-1368.Titsingh, pp. 295-308; Varley, pp. 269-270. *[[Emperor Chokei|Emperor Chōkei]] 1368-1383.Titsingh, p. 308; Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?client=firefox-a&id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&q=chokei#search_anchor ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 158.] *[[Emperor Go-Kameyama]] 1383-1392.Titsingh, p. 320. ==Re-unification of Imperial Courts== Go-Kameyama reached an agreement with Go-Komatsu to return to the old alternations on a ten-year plan. However, Go-Komatsu broke this promise, not only ruling for 20 years, but being succeeded by his own son, rather than by one from the former Southern Court. == Notes == {{reflist|2}} ==References == {{refbegin}} * Mehl, Margaret. (1997). ''History and the State in Nineteenth-Century Japan.'' New York: [[St Martin's Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-312-21160-0; 13-ISBN 978-0-312-21160-8; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/419870136?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 419870136] * Nussbaum, Louis Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2005). ''Japan Encyclopedia.'' Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-674-01753-6; 13-ISBN 978-0-674-01753-5; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/48943301/editions?editionsView=true&referer=br OCLC 48943301] * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * Thomas, Julia Adeney. (2001). ''Reconfiguring Modernity: Concepts of Nature in Japanese Political Ideology.'' Berkeley: [[University of California Press]]. 10-ISBN 0520228545; 13-ISBN 9780520228542; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/47916285 OCLC 47916285] * {{fr icon}} [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh]], Isaac, ed. (1834). ''[[Nipon o daï itsi ran]]''; ou, [http://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon.''] (écrit par Hayashi Gahō en 1652). ''A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns: [[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'' (écrit par [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]] en 1359). revision.text:
The {{nihongo|'''Southern Court'''|南朝|''Nanchō''}} were a set of four emperors whose legitimate claims were usurped during the [[Nanboku-chō period]] spanning from 1336 through 1392.Nussbaum, Louis Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2005). ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 251; n.b., Louis-Frédéric is a pseudonym of Louis-Frédéric Nussbaum, ''see'' [http://dispatch.opac.ddb.de/DB=4.1/PPN?PPN=128842709 Deutsche Nationalbibliothek Authority File]. In spite of the legitimacy of its claims to the throne, the Southern Court was permanently replaced in 1392 by the illegitimate [[Northern Court]]. ==Nanboku-chō overview== [[Image:Nanbokucho-capitals.svg|thumb|right|The Imperial seats during the ''Nanboku-chō'' period were in relatively close proximity, but geographically distinct. They were conventionally identified as:
  • Northern capital : [[Kyoto]]
  • Southern capital : [[Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]].]] The genesis of the Northern Court go back to [[Emperor Go-Saga]], who reigned from 1242 through 1246.Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). ''Annales des empereurs du japon,'' pp. 245-247. Go-Saga was succeeded by two of his sons, [[Emperor Go-Fukakusa]]Titsingh, pp. 248-255. and [[Emperor Kameyama]], who took turns on the throne.Titsingh, pp. 255-261. This because on his death bed in 1272, Go-Saga had insisted that his sons adopt a plan in which future emperors from the two fraternal lines would ascend the throne in alternating succession.Titsingh, p. 261. This plan proved to be unworkable, resulting in rival factions and rival claimants to the throne. Go-Fukakusa's descendants were referred to as the {{nihongo|''Jimyōin-tō''|持明院統}} while Kameyama's descendants were known as the {{nihongo|''Daikakuji-tō''|大覚寺統}}. ==Northern Court== In 1333, when [[Emperor Go-Daigo]] (from the Daikakuji-tō) staged the [[Kemmu Restoration]] and revolted against the [[Kamakura shogunate]], the [[Shogun|Shōgun]] responded by declaring [[Emperor Kōgon]], Go-Daigo's [[second cousin once removed]] and the son of an earlier emperor, [[Emperor Go-Fushimi]] of the ''Jimyōin-tō'', as the new emperor. After the destruction of the Kamakura shogunate in 1333, Kōgon lost his claim, but his brother, [[Emperor Kōmyō]], and two of his sons were supported by the new [[Ashikaga shogunate|Ashikaga shoguns]] as the rightful claimants to the throne. Kōgon's family thus formed an alternate Imperial Court in Kyoto, which came to be called the Northern Court because its seat was in a location north of its rival. During the [[Meiji period]], an Imperial decree dated March 3, 1911 established that the legitimate reigning monarchs of this period were the direct descendants of [[Emperor Go-Daigo]] through [[Emperor Go-Murakami]], whose {{nihongo|Southern Court|南朝|''nanchō''}} had been established in exile in [[Yoshino District|Yoshino]], near Nara.Thomas, Julia Adeney. (2001). [http://books.google.com/books?id=Re4djF3oaTMC&pg=RA1-PA199&dq=1911+texbook+controversy#v=onepage&q=&f=false ''Reconfiguring modernity: concepts of nature in Japanese political ideology,'' p. 199 n57,] citing Mehl, Margaret. (1997). ''History and the State in Nineteenth-Century Japan.'' p. 140-147. The {{nihongo|[[Northern Court]]|北朝|''hokuchō''}} established in [[Kyoto]] by [[Ashikaga Takauji]] is therefore considered illegitimate. ===Northern Pretenders=== These are the ''Hokuchō'' or Northern Court emperors: *[[Emperor Kogon|Emperor Kōgon]] 1332-1333.Titsingh, pp. 286-289. * – *[[Emperor Kōmyō]] 1336-1348.Titsingh, pp. 294-298. *[[Emperor Sukō]] 1348-1351.Titsingh, pp. 298-301. *– *[[Emperor Go-Kogon|Emperor Go-Kōgon]] 1352-1371.Titsingh, pp. 302-309. *[[Emperor Go-En'yu|Emperor Go-En'yū]] 1371-1382.Titsingh, pp. 310-316, 320. *[[Emperor Go-Komatsu]] 1382-1392 (then went on to reign as legitimate emperor 1392-1412)Titsingh, pp. 317-327. ==Southern Court== The Imperial Court supported by the Ashikaga shoguns was rivaled by the {{nihongo|[[Southern Court]]|南朝|''nanchō''}} of Go-Daigo and his descendants. This came to be called the Southern Court because its seat was in a location south of its rival. Although the precise location of the emperors' seat did change, it was often identified as simply [[Yoshino]]. In 1392, [[Emperor Go-Kameyama]] of the Southern Court was defeated and abdicated in favor of Kōgon's great-grandson, [[Emperor Go-Komatsu]], thus ending the divide. The Southern Court of the Japanese Imperial Line is nonetheless seen as legitimate. In fact, Northern Court members are officially called [[pretenders]]. One Southern Court descendant, [[Kumazawa Hiromichi]], declared himself to be Japan's rightful Emperor in the days after the end of the Pacific War. He claimed that Emperor [[Hirohito]] was a fraud, arguing that Hirohito's entire line is descended from the Northern Court. Despite this, he was not arrested for [[lèse majesté]], even when donning the Imperial Crest. He could and did produce a [[koseki]] detailing his bloodline back to Go-Daigo in [[Yoshino]], but his claims and rhetoric failed to inspire anything other than sympathy.Dower, John W. (1999). [http://books.google.com/books?id=hae0dC_NaiUC&pg=PA306&dq=Kumazawa+Hiromichi&client=firefox-a#v=onepage&q=Kumazawa%20Hiromichi&f=false ''Embracing Defeat: Japan in the Wake of World War II,'' pp. 306-307.] ===Southern Court Emperors=== These are the ''Nanchō'' or Southern Court emperors: *[[Emperor Go-Daigo]] 1336-1339.Titsingh, pp. 281-295; Varley, H. Paul. (1980). ''Jinnō Shōtōki,'' pp. 241-269. *[[Emperor Go-Murakami]] 1339-1368.Titsingh, pp. 295-308; Varley, pp. 269-270. *[[Emperor Chokei|Emperor Chōkei]] 1368-1383.Titsingh, p. 308; Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?client=firefox-a&id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&q=chokei#search_anchor ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 158.] *[[Emperor Go-Kameyama]] 1383-1392.Titsingh, p. 320. ==Re-unification of Imperial Courts== Go-Kameyama reached an agreement with Go-Komatsu to return to the old alternations on a ten-year plan. However, Go-Komatsu broke this promise, not only ruling for 20 years, but being succeeded by his own son, rather than by one from the former Southern Court. == Notes == {{reflist|2}} ==References == {{refbegin}} * Dower, John W. (1999). ''[[Embracing Defeat|Embracing Defeat: Japan in the Wake of World War II]].'' New York: [[W. W. Norton]]. 10-ISBN 0-393-04686-9; 13-ISBN 978-0-393-04686-1; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/39143090 OCLC 39143090] * Mehl, Margaret. (1997). ''History and the State in Nineteenth-Century Japan.'' New York: [[St Martin's Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-312-21160-0; 13-ISBN 978-0-312-21160-8; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/419870136?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 419870136] * Nussbaum, Louis Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2005). ''Japan Encyclopedia.'' Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-674-01753-6; 13-ISBN 978-0-674-01753-5; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/48943301/editions?editionsView=true&referer=br OCLC 48943301] * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * Thomas, Julia Adeney. (2001). ''Reconfiguring Modernity: Concepts of Nature in Japanese Political Ideology.'' Berkeley: [[University of California Press]]. 10-ISBN 0520228545; 13-ISBN 9780520228542; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/47916285 OCLC 47916285] * {{fr icon}} [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh]], Isaac, ed. (1834). ''[[Nipon o daï itsi ran]]''; ou, [http://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon.''] (écrit par Hayashi Gahō en 1652). Paris: Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/251800045 OCLC 251800045] * Varley, H. Paul , ed. (1980). ''A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns: [[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'' (écrit par [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]] en 1359). New York: Columbia University Press. 10-ISBN 0-231-04940-4; 13-ISBN 978-0-231-04940-5; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/311157159?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 311157159] {{refend}} [[Category:Feudal Japan]] [[Category:Japanese nobility]] [[Category:Former countries in Japanese history]] [[ja:南朝 (日本)]]

[http://books.google.com/books?id=Re4djF3oaTMC&pg=RA1-PA199&dq=1911+texbook+controversy#v=onepage&q=&f=false ''Reconfiguring modernity: concepts of nature in Japanese political ideology,'' p. 199 n57,] citing Mehl, Margaret. (1997). ''History and the State in Nineteenth-Century Japan.'' p. 140-147. The {{nihongo|[[Northern Court]]|北朝|''hokuchō''}} established in [[Kyoto]] by [[Ashikaga Takauji]] is therefore considered illegitimate. ===Northern Pretenders=== These are the ''Hokuchō'' or Northern Court emperors: *[[Emperor Kogon|Emperor Kōgon]] 1332-1333.Titsingh, pp. 286-289. * – *[[Emperor Kōmyō]] 1336-1348.Titsingh, pp. 294-298. *[[Emperor Sukō]] 1348-1351.Titsingh, pp. 298-301. *– *[[Emperor Go-Kogon|Emperor Go-Kōgon]] 1352-1371.Titsingh, pp. 302-309. *[[Emperor Go-En'yu|Emperor Go-En'yū]] 1371-1382.Titsingh, pp. 310-316, 320. *[[Emperor Go-Komatsu]] 1382-1392 (then went on to reign as legitimate emperor 1392-1412)Titsingh, pp. 317-327. ==Southern Court== The Imperial Court supported by the Ashikaga shoguns was rivaled by the {{nihongo|Southern Court|南朝|''nanchō''}} of Go-Daigo and his descendants. This came to be called the Southern Court because its seat was in a location south of its rival. Although the precise location of the emperors' seat did change, it was often identified as simply [[Yoshino]]. In 1392, [[Emperor Go-Kameyama]] of the Southern Court was defeated and abdicated in favor of Kōgon's great-grandson, [[Emperor Go-Komatsu]], thus ending the divide. The Southern Court of the Japanese Imperial Line is nonetheless seen as legitimate. In fact, Northern Court members are officially called [[pretenders]]. One Southern Court descendant, [[Kumazawa Hiromichi]], declared himself to be Japan's rightful Emperor in the days after the end of the Pacific War. He claimed that Emperor [[Hirohito]] was a fraud, arguing that Hirohito's entire line is descended from the Northern Court. Despite this, he was not arrested for [[lèse majesté]], even when donning the Imperial Crest. He could and did produce a [[koseki]] detailing his bloodline back to Go-Daigo in [[Yoshino]], but his claims and rhetoric failed to inspire anything other than sympathy.Dower, John W. (1999). [http://books.google.com/books?id=hae0dC_NaiUC&pg=PA306&dq=Kumazawa+Hiromichi&client=firefox-a#v=onepage&q=Kumazawa%20Hiromichi&f=false ''Embracing Defeat: Japan in the Wake of World War II,'' pp. 306-307.] ===Southern Court Emperors=== These are the ''Nanchō'' or Southern Court emperors: *[[Emperor Go-Daigo]] 1336-1339.Titsingh, pp. 281-295; Varley, H. Paul. (1980). ''Jinnō Shōtōki,'' pp. 241-269. *[[Emperor Go-Murakami]] 1339-1368.Titsingh, pp. 295-308; Varley, pp. 269-270. *[[Emperor Chokei|Emperor Chōkei]] 1368-1383.Titsingh, p. 308; Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?client=firefox-a&id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&q=chokei#search_anchor ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 158.] *[[Emperor Go-Kameyama]] 1383-1392.Titsingh, p. 320. ==Re-unification of Imperial Courts== Go-Kameyama reached an agreement with Go-Komatsu to return to the old alternations on a ten-year plan. However, Go-Komatsu broke this promise, not only ruling for 20 years, but being succeeded by his own son, rather than by one from the former Southern Court. == Notes == {{reflist|2}} ==References == {{refbegin}} * Dower, John W. (1999). ''[[Embracing Defeat|Embracing Defeat: Japan in the Wake of World War II]].'' New York: [[W. W. Norton]]. 10-ISBN 0-393-04686-9; 13-ISBN 978-0-393-04686-1; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/39143090 OCLC 39143090] * Mehl, Margaret. (1997). ''History and the State in Nineteenth-Century Japan.'' New York: [[St Martin's Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-312-21160-0; 13-ISBN 978-0-312-21160-8; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/419870136?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 419870136] * Nussbaum, Louis Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2005). ''Japan Encyclopedia.'' Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-674-01753-6; 13-ISBN 978-0-674-01753-5; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/48943301/editions?editionsView=true&referer=br OCLC 48943301] * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * Thomas, Julia Adeney. (2001). ''Reconfiguring Modernity: Concepts of Nature in Japanese Political Ideology.'' Berkeley: [[University of California Press]]. 10-ISBN 0520228545; 13-ISBN 9780520228542; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/47916285 OCLC 47916285] * {{fr icon}} [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh]], Isaac, ed. (1834). ''[[Nipon o daï itsi ran]]''; ou, [http://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon.''] (écrit par Hayashi Gahō en 1652). Paris: Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/251800045 OCLC 251800045] * Varley, H. Paul , ed. (1980). ''A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns: [[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'' (écrit par [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]] en 1359). New York: Columbia University Press. 10-ISBN 0-231-04940-4; 13-ISBN 978-0-231-04940-5; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/311157159?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 311157159] {{refend}} [[Category:Feudal Japan]] [[Category:Japanese nobility]] [[Category:Former countries in Japanese history]] [[ja:南朝 (日本)]]

{{Infobox Former Country |native_name = {{lang|ja|南朝}}
''Nanchō'' |conventional_long_name = Southern Court |common_name = Southern Court |continent = Asia |region = Japan |year_start = 1338 |year_end = 1392 |image_coat =Imperial_Seal_of_Japan.svg |symbol_type = [[Imperial Seal]] |symbol_type_article = Imperial Seal |p1 = Kenmu Restoration |flag_p1 =Imperial_Seal_of_Japan.svg |s1 = Ashikaga shogunate |flag_s1 = Ashikaga_mon.svg |event_start = Fall of [[Kyoto]] |date_start = February 23 |date_end = August 11 |event_end= Surrender of Emperor Go-Kameyama |capital = Yoshino |official language = [[Late Middle Japanese]] |government_type = [[Absolute monarchy]] |title_leader = [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] |leader1 = [[Emperor Go-Daigo|Go-Daigo]] |year_leader1 = 1336-1339 |leader2 = [[Emperor Go-Murakami|Go-Murakami]] |year_leader2 = 1339-1368 |leader3 = [[Emperor Chōkei|Chōkei]] |year_leader3 = 1368-1383 |leader4 = [[Emperor Go-Kameyama|Go-Kameyama]] |year_leader4 = 1383-1392 |religion = [[Shinbutsu shūgō]] |common_languages = [[Late Middle Japanese]] }} The {{nihongo|'''Southern Court'''|南朝|''Nanchō''}} were a set of four emperors whose legitimate claims were usurped during the [[Nanboku-chō period]] spanning from 1336 through 1392.Nussbaum, Louis Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2005). ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 251; n.b., Louis-Frédéric is a pseudonym of Louis-Frédéric Nussbaum, ''see'' [http://dispatch.opac.ddb.de/DB=4.1/PPN?PPN=128842709 Deutsche Nationalbibliothek Authority File]. In spite of the legitimacy of its claims to the throne, the Southern Court was permanently replaced in 1392 by the illegitimate [[Northern Court]]. ==Nanboku-chō overview== [[Image:Nanbokucho-capitals.svg|thumb|right|The Imperial seats during the ''Nanboku-chō'' period were in relatively close proximity, but geographically distinct. They were conventionally identified as:
  • Northern capital : [[Kyoto]]
  • Southern capital : [[Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]].]] The genesis of the Northern Court go back to [[Emperor Go-Saga]], who reigned from 1242 through 1246.Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). ''Annales des empereurs du japon,'' pp. 245-247. Go-Saga was succeeded by two of his sons, [[Emperor Go-Fukakusa]]Titsingh, pp. 248-255. and [[Emperor Kameyama]], who took turns on the throne.Titsingh, pp. 255-261. This because on his death bed in 1272, Go-Saga had insisted that his sons adopt a plan in which future emperors from the two fraternal lines would ascend the throne in alternating succession.Titsingh, p. 261. This plan proved to be unworkable, resulting in rival factions and rival claimants to the throne. Go-Fukakusa's descendants were referred to as the {{nihongo|''Jimyōin-tō''|持明院統}} while Kameyama's descendants were known as the {{nihongo|''Daikakuji-tō''|大覚寺統}}. ==Northern Court== In 1333, when [[Emperor Go-Daigo]] (from the Daikakuji-tō) staged the [[Kemmu Restoration]] and revolted against the [[Kamakura shogunate]], the [[Shogun|Shōgun]] responded by declaring [[Emperor Kōgon]], Go-Daigo's [[second cousin once removed]] and the son of an earlier emperor, [[Emperor Go-Fushimi]] of the ''Jimyōin-tō'', as the new emperor. After the destruction of the Kamakura shogunate in 1333, Kōgon lost his claim, but his brother, [[Emperor Kōmyō]], and two of his sons were supported by the new [[Ashikaga shogunate|Ashikaga shoguns]] as the rightful claimants to the throne. Kōgon's family thus formed an alternate Imperial Court in Kyoto, which came to be called the Northern Court because its seat was in a location north of its rival. During the [[Meiji period]], an Imperial decree dated March 3, 1911 established that the legitimate reigning monarchs of this period were the direct descendants of [[Emperor Go-Daigo]] through [[Emperor Go-Murakami]], whose {{nihongo|Southern Court|南朝|''nanchō''}} had been established in exile in [[Yoshino District|Yoshino]], near Nara.Thomas, Julia Adeney. (2001). [http://books.google.com/books?id=Re4djF3oaTMC&pg=RA1-PA199&dq=1911+texbook+controversy#v=onepage&q=&f=false ''Reconfiguring modernity: concepts of nature in Japanese political ideology,'' p. 199 n57], citing Mehl, Margaret. (1997). ''History and the State in Nineteenth-Century Japan.'' p. 140-147. The {{nihongo|[[Northern Court]]|北朝|''hokuchō''}} established in [[Kyoto]] by [[Ashikaga Takauji]] is therefore considered illegitimate. ===Northern Pretenders=== These are the ''Hokuchō'' or Northern Court emperors: *[[Emperor Kogon|Emperor Kōgon]] 1332-1333.Titsingh, pp. 286-289. * – *[[Emperor Kōmyō]] 1336-1348.Titsingh, pp. 294-298. *[[Emperor Sukō]] 1348-1351.Titsingh, pp. 298-301. *– *[[Emperor Go-Kogon|Emperor Go-Kōgon]] 1352-1371.Titsingh, pp. 302-309. *[[Emperor Go-En'yu|Emperor Go-En'yū]] 1371-1382.Titsingh, pp. 310-316, 320. *[[Emperor Go-Komatsu]] 1382-1392 (then went on to reign as legitimate emperor 1392-1412)Titsingh, pp. 317-327. ==Southern Court== The Imperial Court supported by the Ashikaga shoguns was rivaled by the {{nihongo|Southern Court|南朝|''nanchō''}} of Go-Daigo and his descendants. This came to be called the Southern Court because its seat was in a location south of its rival. Although the precise location of the emperors' seat did change, it was often identified as simply [[Yoshino]]. In 1392, [[Emperor Go-Kameyama]] of the Southern Court was defeated and abdicated in favor of Kōgon's great-grandson, [[Emperor Go-Komatsu]], thus ending the divide. The Southern Court of the Japanese Imperial Line is nonetheless seen as legitimate. In fact, Northern Court members are officially called [[pretenders]]. One Southern Court descendant, [[Kumazawa Hiromichi]], declared himself to be Japan's rightful Emperor in the days after the end of the Pacific War. He claimed that Emperor [[Hirohito]] was a fraud, arguing that Hirohito's entire line is descended from the Northern Court. Despite this, he was not arrested for [[lèse majesté]], even when donning the Imperial Crest. He could and did produce a [[koseki]] detailing his bloodline back to Go-Daigo in [[Yoshino]], but his claims and rhetoric failed to inspire anything other than sympathy.Dower, John W. (1999). [http://books.google.com/books?id=hae0dC_NaiUC&pg=PA306&dq=Kumazawa+Hiromichi&client=firefox-a#v=onepage&q=Kumazawa%20Hiromichi&f=false ''Embracing Defeat: Japan in the Wake of World War II,'' pp. 306-307.] ===Southern Court Emperors=== These are the ''Nanchō'' or Southern Court emperors: *[[Emperor Go-Daigo]] 1336-1339.Titsingh, pp. 281-295; Varley, H. Paul. (1980). ''Jinnō Shōtōki,'' pp. 241-269. *[[Emperor Go-Murakami]] 1339-1368.Titsingh, pp. 295-308; Varley, pp. 269-270. *[[Emperor Chokei|Emperor Chōkei]] 1368-1383.Titsingh, p. 308; Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?client=firefox-a&id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&q=chokei#search_anchor ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 158.] *[[Emperor Go-Kameyama]] 1383-1392.Titsingh, p. 320. ==Re-unification of Imperial Courts== Go-Kameyama reached an agreement with Go-Komatsu to return to the old alternations on a ten-year plan. However, Go-Komatsu broke this promise, not only ruling for 20 years, but being succeeded by his own son, rather than by one from the former Southern Court. == Notes == {{reflist|2}} ==References == {{refbegin}} * Dower, John W. (1999). ''[[Embracing Defeat|Embracing Defeat: Japan in the Wake of World War II]].'' New York: [[W. W. Norton]]. 10-ISBN 0-393-04686-9; 13-ISBN 978-0-393-04686-1; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/39143090 OCLC 39143090] * Mehl, Margaret. (1997). ''History and the State in Nineteenth-Century Japan.'' New York: [[St Martin's Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-312-21160-0; 13-ISBN 978-0-312-21160-8; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/419870136?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 419870136] * Nussbaum, Louis Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2005). ''Japan Encyclopedia.'' Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-674-01753-6; 13-ISBN 978-0-674-01753-5; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/48943301/editions?editionsView=true&referer=br OCLC 48943301] * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * Thomas, Julia Adeney. (2001). ''Reconfiguring Modernity: Concepts of Nature in Japanese Political Ideology.'' Berkeley: [[University of California Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-520-22854-5; 13-ISBN 978-0-520-22854-2; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/47916285 OCLC 47916285] * {{fr icon}} [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh]], Isaac, ed. (1834). ''[[Nipon o daï itsi ran]]''; ou, [http://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon.''] (écrit par Hayashi Gahō en 1652). Paris: Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/251800045 OCLC 251800045] * Varley, H. Paul , ed. (1980). ''A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns: [[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'' (écrit par [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]] en 1359). New York: Columbia University Press. 10-ISBN 0-231-04940-4; 13-ISBN 978-0-231-04940-5; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/311157159?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 311157159] {{refend}} [[Category:Feudal Japan]] [[Category:Japanese nobility]] [[Category:Former countries in Japanese history]] [[ja:南朝 (日本)]]
