''A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns: [[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'' (écrit par [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]] en 1359). New York: Columbia University Press. 10-ISBN 0-231-04940-4; 13-ISBN 978-0-231-04940-5; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/311157159?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 311157159] {{refend}} [[Category:Feudal Japan]] [[Category:Japanese nobility]] [[Category:Former countries in Japanese history]] [[ja:南朝 (日本)]]

revision.comment: sp, date & link fixes; unlinking common words using [[Project:AWB|AWB]]
revision.text:
{{Infobox Former Country |native_name = {{lang|ja|南朝}}
''Nanchō'' |conventional_long_name = Southern Court |common_name = Southern Court |continent = Asia |region = Japan |year_start = 1338 |year_end = 1392 |image_coat =Imperial_Seal_of_Japan.svg |symbol_type = [[Imperial Seal]] |symbol_type_article = Imperial Seal |p1 = Kenmu Restoration |flag_p1 =Imperial_Seal_of_Japan.svg |s1 = Ashikaga shogunate |flag_s1 = Ashikaga_mon.svg |event_start = Fall of [[Kyoto]] |date_start = February 23 |date_end = August 11 |event_end= Surrender of Emperor Go-Kameyama |capital = Yoshino |official language = [[Late Middle Japanese]] |government_type = [[Absolute monarchy]] |title_leader = [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] |leader1 = [[Emperor Go-Daigo|Go-Daigo]] |year_leader1 = 1336-1339 |leader2 = [[Emperor Go-Murakami|Go-Murakami]] |year_leader2 = 1339-1368 |leader3 = [[Emperor Chōkei|Chōkei]] |year_leader3 = 1368-1383 |leader4 = [[Emperor Go-Kameyama|Go-Kameyama]] |year_leader4 = 1383-1392 |religion = [[Shinbutsu shūgō]] |common_languages = [[Late Middle Japanese]] }} The {{nihongo|'''Southern Court'''|南朝|''Nanchō''}} were a set of four emperors whose legitimate claims were usurped during the [[Nanboku-chō period]] spanning from 1336 through 1392.Nussbaum, Louis Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2005). ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 251; n.b., Louis-Frédéric is a pseudonym of Louis-Frédéric Nussbaum, ''see'' [http://dispatch.opac.ddb.de/DB=4.1/PPN?PPN=128842709 Deutsche Nationalbibliothek Authority File]. In spite of the legitimacy of its claims to the throne, the Southern Court was permanently replaced in 1392 by the illegitimate [[Northern Court]]. ==Nanboku-chō overview== [[Image:Nanbokucho-capitals.svg|thumb|right|The Imperial seats during the ''Nanboku-chō'' period were in relatively close proximity, but geographically distinct. They were conventionally identified as:
  • Northern capital : [[Kyoto]]
  • Southern capital : [[Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]].]] The genesis of the Northern Court go back to [[Emperor Go-Saga]], who reigned from 1242 through 1246.Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). ''Annales des empereurs du japon'', pp. 245-247. Go-Saga was succeeded by two of his sons, [[Emperor Go-Fukakusa]]Titsingh, pp. 248-255. and [[Emperor Kameyama]], who took turns on the throne.Titsingh, pp. 255-261. This because on his death bed in 1272, Go-Saga had insisted that his sons adopt a plan in which future emperors from the two fraternal lines would ascend the throne in alternating succession.Titsingh, p. 261. This plan proved to be unworkable, resulting in rival factions and rival claimants to the throne. Go-Fukakusa's descendants were referred to as the {{nihongo|''Jimyōin-tō''|持明院統}} while Kameyama's descendants were known as the {{nihongo|''Daikakuji-tō''|大覚寺統}}. ==Northern Court== In 1333, when [[Emperor Go-Daigo]] (from the Daikakuji-tō) staged the [[Kemmu Restoration]] and revolted against the [[Kamakura shogunate]], the [[Shogun|Shōgun]] responded by declaring [[Emperor Kōgon]], Go-Daigo's [[second cousin once removed]] and the son of an earlier emperor, [[Emperor Go-Fushimi]] of the ''Jimyōin-tō'', as the new emperor. After the destruction of the Kamakura shogunate in 1333, Kōgon lost his claim, but his brother, [[Emperor Kōmyō]], and two of his sons were supported by the new [[Ashikaga shogunate|Ashikaga shoguns]] as the rightful claimants to the throne. Kōgon's family thus formed an alternate Imperial Court in Kyoto, which came to be called the Northern Court because its seat was in a location north of its rival. During the [[Meiji period]], an Imperial decree dated March 3, 1911 established that the legitimate reigning monarchs of this period were the direct descendants of [[Emperor Go-Daigo]] through [[Emperor Go-Murakami]], whose {{nihongo|Southern Court|南朝|''nanchō''}} had been established in exile in [[Yoshino District|Yoshino]], near Nara.Thomas, Julia Adeney. (2001). [http://books.google.com/books?id=Re4djF3oaTMC&pg=RA1-PA199&dq=1911+texbook+controversy#v=onepage&q=&f=false ''Reconfiguring modernity: concepts of nature in Japanese political ideology,'' p. 199 n57], citing Mehl, Margaret. (1997). ''History and the State in Nineteenth-Century Japan.'' p. 140-147. The {{nihongo|[[Northern Court]]|北朝|''hokuchō''}} established in [[Kyoto]] by [[Ashikaga Takauji]] is therefore considered illegitimate. ===Northern Pretenders=== These are the ''Hokuchō'' or Northern Court emperors: *[[Emperor Kōgon]] 1332-1333.Titsingh, pp. 286-289. * – *[[Emperor Kōmyō]] 1336-1348.Titsingh, pp. 294-298. *[[Emperor Sukō]] 1348-1351.Titsingh, pp. 298-301. *– *[[Emperor Go-Kōgon]] 1352-1371.Titsingh, pp. 302-309. *[[Emperor Go-En'yū]] 1371-1382.Titsingh, pp. 310-316, 320. *[[Emperor Go-Komatsu]] 1382-1392 (then went on to reign as legitimate emperor 1392-1412)Titsingh, pp. 317-327. ==Southern Court== The Imperial Court supported by the Ashikaga shoguns was rivaled by the {{nihongo|Southern Court|南朝|''nanchō''}} of Go-Daigo and his descendants. This came to be called the Southern Court because its seat was in a location south of its rival. Although the precise location of the emperors' seat did change, it was often identified as simply [[Yoshino]]. In 1392, [[Emperor Go-Kameyama]] of the Southern Court was defeated and abdicated in favor of Kōgon's great-grandson, [[Emperor Go-Komatsu]], thus ending the divide. The Southern Court of the Japanese Imperial Line is nonetheless seen as legitimate. In fact, Northern Court members are officially called [[pretender]]s. One Southern Court descendant, [[Kumazawa Hiromichi]], declared himself to be Japan's rightful Emperor in the days after the end of the Pacific War. He claimed that Emperor [[Hirohito]] was a fraud, arguing that Hirohito's entire line is descended from the Northern Court. Despite this, he was not arrested for [[lèse majesté]], even when donning the Imperial Crest. He could and did produce a [[koseki]] detailing his bloodline back to Go-Daigo in [[Yoshino]], but his claims and rhetoric failed to inspire anything other than sympathy.Dower, John W. (1999). [http://books.google.com/books?id=hae0dC_NaiUC&pg=PA306&dq=Kumazawa+Hiromichi&client=firefox-a#v=onepage&q=Kumazawa%20Hiromichi&f=false ''Embracing Defeat: Japan in the Wake of World War II,'' pp. 306-307.] ===Southern Court Emperors=== These are the ''Nanchō'' or Southern Court emperors: *[[Emperor Go-Daigo]] 1336-1339.Titsingh, pp. 281-295; Varley, H. Paul. (1980). ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 241-269. *[[Emperor Go-Murakami]] 1339-1368.Titsingh, pp. 295-308; Varley, pp. 269-270. *[[Emperor Chōkei]] 1368-1383.Titsingh, p. 308; Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?client=firefox-a&id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&q=chokei#search_anchor ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 158.] *[[Emperor Go-Kameyama]] 1383-1392.Titsingh, p. 320. ==Re-unification of Imperial Courts== Go-Kameyama reached an agreement with Go-Komatsu to return to the old alternations on a ten-year plan. However, Go-Komatsu broke this promise, not only ruling for 20 years, but being succeeded by his own son, rather than by one from the former Southern Court. == Notes == {{reflist|2}} ==References == {{refbegin}} * Dower, John W. (1999). ''[[Embracing Defeat|Embracing Defeat: Japan in the Wake of World War II]].'' New York: [[W. W. Norton]]. 10-ISBN 0-393-04686-9; 13-ISBN 978-0-393-04686-1; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/39143090 OCLC 39143090] * Mehl, Margaret. (1997). ''History and the State in Nineteenth-Century Japan.'' New York: [[St Martin's Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-312-21160-0; 13-ISBN 978-0-312-21160-8; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/419870136?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 419870136] * Nussbaum, Louis Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2005). ''Japan Encyclopedia.'' Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-674-01753-6; 13-ISBN 978-0-674-01753-5; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/48943301/editions?editionsView=true&referer=br OCLC 48943301] * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * Thomas, Julia Adeney. (2001). ''Reconfiguring Modernity: Concepts of Nature in Japanese Political Ideology.'' Berkeley: [[University of California Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-520-22854-5; 13-ISBN 978-0-520-22854-2; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/47916285 OCLC 47916285] * {{fr icon}} [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh]], Isaac, ed. (1834). ''[[Nipon o daï itsi ran]]''; ou, [http://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon.''] (écrit par Hayashi Gahō en 1652). Paris: Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/251800045 OCLC 251800045] * Varley, H. Paul, ed. (1980). ''A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns: [[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'' (écrit par [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]] en 1359). New York: Columbia University Press. 10-ISBN 0-231-04940-4; 13-ISBN 978-0-231-04940-5; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/311157159?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 311157159] {{refend}} [[Category:Feudal Japan]] [[Category:Japanese nobility]] [[Category:Former countries in Japanese history]] [[ja:南朝 (日本)]]

(1997). ''History and the State in Nineteenth-Century Japan.'' p. 140-147. The {{nihongo|[[Northern Court]]|北朝|''hokuchō''}} established in [[Kyoto]] by [[Ashikaga Takauji]] is therefore considered illegitimate. ===Northern Pretenders=== These are the ''Hokuchō'' or Northern Court emperors: *[[Emperor Kōgon]] 1332-1333.Titsingh, pp. 286-289. * – *[[Emperor Kōmyō]] 1336-1348.Titsingh, pp. 294-298. *[[Emperor Sukō]] 1348-1351.Titsingh, pp. 298-301. *– *[[Emperor Go-Kōgon]] 1352-1371.Titsingh, pp. 302-309. *[[Emperor Go-En'yū]] 1371-1382.Titsingh, pp. 310-316, 320. *[[Emperor Go-Komatsu]] 1382-1392 (then went on to reign as legitimate emperor 1392-1412)Titsingh, pp. 317-327. ==Southern Court== The Imperial Court supported by the Ashikaga shoguns was rivaled by the {{nihongo|Southern Court|南朝|''nanchō''}} of Go-Daigo and his descendants. This came to be called the Southern Court because its seat was in a location south of its rival. Although the precise location of the emperors' seat did change, it was often identified as simply [[Yoshino]]. In 1392, [[Emperor Go-Kameyama]] of the Southern Court was defeated and abdicated in favor of Kōgon's great-grandson, [[Emperor Go-Komatsu]], thus ending the divide. The Southern Court of the Japanese Imperial Line is nonetheless seen as legitimate. In fact, Northern Court members are officially called [[pretender]]s. One Southern Court descendant, [[Kumazawa Hiromichi]], declared himself to be Japan's rightful Emperor in the days after the end of the Pacific War. He claimed that Emperor [[Hirohito]] was a fraud, arguing that Hirohito's entire line is descended from the Northern Court. Despite this, he was not arrested for [[lèse majesté]], even when donning the Imperial Crest. He could and did produce a [[koseki]] detailing his bloodline back to Go-Daigo in [[Yoshino]], but his claims and rhetoric failed to inspire anything other than sympathy.Dower, John W. (1999). [http://books.google.com/books?id=hae0dC_NaiUC&pg=PA306&dq=Kumazawa+Hiromichi&client=firefox-a#v=onepage&q=Kumazawa%20Hiromichi&f=false ''Embracing Defeat: Japan in the Wake of World War II,'' pp. 306-307.] ===Southern Court Emperors=== These are the ''Nanchō'' or Southern Court emperors: *[[Emperor Go-Daigo]] 1336-1339.Titsingh, pp. 281-295; Varley, H. Paul. (1980). ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 241-269. *[[Emperor Go-Murakami]] 1339-1368.Titsingh, pp. 295-308; Varley, pp. 269-270. *[[Emperor Chōkei]] 1368-1383.Titsingh, p. 308; Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?client=firefox-a&id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&q=chokei#search_anchor ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 158.] *[[Emperor Go-Kameyama]] 1383-1392.Titsingh, p. 320. ==Re-unification of Imperial Courts== Go-Kameyama reached an agreement with Go-Komatsu to return to the old alternations on a ten-year plan. However, Go-Komatsu broke this promise, not only ruling for 20 years, but being succeeded by his own son, rather than by one from the former Southern Court. == Notes == {{reflist|2}} ==References == {{refbegin}} * Dower, John W. (1999). ''[[Embracing Defeat|Embracing Defeat: Japan in the Wake of World War II]].'' New York: [[W. W. Norton]]. 10-ISBN 0-393-04686-9; 13-ISBN 978-0-393-04686-1; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/39143090 OCLC 39143090] * Mehl, Margaret. (1997). ''History and the State in Nineteenth-Century Japan.'' New York: [[St Martin's Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-312-21160-0; 13-ISBN 978-0-312-21160-8; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/419870136?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 419870136] * Nussbaum, Louis Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2005). ''Japan Encyclopedia.'' Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-674-01753-6; 13-ISBN 978-0-674-01753-5; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/48943301/editions?editionsView=true&referer=br OCLC 48943301] * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * Thomas, Julia Adeney. (2001). ''Reconfiguring Modernity: Concepts of Nature in Japanese Political Ideology.'' Berkeley: [[University of California Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-520-22854-5; 13-ISBN 978-0-520-22854-2; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/47916285 OCLC 47916285] * {{fr icon}} [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh]], Isaac, ed. (1834). ''[[Nipon o daï itsi ran]]''; ou, [http://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon.''] (écrit par Hayashi Gahō en 1652). Paris: Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/251800045 OCLC 251800045] * Varley, H. Paul, ed. (1980). ''A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns: [[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'' (écrit par [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]] en 1359). New York: Columbia University Press. 10-ISBN 0-231-04940-4; 13-ISBN 978-0-231-04940-5; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/311157159?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 311157159] {{refend}} [[Category:Feudal Japan]] [[Category:Japanese nobility]] [[Category:Former countries in Japanese history]] [[ja:南朝 (日本)]] [[simple:Southern Court]]

{{Infobox Former Country |native_name = {{lang|ja|南朝}}
''Nanchō'' |conventional_long_name = Southern Court |common_name = Southern Court |continent = Asia |region = Japan |year_start = 1338 |year_end = 1392 |image_coat =Imperial_Seal_of_Japan.svg |symbol_type = [[Seal (emblem)|Imperial Seal]] |symbol_type_article = Imperial Seal |p1 = Kenmu Restoration |flag_p1 =Imperial_Seal_of_Japan.svg |s1 = Ashikaga shogunate |flag_s1 = Ashikaga_mon.svg |event_start = Fall of [[Kyoto]] |date_start = February 23 |date_end = August 11 |event_end= Surrender of Emperor Go-Kameyama |capital = Yoshino |official language = [[Late Middle Japanese]] |government_type = [[Absolute monarchy]] |title_leader = [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] |leader1 = [[Emperor Go-Daigo|Go-Daigo]] |year_leader1 = 1336-1339 |leader2 = [[Emperor Go-Murakami|Go-Murakami]] |year_leader2 = 1339-1368 |leader3 = [[Emperor Chōkei|Chōkei]] |year_leader3 = 1368-1383 |leader4 = [[Emperor Go-Kameyama|Go-Kameyama]] |year_leader4 = 1383-1392 |religion = [[Shinbutsu shūgō]] |common_languages = [[Late Middle Japanese]] }} The {{nihongo|'''Southern Court'''|南朝|''Nanchō''}} were a set of four emperors whose legitimate claims were usurped during the [[Nanboku-chō period]] spanning from 1336 through 1392.Nussbaum, Louis Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2005). ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 251; n.b., Louis-Frédéric is a pseudonym of Louis-Frédéric Nussbaum, ''see'' [http://dispatch.opac.ddb.de/DB=4.1/PPN?PPN=128842709 Deutsche Nationalbibliothek Authority File]. In spite of the legitimacy of its claims to the throne, the Southern Court was permanently replaced in 1392 by the illegitimate [[Northern Court]]. ==Nanboku-chō overview== [[Image:Nanbokucho-capitals.svg|thumb|right|The Imperial seats during the ''Nanboku-chō'' period were in relatively close proximity, but geographically distinct. They were conventionally identified as:
  • Northern capital : [[Kyoto]]
  • Southern capital : [[Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]].]] The genesis of the Northern Court go back to [[Emperor Go-Saga]], who reigned from 1242 through 1246.Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). ''Annales des empereurs du japon'', pp. 245-247. Go-Saga was succeeded by two of his sons, [[Emperor Go-Fukakusa]]Titsingh, pp. 248-255. and [[Emperor Kameyama]], who took turns on the throne.Titsingh, pp. 255-261. This because on his death bed in 1272, Go-Saga had insisted that his sons adopt a plan in which future emperors from the two fraternal lines would ascend the throne in alternating succession.Titsingh, p. 261. This plan proved to be unworkable, resulting in rival factions and rival claimants to the throne. Go-Fukakusa's descendants were referred to as the {{nihongo|''Jimyōin-tō''|持明院統}} while Kameyama's descendants were known as the {{nihongo|''Daikakuji-tō''|大覚寺統}}. ==Northern Court== In 1333, when [[Emperor Go-Daigo]] (from the Daikakuji-tō) staged the [[Kemmu Restoration]] and revolted against the [[Kamakura shogunate]], the [[Shogun|Shōgun]] responded by declaring [[Emperor Kōgon]], Go-Daigo's [[second cousin once removed]] and the son of an earlier emperor, [[Emperor Go-Fushimi]] of the ''Jimyōin-tō'', as the new emperor. After the destruction of the Kamakura shogunate in 1333, Kōgon lost his claim, but his brother, [[Emperor Kōmyō]], and two of his sons were supported by the new [[Ashikaga shogunate|Ashikaga shoguns]] as the rightful claimants to the throne. Kōgon's family thus formed an alternate Imperial Court in Kyoto, which came to be called the Northern Court because its seat was in a location north of its rival. During the [[Meiji period]], an Imperial decree dated March 3, 1911 established that the legitimate reigning monarchs of this period were the direct descendants of [[Emperor Go-Daigo]] through [[Emperor Go-Murakami]], whose {{nihongo|Southern Court|南朝|''nanchō''}} had been established in exile in [[Yoshino District|Yoshino]], near Nara.Thomas, Julia Adeney. (2001). [http://books.google.com/books?id=Re4djF3oaTMC&pg=RA1-PA199&dq=1911+texbook+controversy#v=onepage&q=&f=false ''Reconfiguring modernity: concepts of nature in Japanese political ideology,'' p. 199 n57], citing Mehl, Margaret. (1997). ''History and the State in Nineteenth-Century Japan.'' p. 140-147. The {{nihongo|[[Northern Court]]|北朝|''hokuchō''}} established in [[Kyoto]] by [[Ashikaga Takauji]] is therefore considered illegitimate. ===Northern Pretenders=== These are the ''Hokuchō'' or Northern Court emperors: *[[Emperor Kōgon]] 1332-1333.Titsingh, pp. 286-289. * – *[[Emperor Kōmyō]] 1336-1348.Titsingh, pp. 294-298. *[[Emperor Sukō]] 1348-1351.Titsingh, pp. 298-301. *– *[[Emperor Go-Kōgon]] 1352-1371.Titsingh, pp. 302-309. *[[Emperor Go-En'yū]] 1371-1382.Titsingh, pp. 310-316, 320. *[[Emperor Go-Komatsu]] 1382-1392 (then went on to reign as legitimate emperor 1392-1412)Titsingh, pp. 317-327. ==Southern Court== The Imperial Court supported by the Ashikaga shoguns was rivaled by the {{nihongo|Southern Court|南朝|''nanchō''}} of Go-Daigo and his descendants. This came to be called the Southern Court because its seat was in a location south of its rival. Although the precise location of the emperors' seat did change, it was often identified as simply [[Yoshino]]. In 1392, [[Emperor Go-Kameyama]] of the Southern Court was defeated and abdicated in favor of Kōgon's great-grandson, [[Emperor Go-Komatsu]], thus ending the divide. The Southern Court of the Japanese Imperial Line is nonetheless seen as legitimate. In fact, Northern Court members are officially called [[pretender]]s. One Southern Court descendant, [[Kumazawa Hiromichi]], declared himself to be Japan's rightful Emperor in the days after the end of the Pacific War. He claimed that Emperor [[Hirohito]] was a fraud, arguing that Hirohito's entire line is descended from the Northern Court. Despite this, he was not arrested for [[lèse majesté]], even when donning the Imperial Crest. He could and did produce a [[koseki]] detailing his bloodline back to Go-Daigo in [[Yoshino]], but his claims and rhetoric failed to inspire anything other than sympathy.Dower, John W. (1999). [http://books.google.com/books?id=hae0dC_NaiUC&pg=PA306&dq=Kumazawa+Hiromichi&client=firefox-a#v=onepage&q=Kumazawa%20Hiromichi&f=false ''Embracing Defeat: Japan in the Wake of World War II,'' pp. 306-307.] ===Southern Court Emperors=== These are the ''Nanchō'' or Southern Court emperors: *[[Emperor Go-Daigo]] 1336-1339.Titsingh, pp. 281-295; Varley, H. Paul. (1980). ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 241-269. *[[Emperor Go-Murakami]] 1339-1368.Titsingh, pp. 295-308; Varley, pp. 269-270. *[[Emperor Chōkei]] 1368-1383.Titsingh, p. 308; Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?client=firefox-a&id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&q=chokei#search_anchor ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 158.] *[[Emperor Go-Kameyama]] 1383-1392.Titsingh, p. 320. ==Re-unification of Imperial Courts== Go-Kameyama reached an agreement with Go-Komatsu to return to the old alternations on a ten-year plan. However, Go-Komatsu broke this promise, not only ruling for 20 years, but being succeeded by his own son, rather than by one from the former Southern Court. == Notes == {{reflist|2}} ==References == {{refbegin}} * Dower, John W. (1999). ''[[Embracing Defeat|Embracing Defeat: Japan in the Wake of World War II]].'' New York: [[W. W. Norton]]. 10-ISBN 0-393-04686-9; 13-ISBN 978-0-393-04686-1; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/39143090 OCLC 39143090] * Mehl, Margaret. (1997). ''History and the State in Nineteenth-Century Japan.'' New York: [[St Martin's Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-312-21160-0; 13-ISBN 978-0-312-21160-8; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/419870136?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 419870136] * Nussbaum, Louis Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2005). ''Japan Encyclopedia.'' Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-674-01753-6; 13-ISBN 978-0-674-01753-5; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/48943301/editions?editionsView=true&referer=br OCLC 48943301] * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * Thomas, Julia Adeney. (2001). ''Reconfiguring Modernity: Concepts of Nature in Japanese Political Ideology.'' Berkeley: [[University of California Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-520-22854-5; 13-ISBN 978-0-520-22854-2; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/47916285 OCLC 47916285] * {{fr icon}} [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh]], Isaac, ed. (1834). ''[[Nipon o daï itsi ran]]''; ou, [http://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon.''] (écrit par Hayashi Gahō en 1652). Paris: Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/251800045 OCLC 251800045] * Varley, H. Paul, ed. (1980). ''A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns: [[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'' (écrit par [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]] en 1359). New York: Columbia University Press. 10-ISBN 0-231-04940-4; 13-ISBN 978-0-231-04940-5; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/311157159?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 311157159] {{refend}} [[Category:Feudal Japan]] [[Category:Japanese nobility]] [[Category:Former countries in Japanese history]] [[fr:Cour du Sud]] [[ja:南朝 (日本)]] [[simple:Southern Court]]

revision.comment: NPOV. Frederic is not a reliable source -- not only is the book cited a translation (no transparency), but it is a tertiary source, and contains several demonstrable errors. I'm editing from memory, but my source is Sansom "A History of Japan 1334-1615".

{{Infobox Former Country |native_name = {{lang|ja|南朝}}
''Nanchō'' |conventional_long_name = Southern Court |common_name = Southern Court |continent = Asia |region = Japan |year_start = 1338 |year_end = 1392 |image_coat =Imperial_Seal_of_Japan.svg |symbol_type = [[Seal (emblem)|Imperial Seal]] |symbol_type_article = Imperial Seal |p1 = Kenmu Restoration |flag_p1 =Imperial_Seal_of_Japan.svg |s1 = Ashikaga shogunate |flag_s1 = Ashikaga_mon.svg |event_start = Fall of [[Kyoto]] |date_start = February 23 |date_end = August 11 |event_end= Surrender of Emperor Go-Kameyama |capital = Yoshino |official language = [[Late Middle Japanese]] |government_type = [[Absolute monarchy]] |title_leader = [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] |leader1 = [[Emperor Go-Daigo|Go-Daigo]] |year_leader1 = 1336-1339 |leader2 = [[Emperor Go-Murakami|Go-Murakami]] |year_leader2 = 1339-1368 |leader3 = [[Emperor Chōkei|Chōkei]] |year_leader3 = 1368-1383 |leader4 = [[Emperor Go-Kameyama|Go-Kameyama]] |year_leader4 = 1383-1392 |religion = [[Shinbutsu shūgō]] |common_languages = [[Late Middle Japanese]] }} The {{nihongo|'''Southern Court'''|南朝|''Nanchō''}} were a set of four emperors ([[Emperor Go-Daigo]] and his line) whose claims to sovereignty during the [[Nanboku-chō period]] spanning from 1336 through 1392 were usurped by the [[Northern Court]]. This period ended with the Southern Court definitively losing the war, and they were forced to completely submit sovereignty to the Northern Court. This had the result that while later Japanese sovereigns were descended from the Northern Court, posterity assigns sole legitimacy during this period to the Southern Court. ==Nanboku-chō overview== [[Image:Nanbokucho-capitals.svg|thumb|right|The Imperial seats during the ''Nanboku-chō'' period were in relatively close proximity, but geographically distinct. They were conventionally identified as:
  • Northern capital : [[Kyoto]]

Go-Fukakusa's descendants were referred to as the {{nihongo|''Jimyōin-tō''|持明院統}} while Kameyama's descendants were known as the {{nihongo|''Daikakuji-tō''|大覚寺統}}. ==Northern Court== In 1333, when [[Emperor Go-Daigo]] (from the Daikakuji-tō) staged the [[Kemmu Restoration]] and revolted against the [[Kamakura shogunate]], the [[Shogun|Shōgun]] responded by declaring [[Emperor Kōgon]], Go-Daigo's [[second cousin once removed]] and the son of an earlier emperor, [[Emperor Go-Fushimi]] of the ''Jimyōin-tō'', as the new emperor. After the destruction of the Kamakura shogunate in 1333, Kōgon lost his claim, but his brother, [[Emperor Kōmyō]], and two of his sons were supported by the new [[Ashikaga shogunate|Ashikaga shoguns]] as the rightful claimants to the throne. Kōgon's family thus formed an alternate Imperial Court in Kyoto, which came to be called the Northern Court because its seat was in a location north of its rival. During the [[Meiji period]], an Imperial decree dated March 3, 1911 established that the legitimate reigning monarchs of this period were the direct descendants of [[Emperor Go-Daigo]] through [[Emperor Go-Murakami]], whose {{nihongo|Southern Court|南朝|''nanchō''}} had been established in exile in [[Yoshino District|Yoshino]], near Nara.Thomas, Julia Adeney. (2001). [http://books.google.com/books?id=Re4djF3oaTMC&pg=RA1-PA199&dq=1911+texbook+controversy#v=onepage&q=&f=false ''Reconfiguring modernity: concepts of nature in Japanese political ideology,'' p. 199 n57], citing Mehl, Margaret. (1997). ''History and the State in Nineteenth-Century Japan.'' p. 140-147. The {{nihongo|[[Northern Court]]|北朝|''hokuchō''}} established in [[Kyoto]] by [[Ashikaga Takauji]] is therefore considered illegitimate. ===Northern Pretenders=== These are the ''Hokuchō'' or Northern Court emperors: *[[Emperor Kōgon]] 1332-1333.Titsingh, pp. 286-289. * – *[[Emperor Kōmyō]] 1336-1348.Titsingh, pp. 294-298. *[[Emperor Sukō]] 1348-1351.Titsingh, pp. 298-301. *– *[[Emperor Go-Kōgon]] 1352-1371.Titsingh, pp. 302-309. *[[Emperor Go-En'yū]] 1371-1382.Titsingh, pp. 310-316, 320. *[[Emperor Go-Komatsu]] 1382-1392 (then went on to reign as legitimate emperor 1392-1412)Titsingh, pp. 317-327. ==Southern Court== The Imperial Court supported by the Ashikaga shoguns was rivaled by the {{nihongo|Southern Court|南朝|''nanchō''}} of Go-Daigo and his descendants. The Southern Court of the Japanese Imperial Line is nonetheless seen as legitimate. In fact, Northern Court members are officially called [[pretender]]s. One Southern Court descendant, [[Kumazawa Hiromichi]], declared himself to be Japan's rightful Emperor in the days after the end of the Pacific War. He claimed that Emperor [[Hirohito]] was a fraud, arguing that Hirohito's entire line is descended from the Northern Court. Despite this, he was not arrested for [[lèse majesté]], even when donning the Imperial Crest. [http://books.google.com/books?id=hae0dC_NaiUC&pg=PA306&dq=Kumazawa+Hiromichi&client=firefox-a#v=onepage&q=Kumazawa%20Hiromichi&f=false ''Embracing Defeat: Japan in the Wake of World War II,'' pp. 306-307.] ===Southern Court Emperors=== These are the ''Nanchō'' or Southern Court emperors: *[[Emperor Go-Daigo]] 1336-1339.Titsingh, pp. 281-295; Varley, H. (1834). ''[[Nipon o daï itsi ran]]''; ou, [http://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon.''] (écrit par Hayashi Gahō en 1652). Paris: Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/251800045 OCLC 251800045] * Varley, H. Paul, ed. (1980). ''A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns: [[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'' (écrit par [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]] en 1359). New York: Columbia University Press. 10-ISBN 0-231-04940-4; 13-ISBN 978-0-231-04940-5; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/311157159?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 311157159] {{refend}} [[Category:Feudal Japan]] [[Category:Japanese nobility]] [[Category:Former countries in Japanese history]] [[fr:Cour du Sud]] [[ja:南朝 (日本)]] [[simple:Southern Court]]

revision.comment: /* References */ Removed unreliable tertiary source that is no longer referred to in the article. This article seems to be based entirely on French sources -- there's nothing wrong with that, but if we cite non-English sources, shouldn't they be Japanese?
revision.text:
{{Infobox Former Country |native_name = {{lang|ja|南朝}}
''Nanchō'' |conventional_long_name = Southern Court |common_name = Southern Court |continent = Asia |region = Japan |year_start = 1338 |year_end = 1392 |image_coat =Imperial_Seal_of_Japan.svg |symbol_type = [[Seal (emblem)|Imperial Seal]] |symbol_type_article = Imperial Seal |p1 = Kenmu Restoration |flag_p1 =Imperial_Seal_of_Japan.svg |s1 = Ashikaga shogunate |flag_s1 = Ashikaga_mon.svg |event_start = Fall of [[Kyoto]] |date_start = February 23 |date_end = August 11 |event_end= Surrender of Emperor Go-Kameyama |capital = Yoshino |official language = [[Late Middle Japanese]] |government_type = [[Absolute monarchy]] |title_leader = [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] |leader1 = [[Emperor Go-Daigo|Go-Daigo]] |year_leader1 = 1336-1339 |leader2 = [[Emperor Go-Murakami|Go-Murakami]] |year_leader2 = 1339-1368 |leader3 = [[Emperor Chōkei|Chōkei]] |year_leader3 = 1368-1383 |leader4 = [[Emperor Go-Kameyama|Go-Kameyama]] |year_leader4 = 1383-1392 |religion = [[Shinbutsu shūgō]] |common_languages = [[Late Middle Japanese]] }} The {{nihongo|'''Southern Court'''|南朝|''Nanchō''}} were a set of four emperors ([[Emperor Go-Daigo]] and his line) whose claims to sovereignty during the [[Nanboku-chō period]] spanning from 1336 through 1392 were usurped by the [[Northern Court]]. This period ended with the Southern Court definitively losing the war, and they were forced to completely submit sovereignty to the Northern Court. This had the result that while later Japanese sovereigns were descended from the Northern Court, posterity assigns sole legitimacy during this period to the Southern Court. ==Nanboku-chō overview== [[Image:Nanbokucho-capitals.svg|thumb|right|The Imperial seats during the ''Nanboku-chō'' period were in relatively close proximity, but geographically distinct. They were conventionally identified as:
  • Northern capital : [[Kyoto]]
  • Southern capital : [[Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]].]] The genesis of the Northern Court go back to [[Emperor Go-Saga]], who reigned from 1242 through 1246.Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). ''Annales des empereurs du japon'', pp. 245-247. Go-Saga was succeeded by two of his sons, [[Emperor Go-Fukakusa]]Titsingh, pp. 248-255. and [[Emperor Kameyama]], who took turns on the throne.Titsingh, pp. 255-261. This because on his death bed in 1272, Go-Saga had insisted that his sons adopt a plan in which future emperors from the two fraternal lines would ascend the throne in alternating succession.Titsingh, p. 261. This plan proved to be unworkable, resulting in rival factions and rival claimants to the throne. Go-Fukakusa's descendants were referred to as the {{nihongo|''Jimyōin-tō''|持明院統}} while Kameyama's descendants were known as the {{nihongo|''Daikakuji-tō''|大覚寺統}}. ==Northern Court== In 1333, when [[Emperor Go-Daigo]] (from the Daikakuji-tō) staged the [[Kemmu Restoration]] and revolted against the [[Kamakura shogunate]], the [[Shogun|Shōgun]] responded by declaring [[Emperor Kōgon]], Go-Daigo's [[second cousin once removed]] and the son of an earlier emperor, [[Emperor Go-Fushimi]] of the ''Jimyōin-tō'', as the new emperor. After the destruction of the Kamakura shogunate in 1333, Kōgon lost his claim, but his brother, [[Emperor Kōmyō]], and two of his sons were supported by the new [[Ashikaga shogunate|Ashikaga shoguns]] as the rightful claimants to the throne. Kōgon's family thus formed an alternate Imperial Court in Kyoto, which came to be called the Northern Court because its seat was in a location north of its rival. During the [[Meiji period]], an Imperial decree dated March 3, 1911 established that the legitimate reigning monarchs of this period were the direct descendants of [[Emperor Go-Daigo]] through [[Emperor Go-Murakami]], whose {{nihongo|Southern Court|南朝|''nanchō''}} had been established in exile in [[Yoshino District|Yoshino]], near Nara.Thomas, Julia Adeney. (2001). [http://books.google.com/books?id=Re4djF3oaTMC&pg=RA1-PA199&dq=1911+texbook+controversy#v=onepage&q=&f=false ''Reconfiguring modernity: concepts of nature in Japanese political ideology,'' p. 199 n57], citing Mehl, Margaret. (1997). ''History and the State in Nineteenth-Century Japan.'' p. 140-147. The {{nihongo|[[Northern Court]]|北朝|''hokuchō''}} established in [[Kyoto]] by [[Ashikaga Takauji]] is therefore considered illegitimate. ===Northern Pretenders=== These are the ''Hokuchō'' or Northern Court emperors: *[[Emperor Kōgon]] 1332-1333.Titsingh, pp. 286-289. * – *[[Emperor Kōmyō]] 1336-1348.Titsingh, pp. 294-298. *[[Emperor Sukō]] 1348-1351.Titsingh, pp. 298-301. *– *[[Emperor Go-Kōgon]] 1352-1371.Titsingh, pp. 302-309. *[[Emperor Go-En'yū]] 1371-1382.Titsingh, pp. 310-316, 320. *[[Emperor Go-Komatsu]] 1382-1392 (then went on to reign as legitimate emperor 1392-1412)Titsingh, pp. 317-327. ==Southern Court== The Imperial Court supported by the Ashikaga shoguns was rivaled by the {{nihongo|Southern Court|南朝|''nanchō''}} of Go-Daigo and his descendants. This came to be called the Southern Court because its seat was in a location south of its rival. Although the precise location of the emperors' seat did change, it was often identified as simply [[Yoshino]]. In 1392, [[Emperor Go-Kameyama]] of the Southern Court was defeated and abdicated in favor of Kōgon's great-grandson, [[Emperor Go-Komatsu]], thus ending the divide. The Southern Court of the Japanese Imperial Line is nonetheless seen as legitimate. In fact, Northern Court members are officially called [[pretender]]s. One Southern Court descendant, [[Kumazawa Hiromichi]], declared himself to be Japan's rightful Emperor in the days after the end of the Pacific War. He claimed that Emperor [[Hirohito]] was a fraud, arguing that Hirohito's entire line is descended from the Northern Court. Despite this, he was not arrested for [[lèse majesté]], even when donning the Imperial Crest. He could and did produce a [[koseki]] detailing his bloodline back to Go-Daigo in [[Yoshino]], but his claims and rhetoric failed to inspire anything other than sympathy.Dower, John W. (1999). [http://books.google.com/books?id=hae0dC_NaiUC&pg=PA306&dq=Kumazawa+Hiromichi&client=firefox-a#v=onepage&q=Kumazawa%20Hiromichi&f=false ''Embracing Defeat: Japan in the Wake of World War II,'' pp. 306-307.] ===Southern Court Emperors=== These are the ''Nanchō'' or Southern Court emperors: *[[Emperor Go-Daigo]] 1336-1339.Titsingh, pp. 281-295; Varley, H. Paul. (1980). ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 241-269. *[[Emperor Go-Murakami]] 1339-1368.Titsingh, pp. 295-308; Varley, pp. 269-270. *[[Emperor Chōkei]] 1368-1383.Titsingh, p. 308; Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?client=firefox-a&id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&q=chokei#search_anchor ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 158.] *[[Emperor Go-Kameyama]] 1383-1392.Titsingh, p. 320. ==Re-unification of Imperial Courts== Go-Kameyama reached an agreement with Go-Komatsu to return to the old alternations on a ten-year plan. However, Go-Komatsu broke this promise, not only ruling for 20 years, but being succeeded by his own son, rather than by one from the former Southern Court. == Notes == {{reflist|2}} ==References == {{refbegin}} * Dower, John W. (1999). ''[[Embracing Defeat|Embracing Defeat: Japan in the Wake of World War II]].'' New York: [[W. W. Norton]]. 10-ISBN 0-393-04686-9; 13-ISBN 978-0-393-04686-1; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/39143090 OCLC 39143090] * Mehl, Margaret. (1997). ''History and the State in Nineteenth-Century Japan.'' New York: [[St Martin's Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-312-21160-0; 13-ISBN 978-0-312-21160-8; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/419870136?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 419870136] * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * Thomas, Julia Adeney. (2001). ''Reconfiguring Modernity: Concepts of Nature in Japanese Political Ideology.'' Berkeley: [[University of California Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-520-22854-5; 13-ISBN 978-0-520-22854-2; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/47916285 OCLC 47916285] * {{fr icon}} [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh]], Isaac, ed. (1834). ''[[Nipon o daï itsi ran]]''; ou, [http://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon.''] (écrit par Hayashi Gahō en 1652). Paris: Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/251800045 OCLC 251800045] * Varley, H. Paul, ed. (1980). ''A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns: [[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'' (translated from the 1359 [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]] work). New York: Columbia University Press. 10-ISBN 0-231-04940-4; 13-ISBN 978-0-231-04940-5; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/311157159?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 311157159] {{refend}} [[Category:Feudal Japan]] [[Category:Japanese nobility]] [[Category:Former countries in Japanese history]] [[fr:Cour du Sud]] [[ja:南朝 (日本)]] [[simple:Southern Court]]

In 1392, [[Emperor Go-Kameyama]] of the Southern Court was defeated and abdicated in favor of Kōgon's great-grandson, [[Emperor Go-Komatsu]], thus ending the divide. The Southern Court of the Japanese Imperial Line is nonetheless seen as legitimate. In fact, Northern Court members are officially called [[pretender]]s. One Southern Court descendant, [[Kumazawa Hiromichi]], declared himself to be Japan's rightful Emperor in the days after the end of the Pacific War. He claimed that Emperor [[Hirohito]] was a fraud, arguing that Hirohito's entire line is descended from the Northern Court. Despite this, he was not arrested for [[lèse majesté]], even when donning the Imperial Crest. He could and did produce a [[koseki]] detailing his bloodline back to Go-Daigo in Yoshino, but his claims and rhetoric failed to inspire anything other than sympathy.Dower, John W. (1999). [http://books.google.com/books?id=hae0dC_NaiUC&pg=PA306&dq=Kumazawa+Hiromichi&client=firefox-a#v=onepage&q=Kumazawa%20Hiromichi&f=false ''Embracing Defeat: Japan in the Wake of World War II,'' pp. 306-307.] ===Southern Court Emperors=== These are the ''Nanchō'' or Southern Court emperors: *[[Emperor Go-Daigo]] 1336-1339.Titsingh, pp. 281-295; Varley, H. Paul. (1980). ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 241-269. *[[Emperor Go-Murakami]] 1339-1368.Titsingh, pp. 295-308; Varley, pp. 269-270. *[[Emperor Chōkei]] 1368-1383.Titsingh, p. 308; Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?client=firefox-a&id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&q=chokei#search_anchor ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 158.] *[[Emperor Go-Kameyama]] 1383-1392.Titsingh, p. 320. ==Re-unification of Imperial Courts== Go-Kameyama reached an agreement with Go-Komatsu to return to the old alternations on a ten-year plan. However, Go-Komatsu broke this promise, not only ruling for 20 years, but being succeeded by his own son, rather than by one from the former Southern Court. == Notes == {{reflist|2}} ==References == {{refbegin}} * Dower, John W. (1999). ''[[Embracing Defeat|Embracing Defeat: Japan in the Wake of World War II]].'' New York: [[W. W. Norton]]. 10-ISBN 0-393-04686-9; 13-ISBN 978-0-393-04686-1; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/39143090 OCLC 39143090] * Mehl, Margaret. (1997). ''History and the State in Nineteenth-Century Japan.'' New York: [[St Martin's Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-312-21160-0; 13-ISBN 978-0-312-21160-8; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/419870136?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 419870136] * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * Thomas, Julia Adeney. (2001). ''Reconfiguring Modernity: Concepts of Nature in Japanese Political Ideology.'' Berkeley: [[University of California Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-520-22854-5; 13-ISBN 978-0-520-22854-2; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/47916285 OCLC 47916285] * {{fr icon}} [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh]], Isaac, ed. (1834). ''[[Nipon o daï itsi ran]]''; ou, [http://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon.''] (écrit par Hayashi Gahō en 1652). Paris: Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/251800045 OCLC 251800045] * Varley, H. Paul, ed. (1980). ''A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns: [[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'' (translated from the 1359 [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]] work). New York: Columbia University Press. 10-ISBN 0-231-04940-4; 13-ISBN 978-0-231-04940-5; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/311157159?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 311157159] {{refend}} [[Category:Feudal Japan]] [[Category:Japanese nobility]] [[Category:Former countries in Japanese history]] [[fr:Cour du Sud]] [[ja:南朝 (日本)]] [[simple:Southern Court]]

{{Infobox Former Country |native_name = {{lang|ja|南朝}}
''Nanchō'' |conventional_long_name = Southern Court |common_name = Southern Court |continent = Asia |region = Japan |year_start = 1338 |year_end = 1392 |image_coat =Imperial_Seal_of_Japan.svg |symbol_type = [[Seal (emblem)|Imperial Seal]] |symbol_type_article = Imperial Seal |p1 = Kenmu Restoration |flag_p1 =Imperial_Seal_of_Japan.svg |s1 = Ashikaga shogunate |flag_s1 = Ashikaga_mon.svg |event_start = Fall of [[Kyoto]] |date_start = February 23 |date_end = August 11 |event_end= Surrender of Emperor Go-Kameyama |capital = Yoshino |official language = [[Late Middle Japanese]] |government_type = [[Absolute monarchy]] |title_leader = [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] |leader1 = [[Emperor Go-Daigo|Go-Daigo]] |year_leader1 = 1336-1339 |leader2 = [[Emperor Go-Murakami|Go-Murakami]] |year_leader2 = 1339-1368 |leader3 = [[Emperor Chōkei|Chōkei]] |year_leader3 = 1368-1383 |leader4 = [[Emperor Go-Kameyama|Go-Kameyama]] |year_leader4 = 1383-1392 |religion = [[Shinbutsu shūgō]] |common_languages = [[Late Middle Japanese]] }} The {{nihongo|'''Southern Court'''|南朝|''Nanchō''}} were a set of four emperors ([[Emperor Go-Daigo]] and his line) whose claims to sovereignty during the [[Nanboku-chō period]] spanning from 1336 through 1392 were usurped by the [[Northern Court]]. This period ended with the Southern Court definitively losing the war, and they were forced to completely submit sovereignty to the Northern Court. This had the result that while later Japanese sovereigns were descended from the Northern Court, posterity assigns sole legitimacy during this period to the Southern Court. ==Nanboku-chō overview== [[Image:Nanbokucho-capitals.svg|thumb|right|The Imperial seats during the ''Nanboku-chō'' period were in relatively close proximity, but geographically distinct. They were conventionally identified as:
  • Northern capital : [[Kyoto]]
  • Southern capital: [[Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]].]] The genesis of the Northern Court go back to [[Emperor Go-Saga]], who reigned from 1242 through 1246.Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). ''Annales des empereurs du japon'', pp. 245-247. Go-Saga was succeeded by two of his sons, [[Emperor Go-Fukakusa]]Titsingh, pp. 248-255. and [[Emperor Kameyama]], who took turns on the throne.Titsingh, pp. 255-261. This because on his death bed in 1272, Go-Saga had insisted that his sons adopt a plan in which future emperors from the two fraternal lines would ascend the throne in alternating succession.Titsingh, p. 261. This plan proved to be unworkable, resulting in rival factions and rival claimants to the throne. Go-Fukakusa's descendants were referred to as the {{nihongo|''Jimyōin-tō''|持明院統}} while Kameyama's descendants were known as the {{nihongo|''Daikakuji-tō''|大覚寺統}}. ==Northern Court== In 1333, when [[Emperor Go-Daigo]] (from the Daikakuji-tō) staged the [[Kemmu Restoration]] and revolted against the [[Kamakura shogunate]], the [[Shogun|Shōgun]] responded by declaring [[Emperor Kōgon]], Go-Daigo's [[second cousin once removed]] and the son of an earlier emperor, [[Emperor Go-Fushimi]] of the ''Jimyōin-tō'', as the new emperor. After the destruction of the Kamakura shogunate in 1333, Kōgon lost his claim, but his brother, [[Emperor Kōmyō]], and two of his sons were supported by the new [[Ashikaga shogunate|Ashikaga shoguns]] as the rightful claimants to the throne. Kōgon's family thus formed an alternate Imperial Court in Kyoto, which came to be called the Northern Court because its seat was in a location north of its rival. During the [[Meiji period]], an Imperial decree dated March 3, 1911 established that the legitimate reigning monarchs of this period were the direct descendants of [[Emperor Go-Daigo]] through [[Emperor Go-Murakami]], whose {{nihongo|Southern Court|南朝|''nanchō''}} had been established in exile in [[Yoshino District|Yoshino]], near Nara.Thomas, Julia Adeney. (2001). [http://books.google.com/books?id=Re4djF3oaTMC&pg=RA1-PA199&dq=1911+texbook+controversy#v=onepage&q=&f=false ''Reconfiguring modernity: concepts of nature in Japanese political ideology,'' p. 199 n57], citing Mehl, Margaret. (1997). ''History and the State in Nineteenth-Century Japan.'' p. 140-147. The {{nihongo|[[Northern Court]]|北朝|''hokuchō''}} established in [[Kyoto]] by [[Ashikaga Takauji]] is therefore considered illegitimate. ===Northern Pretenders=== These are the ''Hokuchō'' or Northern Court emperors: *[[Emperor Kōgon]] 1332-1333.Titsingh, pp. 286-289. * – *[[Emperor Kōmyō]] 1336-1348.Titsingh, pp. 294-298. *[[Emperor Sukō]] 1348-1351.Titsingh, pp. 298-301. *– *[[Emperor Go-Kōgon]] 1352-1371.Titsingh, pp. 302-309. *[[Emperor Go-En'yū]] 1371-1382.Titsingh, pp. 310-316, 320. *[[Emperor Go-Komatsu]] 1382-1392 (then went on to reign as legitimate emperor 1392-1412)Titsingh, pp. 317-327. ==Southern Court== The Imperial Court supported by the Ashikaga shoguns was rivaled by the {{nihongo|Southern Court|南朝|''nanchō''}} of Go-Daigo and his descendants. This came to be called the Southern Court because its seat was in a location south of its rival. Although the precise location of the emperors' seat did change, it was often identified as simply [[Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]]. In 1392, [[Emperor Go-Kameyama]] of the Southern Court was defeated and abdicated in favor of Kōgon's great-grandson, [[Emperor Go-Komatsu]], thus ending the divide. The Southern Court of the Japanese Imperial Line is nonetheless seen as legitimate. In fact, Northern Court members are officially called [[pretender]]s. One Southern Court descendant, [[Kumazawa Hiromichi]], declared himself to be Japan's rightful Emperor in the days after the end of the Pacific War. He claimed that Emperor [[Hirohito]] was a fraud, arguing that Hirohito's entire line is descended from the Northern Court. Despite this, he was not arrested for [[lèse majesté]], even when donning the Imperial Crest. He could and did produce a [[koseki]] detailing his bloodline back to Go-Daigo in Yoshino, but his claims and rhetoric failed to inspire anything other than sympathy.Dower, John W. (1999). [http://books.google.com/books?id=hae0dC_NaiUC&pg=PA306&dq=Kumazawa+Hiromichi&client=firefox-a#v=onepage&q=Kumazawa%20Hiromichi&f=false ''Embracing Defeat: Japan in the Wake of World War II,'' pp. 306-307.] ===Southern Court Emperors=== These are the ''Nanchō'' or Southern Court emperors: *[[Emperor Go-Daigo]] 1336-1339.Titsingh, pp. 281-295; Varley, H. Paul. (1980). ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 241-269. *[[Emperor Go-Murakami]] 1339-1368.Titsingh, pp. 295-308; Varley, pp. 269-270. *[[Emperor Chōkei]] 1368-1383.Titsingh, p. 308; Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?client=firefox-a&id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&q=chokei#search_anchor ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 158.] *[[Emperor Go-Kameyama]] 1383-1392.Titsingh, p. 320. ==Re-unification of Imperial Courts== Go-Kameyama reached an agreement with Go-Komatsu to return to the old alternations on a ten-year plan. However, Go-Komatsu broke this promise, not only ruling for 20 years, but being succeeded by his own son, rather than by one from the former Southern Court. == Notes == {{reflist|2}} ==References == {{refbegin}} * Dower, John W. (1999). ''[[Embracing Defeat|Embracing Defeat: Japan in the Wake of World War II]].'' New York: [[W. W. Norton]]. 10-ISBN 0-393-04686-9; 13-ISBN 978-0-393-04686-1; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/39143090 OCLC 39143090] * Mehl, Margaret. (1997). ''History and the State in Nineteenth-Century Japan.'' New York: [[St Martin's Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-312-21160-0; 13-ISBN 978-0-312-21160-8; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/419870136?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 419870136] * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * Thomas, Julia Adeney. (2001). ''Reconfiguring Modernity: Concepts of Nature in Japanese Political Ideology.'' Berkeley: [[University of California Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-520-22854-5; 13-ISBN 978-0-520-22854-2; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/47916285 OCLC 47916285] * {{fr icon}} [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh]], Isaac, ed. (1834). ''[[Nipon o daï itsi ran]]''; ou, [http://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon.''] (écrit par Hayashi Gahō en 1652). Paris: Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/251800045 OCLC 251800045] * Varley, H. Paul, ed. (1980). ''A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns: [[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'' (translated from the 1359 [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]] work). New York: Columbia University Press. 10-ISBN 0-231-04940-4; 13-ISBN 978-0-231-04940-5; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/311157159?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 311157159] {{refend}} [[Category:Feudal Japan]] [[Category:Japanese nobility]] [[Category:Former countries in Japanese history]] [[fr:Cour du Sud]] [[ja:南朝 (日本)]] [[simple:Southern Court]]

This had the result that while later Japanese sovereigns were descended from the Northern Court, posterity assigns sole legitimacy during this period to the Southern Court. ==Nanboku-chō overview== [[File:Nanbokucho-capitals.svg|thumb|The Imperial seats during the ''Nanboku-chō'' period were in relatively close proximity, but geographically distinct. They were conventionally identified as: {{unordered list|Northern capital : [[Kyoto]] |Southern capital: [[Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]].}}]] The genesis of the Northern Court go back to [[Emperor Go-Saga]], who reigned from 1242 through 1246.Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). ''Annales des empereurs du japon'', pp. 245-247. Go-Saga was succeeded by two of his sons, [[Emperor Go-Fukakusa]]Titsingh, pp. 248-255. and [[Emperor Kameyama]], who took turns on the throne.Titsingh, pp. 255-261. This because on his death bed in 1272, Go-Saga had insisted that his sons adopt a plan in which future emperors from the two fraternal lines would ascend the throne in alternating succession.Titsingh, p. 261. This plan proved to be unworkable, resulting in rival factions and rival claimants to the throne. Go-Fukakusa's descendants were referred to as the {{nihongo|''Jimyōin-tō''|持明院統}} while Kameyama's descendants were known as the {{nihongo|''Daikakuji-tō''|大覚寺統}}. ==Northern Court== In 1333, when [[Emperor Go-Daigo]] (from the Daikakuji-tō) staged the [[Kemmu Restoration]] and revolted against the [[Kamakura shogunate]], the [[Shogun|Shōgun]] responded by declaring [[Emperor Kōgon]], Go-Daigo's [[second cousin once removed]] and the son of an earlier emperor, [[Emperor Go-Fushimi]] of the ''Jimyōin-tō'', as the new emperor. After the destruction of the Kamakura shogunate in 1333, Kōgon lost his claim, but his brother, [[Emperor Kōmyō]], and two of his sons were supported by the new [[Ashikaga shogunate|Ashikaga shoguns]] as the rightful claimants to the throne. Kōgon's family thus formed an alternate Imperial Court in Kyoto, which came to be called the Northern Court because its seat was in a location north of its rival. During the [[Meiji period]], an Imperial decree dated March 3, 1911 established that the legitimate reigning monarchs of this period were the direct descendants of [[Emperor Go-Daigo]] through [[Emperor Go-Murakami]], whose {{nihongo|Southern Court|南朝|''nanchō''}} had been established in exile in [[Yoshino District|Yoshino]], near Nara.Thomas, Julia Adeney. (2001). [http://books.google.com/books?id=Re4djF3oaTMC&pg=RA1-PA199&dq=1911+texbook+controversy#v=onepage&q=&f=false ''Reconfiguring modernity: concepts of nature in Japanese political ideology,'' p. 199 n57], citing Mehl, Margaret. (1997). ''History and the State in Nineteenth-Century Japan.'' p. 140-147. The {{nihongo|[[Northern Court]]|北朝|''hokuchō''}} established in [[Kyoto]] by [[Ashikaga Takauji]] is therefore considered illegitimate. ===Northern Pretenders=== These are the ''Hokuchō'' or Northern Court emperors: *[[Emperor Kōgon]] 1332-1333.Titsingh, pp. 286-289. * – *[[Emperor Kōmyō]] 1336-1348.Titsingh, pp. 294-298. *[[Emperor Sukō]] 1348-1351.Titsingh, pp. 298-301. *– *[[Emperor Go-Kōgon]] 1352-1371.Titsingh, pp. 302-309. *[[Emperor Go-En'yū]] 1371-1382.Titsingh, pp. 310-316, 320. *[[Emperor Go-Komatsu]] 1382-1392 (then went on to reign as legitimate emperor 1392-1412)Titsingh, pp. 317-327. ==Southern Court== The Imperial Court supported by the Ashikaga shoguns was rivaled by the {{nihongo|Southern Court|南朝|''nanchō''}} of Go-Daigo and his descendants. This came to be called the Southern Court because its seat was in a location south of its rival. Although the precise location of the emperors' seat did change, it was often identified as simply [[Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]]. In 1392, [[Emperor Go-Kameyama]] of the Southern Court was defeated and abdicated in favor of Kōgon's great-grandson, [[Emperor Go-Komatsu]], thus ending the divide. The Southern Court of the Japanese Imperial Line is nonetheless seen as legitimate. In fact, Northern Court members are officially called [[pretender]]s. One Southern Court descendant, [[Kumazawa Hiromichi]], declared himself to be Japan's rightful Emperor in the days after the end of the Pacific War. He claimed that Emperor [[Hirohito]] was a fraud, arguing that Hirohito's entire line is descended from the Northern Court. Despite this, he was not arrested for [[lèse majesté]], even when donning the Imperial Crest. He could and did produce a [[koseki]] detailing his bloodline back to Go-Daigo in Yoshino, but his claims and rhetoric failed to inspire anything other than sympathy.Dower, John W. (1999). [http://books.google.com/books?id=hae0dC_NaiUC&pg=PA306&dq=Kumazawa+Hiromichi&client=firefox-a#v=onepage&q=Kumazawa%20Hiromichi&f=false ''Embracing Defeat: Japan in the Wake of World War II,'' pp. 306-307.] ===Southern Court Emperors=== These are the ''Nanchō'' or Southern Court emperors: *[[Emperor Go-Daigo]] 1336-1339.Titsingh, pp. 281-295; Varley, H. Paul. (1980). ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 241-269. *[[Emperor Go-Murakami]] 1339-1368.Titsingh, pp. 295-308; Varley, pp. 269-270. *[[Emperor Chōkei]] 1368-1383.Titsingh, p. 308; Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?client=firefox-a&id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&q=chokei#search_anchor ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 158.] *[[Emperor Go-Kameyama]] 1383-1392.Titsingh, p. 320. ==Re-unification of Imperial Courts== Go-Kameyama reached an agreement with Go-Komatsu to return to the old alternations on a ten-year plan. However, Go-Komatsu broke this promise, not only ruling for 20 years, but being succeeded by his own son, rather than by one from the former Southern Court. == Notes == {{reflist|2}} ==References == {{refbegin}} * Dower, John W. (1999). ''[[Embracing Defeat|Embracing Defeat: Japan in the Wake of World War II]].'' New York: [[W. W. Norton]]. 10-ISBN 0-393-04686-9; 13-ISBN 978-0-393-04686-1; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/39143090 OCLC 39143090] * Mehl, Margaret. (1997). ''History and the State in Nineteenth-Century Japan.'' New York: [[St Martin's Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-312-21160-0; 13-ISBN 978-0-312-21160-8; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/419870136?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 419870136] * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * Thomas, Julia Adeney. (2001). ''Reconfiguring Modernity: Concepts of Nature in Japanese Political Ideology.'' Berkeley: [[University of California Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-520-22854-5; 13-ISBN 978-0-520-22854-2; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/47916285 OCLC 47916285] * {{fr icon}} [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh]], Isaac, ed. (1834). ''[[Nipon o daï itsi ran]]''; ou, [http://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon.''] (écrit par Hayashi Gahō en 1652). Paris: Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/251800045 OCLC 251800045] * Varley, H. Paul, ed. (1980). ''A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns: [[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'' (translated from the 1359 [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]] work). New York: Columbia University Press. 10-ISBN 0-231-04940-4; 13-ISBN 978-0-231-04940-5; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/311157159?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 311157159] {{refend}} [[Category:Feudal Japan]] [[Category:Japanese nobility]] [[Category:Former countries in Japanese history]]

Berkeley: [[University of California Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-520-22854-5; 13-ISBN 978-0-520-22854-2; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/47916285 OCLC 47916285] * {{fr icon}} [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh]], Isaac, ed. (1834). ''[[Nipon o daï itsi ran]]''; ou, [http://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon.''] (écrit par Hayashi Gahō en 1652). Paris: Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/251800045 OCLC 251800045] * Varley, H. Paul, ed. (1980). ''A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns: [[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'' (translated from the 1359 [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]] work). New York: Columbia University Press. 10-ISBN 0-231-04940-4; 13-ISBN 978-0-231-04940-5; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/311157159?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 311157159] {{refend}} [[Category:Feudal Japan]] [[Category:Japanese nobility]] [[Category:Former countries in Japanese history]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox former country | native_name = {{lang|ja|南朝}}
''Nanchō'' | conventional_long_name = Southern Court | common_name = Southern Court | continent = Asia | region = Japan | year_start = 1338 | year_end = 1392 | image_coat = Imperial_Seal_of_Japan.svg | symbol_type = [[Seal (emblem)|Imperial Seal]] | symbol_type_article = Imperial Seal | p1 = Kenmu Restoration | flag_p1 = Imperial_Seal_of_Japan.svg | s1 = Ashikaga shogunate | flag_s1 = Ashikaga_mon.svg | event_start = Fall of [[Kyoto]] | date_start = February 23 | date_end = August 11 | event_end = Surrender of Emperor Go-Kameyama | capital = Yoshino | official language = [[Late Middle Japanese]] | government_type = [[Absolute monarchy]] | title_leader = [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] | leader1 = [[Emperor Go-Daigo|Go-Daigo]] | year_leader1 = 1336-1339 | leader2 = [[Emperor Go-Murakami|Go-Murakami]] | year_leader2 = 1339-1368 | leader3 = [[Emperor Chōkei|Chōkei]] | year_leader3 = 1368-1383 | leader4 = [[Emperor Go-Kameyama|Go-Kameyama]] | year_leader4 = 1383-1392 | religion = [[Shinbutsu shūgō]] | common_languages = [[Late Middle Japanese]] }} The {{nihongo|'''Southern Court'''|南朝|''Nanchō''}} were a set of four emperors ([[Emperor Go-Daigo]] and his line) whose claims to sovereignty during the [[Nanboku-chō period]] spanning from 1336 through 1392 were usurped by the [[Northern Court]]. This period ended with the Southern Court definitively losing the war, and they were forced to completely submit sovereignty to the Northern Court. This had the result that while later Japanese sovereigns were descended from the Northern Court, posterity assigns sole legitimacy during this period to the Southern Court. ==Nanboku-chō overview== [[File:Nanbokucho-capitals.svg|thumb|The Imperial seats during the ''Nanboku-chō'' period were in relatively close proximity, but geographically distinct. They were conventionally identified as: {{unordered list|Northern capital : [[Kyoto]] |Southern capital: [[Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]].}}]] The genesis of the Northern Court go back to [[Emperor Go-Saga]], who reigned from 1242 through 1246.Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). ''Annales des empereurs du japon'', pp. 245-247. Go-Saga was succeeded by two of his sons, [[Emperor Go-Fukakusa]]Titsingh, pp. 248-255. and [[Emperor Kameyama]], who took turns on the throne.Titsingh, pp. 255-261. This because on his death bed in 1272, Go-Saga had insisted that his sons adopt a plan in which future emperors from the two fraternal lines would ascend the throne in alternating succession.Titsingh, p. 261. This plan proved to be unworkable, resulting in rival factions and rival claimants to the throne. Go-Fukakusa's descendants were referred to as the {{nihongo|''Jimyōin-tō''|持明院統}} while Kameyama's descendants were known as the {{nihongo|''Daikakuji-tō''|大覚寺統}}. ==Northern Court== In 1333, when [[Emperor Go-Daigo]] (from the Daikakuji-tō) staged the [[Kemmu Restoration]] and revolted against the [[Kamakura shogunate]], the [[Shogun|Shōgun]] responded by declaring [[Emperor Kōgon]], Go-Daigo's [[second cousin once removed]] and the son of an earlier emperor, [[Emperor Go-Fushimi]] of the ''Jimyōin-tō'', as the new emperor. After the destruction of the Kamakura shogunate in 1333, Kōgon lost his claim, but his brother, [[Emperor Kōmyō]], and two of his sons were supported by the new [[Ashikaga shogunate|Ashikaga shoguns]] as the rightful claimants to the throne. Kōgon's family thus formed an alternate Imperial Court in Kyoto, which came to be called the Northern Court because its seat was in a location north of its rival. During the [[Meiji period]], an Imperial decree dated March 3, 1911 established that the legitimate reigning monarchs of this period were the direct descendants of [[Emperor Go-Daigo]] through [[Emperor Go-Murakami]], whose {{nihongo|Southern Court|南朝|''nanchō''}} had been established in exile in [[Yoshino District|Yoshino]], near Nara.Thomas, Julia Adeney. (2001). [http://books.google.com/books?id=Re4djF3oaTMC&pg=RA1-PA199&dq=1911+texbook+controversy#v=onepage&q=&f=false ''Reconfiguring modernity: concepts of nature in Japanese political ideology,'' p. 199 n57], citing Mehl, Margaret. (1997). ''History and the State in Nineteenth-Century Japan.'' p. 140-147. The {{nihongo|[[Northern Court]]|北朝|''hokuchō''}} established in [[Kyoto]] by [[Ashikaga Takauji]] is therefore considered illegitimate. ===Northern Pretenders=== These are the ''Hokuchō'' or Northern Court emperors: *[[Emperor Kōgon]] 1332-1333.Titsingh, pp. 286-289. * – *[[Emperor Kōmyō]] 1336-1348.Titsingh, pp. 294-298. *[[Emperor Sukō]] 1348-1351.Titsingh, pp. 298-301. *– *[[Emperor Go-Kōgon]] 1352-1371.Titsingh, pp. 302-309. *[[Emperor Go-En'yū]] 1371-1382.Titsingh, pp. 310-316, 320. *[[Emperor Go-Komatsu]] 1382-1392 (then went on to reign as legitimate emperor 1392-1412)Titsingh, pp. 317-327. ==Southern Court== The Imperial Court supported by the Ashikaga shoguns was rivaled by the {{nihongo|Southern Court|南朝|''nanchō''}} of Go-Daigo and his descendants. This came to be called the Southern Court because its seat was in a location south of its rival. Although the precise location of the emperors' seat did change, it was often identified as simply [[Yoshino, Nara|Yoshino]]. In 1392, [[Emperor Go-Kameyama]] of the Southern Court was defeated and abdicated in favor of Kōgon's great-grandson, [[Emperor Go-Komatsu]], thus ending the divide. The Southern Court of the Japanese Imperial Line is nonetheless seen as legitimate. In fact, Northern Court members are officially called [[pretender]]s. One Southern Court descendant, [[Kumazawa Hiromichi]], declared himself to be Japan's rightful Emperor in the days after the end of the Pacific War. He claimed that Emperor [[Hirohito]] was a fraud, arguing that Hirohito's entire line is descended from the Northern Court. Despite this, he was not arrested for [[lèse majesté]], even when donning the Imperial Crest. He could and did produce a [[koseki]] detailing his bloodline back to Go-Daigo in Yoshino, but his claims and rhetoric failed to inspire anything other than sympathy.Dower, John W. (1999). [http://books.google.com/books?id=hae0dC_NaiUC&pg=PA306&dq=Kumazawa+Hiromichi&client=firefox-a#v=onepage&q=Kumazawa%20Hiromichi&f=false ''Embracing Defeat: Japan in the Wake of World War II,'' pp. 306-307.] ===Southern Court Emperors=== These are the ''Nanchō'' or Southern Court emperors: *[[Emperor Go-Daigo]] 1336-1339.Titsingh, pp. 281-295; Varley, H. Paul. (1980). ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 241-269. *[[Emperor Go-Murakami]] 1339-1368.Titsingh, pp. 295-308; Varley, pp. 269-270. *[[Emperor Chōkei]] 1368-1383.Titsingh, p. 308; Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?client=firefox-a&id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&q=chokei#search_anchor ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 158.] *[[Emperor Go-Kameyama]] 1383-1392.Titsingh, p. 320. ==Re-unification of Imperial Courts== Go-Kameyama reached an agreement with Go-Komatsu to return to the old alternations on a ten-year plan. However, Go-Komatsu broke this promise, not only ruling for 20 years, but being succeeded by his own son, rather than by one from the former Southern Court. == Notes == {{reflist|2}} ==References == {{refbegin}} * Dower, John W. (1999). ''[[Embracing Defeat|Embracing Defeat: Japan in the Wake of World War II]].'' New York: [[W. W. Norton]]. 10-ISBN 0-393-04686-9; 13-ISBN 978-0-393-04686-1; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/39143090 OCLC 39143090] * Mehl, Margaret. (1997). ''History and the State in Nineteenth-Century Japan.'' New York: [[St Martin's Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-312-21160-0; 13-ISBN 978-0-312-21160-8; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/419870136?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 419870136] * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * Thomas, Julia Adeney. (2001). ''Reconfiguring Modernity: Concepts of Nature in Japanese Political Ideology.'' Berkeley: [[University of California Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-520-22854-5; 13-ISBN 978-0-520-22854-2; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/47916285 OCLC 47916285] * {{fr icon}} [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh]], Isaac, ed. (1834). ''[[Nipon o daï itsi ran]]''; ou, [http://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon.''] (écrit par Hayashi Gahō en 1652). Paris: Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/251800045 OCLC 251800045] * Varley, H. Paul, ed. (1980). ''A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns: [[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'' (translated from the 1359 [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]] work). New York: Columbia University Press. 10-ISBN 0-231-04940-4; 13-ISBN 978-0-231-04940-5; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/311157159?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 311157159] {{refend}} {{coord missing}} [[Category:Feudal Japan]] [[Category:Japanese nobility]] [[Category:Former countries in Japanese history]]
