monarchy|WD=Q184558|lt=Absolute monarchy}} | title_leader = {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor}} | leader1 = {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Go-Daigo}} | year_leader1 = 1336–1339 | leader2 = {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|Emperor Go-Murakami|WD=Q440447|lt=Go-Murakami}} | year_leader2 = 1339–1368 | leader3 = {{ill|Emperor Chōkei|en|Emperor Chōkei|WD=Q464039|lt=Chōkei}} | year_leader3 = 1368–1383 | leader4 = {{ill|Emperor Go-Kameyama|en|Emperor Go-Kameyama|WD=Q451864|lt=Go-Kameyama}} | year_leader4 = 1383–1392 | religion = [[Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu shūgō]] | common_languages = {{ill|Late Middle Japanese|en|Late Middle Japanese|WD=Q1816184|lt=Late Middle Japanese}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Southern Court'''|南朝|''Nanchō''}} were a set of four emperors ({{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo}} and his line) whose {{ill|Royalist|en|Royalist|WD=Q6505435|lt=claims to sovereignty}} during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period}} spanning from 1336 through 1392 were usurped by the {{ill|Northern Court|en|Northern Court|WD=Q1201819|lt=Northern Court}}. This period ended with the Southern Court definitively losing the war, and they were forced to completely submit sovereignty to the Northern Court. This had the result that, while later Japanese sovereigns were descended from the Northern Court, posterity assigns sole legitimacy during this period to the Southern Court. The Southern descendants are also known as the "junior line" and the {{nihongo|Daikakuji line|大覚寺統|Daikakuji-tō}}, {{ill|Daikaku-ji|en|Daikaku-ji|WD=Q1157585|lt=Daikaku-ji}} being the {{ill|Cloistered rule|en|Cloistered rule|WD=Q865416|lt=cloistered}} home of [[:en:Go-Uda|Go-Uda]], a Southern ruler.{{cite book|last1=Kanai|first1=Madoka|last2=Nitta|first2=Hideharu |last3=Yamagiwa|first3=Joseph Koshimi |title=A Topical History of Japan |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=EzI8AAAAMAAJ&pg=RA1-PA42 |year=1966|publisher=UM Libraries |page=42|id=UOM:39015005373116}} Because it was based in {{ill|Yoshino, Nara|en|Yoshino, Nara|WD=Q1203731|lt=Yoshino, Nara}}, it is also called the {{nihongo|Yoshino court|吉野朝廷|Yoshino chōtei}}.{{cite book|last=Brownlee|first=John S. |title=Japanese Historians and the National Myths |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gLB7XUPTKlsC&pg=PA122|year=2011|publisher=UBC Press|isbn=978-0-7748-4254-9 |page=122|quote= [A 1911 Japanese textbook reads: 'After 1336,] the Yoshino Court was called the Southern Court, and the Kyoto Court was called the Northern Court.'}} ==Nanboku-chō overview== [[File:Nanbokucho-capitals.svg|thumb|The Imperial seats during the ''Nanboku-chō'' period were in relatively close proximity, but geographically distinct. They were conventionally identified as: {{unordered list|Northern capital: {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} |Southern capital: {{ill|Yoshino, Nara|en|Yoshino, Nara|WD=Q1203731|lt=Yoshino}}.}}]] The genesis of the Northern Court go back to {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|en|Emperor Go-Saga|WD=Q349259|lt=Emperor Go-Saga}}, who reigned from 1242 through 1246.Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). ''Annales des empereurs du japon'', pp. 245-247. Go-Saga was succeeded by two of his sons, {{ill|Emperor Go-Fukakusa|en|Emperor Go-Fukakusa|WD=Q349246|lt=Emperor Go-Fukakusa}}Titsingh, pp. 248-255. and {{ill|Emperor Kameyama|en|Emperor Kameyama|WD=Q360972|lt=Emperor Kameyama}}, who took turns on the throne.Titsingh, pp. 255-261. This was because on his death bed in 1272, Go-Saga had insisted that his sons adopt a plan in which future emperors from the two fraternal lines would ascend the throne in alternating succession.Titsingh, p. 261. This plan proved to be unworkable, resulting in rival factions and rival claimants to the throne. ==Northern Court== In 1333, when the Southern {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo}} staged the [[Kenmu Restoration]] and revolted against the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate}}, the ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}'' responded by declaring {{ill|Emperor Kōgon|en|Emperor Kōgon|WD=Q471885|lt=Emperor Kōgon}}, Go-Daigo's [[:en:Second cousin once removed|Second cousin once removed]] and the son of an earlier emperor, {{ill|Emperor Go-Fushimi|en|Emperor Go-Fushimi|WD=Q440454|lt=Emperor Go-Fushimi}} of the ''Jimyōin-tō'', as the new emperor. After the destruction of the Kamakura shogunate in 1333, Kōgon lost his claim, but his brother, {{ill|Emperor Kōmyō|en|Emperor Kōmyō|WD=Q555520|lt=Emperor Kōmyō}}, and two of his sons were supported by the new {{ill|Ashikaga shogunate|en|Ashikaga shogunate|WD=Q1154406|lt=Ashikaga ''shōguns''}} as the rightful claimants to the throne. Kōgon's family thus formed an alternate Imperial Court in Kyoto, which came to be called the Northern Court because its seat was in a location north of its rival. During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}, an Imperial decree dated April 3, 1911 established that the legitimate reigning monarchs of this period were the direct descendants of {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo}} through {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|Emperor Go-Murakami|WD=Q440447|lt=Emperor Go-Murakami}}, whose Southern Court had been established in exile in [[:en:Yoshino District|Yoshino]], near Nara.Thomas, Julia Adeney. (2001). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Re4djF3oaTMC&dq=1911+texbook+controversy&pg=RA1-PA199 ''Reconfiguring modernity: concepts of nature in Japanese political ideology,'' p. 199 n57], citing Mehl, Margaret. (1997). ''History and the State in Nineteenth-Century Japan.'' p. 140-147. The {{ill|Northern Court|en|Northern Court|WD=Q1201819|lt=Northern Court}} established in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} by {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji}} is therefore considered illegitimate. ===Northern Pretenders=== These are the ''Hokuchō'' or Northern Court emperors: *{{ill|Emperor Kōgon|en|Emperor Kōgon|WD=Q471885|lt=Emperor Kōgon}} 1332–1333.Titsingh, pp. 286–289. * – *{{ill|Emperor Kōmyō|en|Emperor Kōmyō|WD=Q555520|lt=Emperor Kōmyō}} 1336–1348.Titsingh, pp. 294–298. *{{ill|Emperor Sukō|en|Emperor Sukō|WD=Q471888|lt=Emperor Sukō}} 1348–1351.Titsingh, pp. 298–301. *– *{{ill|Emperor Go-Kōgon|en|Emperor Go-Kōgon|WD=Q555465|lt=Emperor Go-Kōgon}} 1352–1371.Titsingh, pp. 302–309. *{{ill|Emperor Go-En'yū|en|Emperor Go-En'yū|WD=Q471799|lt=Emperor Go-En'yū}} 1371–1382.Titsingh, pp. 310–316, 320. *{{ill|Emperor Go-Komatsu|en|Emperor Go-Komatsu|WD=Q123052|lt=Emperor Go-Komatsu}} 1382–1392 (then went on to reign as legitimate emperor 1392–1412)Titsingh, pp. 317–327. The Imperial Court supported by the Ashikaga ''shōguns'' was rivaled by the Southern Court of Go-Daigo and his descendants. This came to be called the Southern Court because its seat was in a location south of its rival. Although the precise location of the emperors' seat did change, it was often identified as simply {{ill|Yoshino, Nara|en|Yoshino, Nara|WD=Q1203731|lt=Yoshino}}. In 1392, {{ill|Emperor Go-Kameyama|en|Emperor Go-Kameyama|WD=Q451864|lt=Emperor Go-Kameyama}} of the Southern Court was defeated and abdicated in favor of Kōgon's great-grandson, {{ill|Emperor Go-Komatsu|en|Emperor Go-Komatsu|WD=Q123052|lt=Emperor Go-Komatsu}}, thus ending the divide. But the Northern Court was under the power of the Ashikaga ''shōguns'' and had little real independence. Partly because of this, since the 19th century, the Emperors of the Southern Imperial Court have been considered the legitimate Emperors of Japan. Moreover, the Southern Court controlled the Japanese imperial regalia. The Northern Court members are officially called {{ill|Pretender|en|Pretender|WD=Q845929|lt=Pretender}}s. One Southern Court descendant, {{ill|Kumazawa Hiromichi|en|Kumazawa Hiromichi|WD=Q1094280|lt=Kumazawa Hiromichi}}, declared himself to be Japan's rightful Emperor in the days after the end of the Pacific War in {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War}}. He claimed that Emperor {{ill|Hirohito|en|Hirohito|WD=Q34479|lt=Hirohito}} was a fraud, arguing that Hirohito's entire line is descended from the Northern Court. Despite this, he was not arrested for {{ill|Lèse-majesté|en|Lèse-majesté|WD=Q1134057|lt=Lèse-majesté}}, even when donning the Imperial Crest. He could and did produce a {{ill|Koseki|en|Koseki|WD=Q2004750|lt=Koseki}} detailing his bloodline back to Go-Daigo in Yoshino, but his claims and rhetoric failed to inspire anything other than sympathy.Dower, John W. (1999). [https://archive.org/details/embracingdefeatj00dowe/page/306 ''Embracing Defeat: Japan in the Wake of World War II'', pp. 306–307.] ==Southern Court emperors== These are the ''Nanchō'' or Southern Court emperors: *{{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo}} 1336–1339.Titsingh, pp. 281–295; Varley, H. Paul. (1980). ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 241–269. *{{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|Emperor Go-Murakami|WD=Q440447|lt=Emperor Go-Murakami}} 1339–1368.Titsingh, pp. 295–308; Varley, pp. 269–270. *{{ill|Emperor Chōkei|en|Emperor Chōkei|WD=Q464039|lt=Emperor Chōkei}} 1368–1383.Titsingh, p. 308; Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=chokei ''The Imperial House of Japan'', p. 158.] *{{ill|Emperor Go-Kameyama|en|Emperor Go-Kameyama|WD=Q451864|lt=Emperor Go-Kameyama}} 1383–1392.Titsingh, p. 320. ==Re-unification Agreement== Go-Kameyama reached an agreement with Go-Komatsu to return to the old alternations on a ten-year plan. However, Go-Komatsu broke this promise, not only ruling for 20 years, but being succeeded by his own son, rather than by one from the former Southern Court. == Notes == {{reflist|2}} ==References == {{refbegin}} * Dower, John W. (1999). ''{{ill|Embracing Defeat|en|Embracing Defeat|WD=Q4185382|lt=Embracing Defeat: Japan in the Wake of World War II}}''. New York: [[:en:W. W. Norton|W. W. Norton]]. {{ISBN|978-0-393-04686-1}}; {{OCLC|39143090}} * Mehl, Margaret. (1997). ''History and the State in Nineteenth-Century Japan''. New York: [[:en:St Martin's Press|St Martin's Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-312-21160-8}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/419870136?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 419870136] * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan''.] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * Thomas, Julia Adeney. (2001). ''Reconfiguring Modernity: Concepts of Nature in Japanese Political Ideology''. Berkeley: {{ill|University of California Press|en|University of California Press|WD=Q27816|lt=University of California Press}}. {{ISBN|978-0-520-22854-2}}; {{OCLC|47916285}} * {{in lang|fr}} {{ill|Isaac Titsingh|en|Isaac Titsingh|WD=Q332779|lt=Titsingh}}, Isaac, ed. (1834). ''[[:en:Nipon o daï itsi ran|Nipon o daï itsi ran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] (written by Hayashi Gahō in 1652). Paris: Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. {{OCLC|251800045}} * Varley, H. Paul, ed. (1980). ''A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns: [[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'' (translated from the 1359 [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]] work). ''Nanchō'' | conventional_long_name = Southern Court | common_name = Southern Court | year_start = 1338 | year_end = 1392 | image_flag = Nishiki no Mihata.svg | flag = List of Japanese flags | flag_type = Military flag | image_coat = Imperial_Seal_of_Japan.svg | symbol_type = {{ill|Seal (emblem)|en|Seal (emblem)|WD=Q162919|lt=Imperial Seal}} | p1 = Kenmu Restoration | flag_p1 = Imperial_Seal_of_Japan.svg | s1 = Imperial House of Japan | flag_s2 = Imperial_Seal_of_Japan.svg | s2 = Later Southern Court | flag_s1 = Imperial_Seal_of_Japan.svg | s3 = Ashikaga shogunate | flag_s3 = Ashikaga_mon.svg | event_start = Fall of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} | date_start = February 23 | date_end = August 11 | event_end = Re-unification of Imperial courts | capital = {{ill|Yoshino, Nara|en|Yoshino, Nara|WD=Q1203731|lt=Yoshino}}, {{ill|Yoshino Province|en|Yoshino Province|WD=Q1371179|lt=Yoshino Province}} | government_type = {{ill|Absolute monarchy|en|Absolute monarchy|WD=Q184558|lt=Absolute monarchy}} | title_leader = {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor}} | leader1 = {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Go-Daigo}} | year_leader1 = 1336–1339 | leader2 = {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|Emperor Go-Murakami|WD=Q440447|lt=Go-Murakami}} | year_leader2 = 1339–1368 | leader3 = {{ill|Emperor Chōkei|en|Emperor Chōkei|WD=Q464039|lt=Chōkei}} | year_leader3 = 1368–1383 | leader4 = {{ill|Emperor Go-Kameyama|en|Emperor Go-Kameyama|WD=Q451864|lt=Go-Kameyama}} | year_leader4 = 1383–1392 | religion = [[Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu shūgō]] | common_languages = {{ill|Late Middle Japanese|en|Late Middle Japanese|WD=Q1816184|lt=Late Middle Japanese}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Southern Court'''|南朝|''Nanchō''}} were a set of four emperors ({{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo}} and his line) whose {{ill|Royalist|en|Royalist|WD=Q6505435|lt=claims to sovereignty}} during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period}} spanning from 1336 through 1392 were usurped by the {{ill|Northern Court|en|Northern Court|WD=Q1201819|lt=Northern Court}}. This period ended with the Southern Court definitively losing the war, and they were forced to completely submit sovereignty to the Northern Court. This had the result that, while later Japanese sovereigns were descended from the Northern Court, posterity assigns sole legitimacy during this period to the Southern Court. The Southern descendants are also known as the "junior line" and the {{nihongo|Daikakuji line|大覚寺統|Daikakuji-tō}}, {{ill|Daikaku-ji|en|Daikaku-ji|WD=Q1157585|lt=Daikaku-ji}} being the {{ill|Cloistered rule|en|Cloistered rule|WD=Q865416|lt=cloistered}} home of [[:en:Go-Uda|Go-Uda]], a Southern ruler.{{cite book|last1=Kanai|first1=Madoka|last2=Nitta|first2=Hideharu |last3=Yamagiwa|first3=Joseph Koshimi |title=A Topical History of Japan |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=EzI8AAAAMAAJ&pg=RA1-PA42 |year=1966|publisher=UM Libraries |page=42|id=UOM:39015005373116}} Because it was based in {{ill|Yoshino, Nara|en|Yoshino, Nara|WD=Q1203731|lt=Yoshino, Nara}}, it is also called the {{nihongo|Yoshino court|吉野朝廷|Yoshino chōtei}}.{{cite book|last=Brownlee|first=John S. |title=Japanese Historians and the National Myths |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gLB7XUPTKlsC&pg=PA122|year=2011|publisher=UBC Press|isbn=978-0-7748-4254-9 |page=122|quote= [A 1911 Japanese textbook reads: 'After 1336,] the Yoshino Court was called the Southern Court, and the Kyoto Court was called the Northern Court.'}} ==Nanboku-chō overview== [[File:Nanbokucho-capitals.svg|thumb|The Imperial seats during the ''Nanboku-chō'' period were in relatively close proximity, but geographically distinct. They were conventionally identified as: {{unordered list|Northern capital: {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} |Southern capital: {{ill|Yoshino, Nara|en|Yoshino, Nara|WD=Q1203731|lt=Yoshino}}.}}]] The genesis of the Northern Court go back to {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|en|Emperor Go-Saga|WD=Q349259|lt=Emperor Go-Saga}}, who reigned from 1242 through 1246.Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). ''Annales des empereurs du japon'', pp. 245-247. Go-Saga was succeeded by two of his sons, {{ill|Emperor Go-Fukakusa|en|Emperor Go-Fukakusa|WD=Q349246|lt=Emperor Go-Fukakusa}}Titsingh, pp. 248-255. and {{ill|Emperor Kameyama|en|Emperor Kameyama|WD=Q360972|lt=Emperor Kameyama}}, who took turns on the throne.Titsingh, pp. 255-261. This was because on his death bed in 1272, Go-Saga had insisted that his sons adopt a plan in which future emperors from the two fraternal lines would ascend the throne in alternating succession.Titsingh, p. 261. This plan proved to be unworkable, resulting in rival factions and rival claimants to the throne. ==Northern Court== In 1333, when the Southern {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo}} staged the [[Kenmu Restoration]] and revolted against the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate}}, the ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}'' responded by declaring {{ill|Emperor Kōgon|en|Emperor Kōgon|WD=Q471885|lt=Emperor Kōgon}}, Go-Daigo's [[:en:Second cousin once removed|Second cousin once removed]] and the son of an earlier emperor, {{ill|Emperor Go-Fushimi|en|Emperor Go-Fushimi|WD=Q440454|lt=Emperor Go-Fushimi}} of the ''Jimyōin-tō'', as the new emperor. After the destruction of the Kamakura shogunate in 1333, Kōgon lost his claim, but his brother, {{ill|Emperor Kōmyō|en|Emperor Kōmyō|WD=Q555520|lt=Emperor Kōmyō}}, and two of his sons were supported by the new {{ill|Ashikaga shogunate|en|Ashikaga shogunate|WD=Q1154406|lt=Ashikaga ''shōguns''}} as the rightful claimants to the throne. Kōgon's family thus formed an alternate Imperial Court in Kyoto, which came to be called the Northern Court because its seat was in a location north of its rival. During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}, an Imperial decree dated April 3, 1911 established that the legitimate reigning monarchs of this period were the direct descendants of {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo}} through {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|Emperor Go-Murakami|WD=Q440447|lt=Emperor Go-Murakami}}, whose Southern Court had been established in exile in [[:en:Yoshino District|Yoshino]], near Nara.Thomas, Julia Adeney. (2001). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Re4djF3oaTMC&dq=1911+texbook+controversy&pg=RA1-PA199 ''Reconfiguring modernity: concepts of nature in Japanese political ideology,'' p. 199 n57], citing Mehl, Margaret. (1997). ''History and the State in Nineteenth-Century Japan.'' p. 140-147. The {{ill|Northern Court|en|Northern Court|WD=Q1201819|lt=Northern Court}} established in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} by {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji}} is therefore considered illegitimate. ===Northern Pretenders=== These are the ''Hokuchō'' or Northern Court emperors: *{{ill|Emperor Kōgon|en|Emperor Kōgon|WD=Q471885|lt=Emperor Kōgon}} 1332–1333.Titsingh, pp. 286–289. * – *{{ill|Emperor Kōmyō|en|Emperor Kōmyō|WD=Q555520|lt=Emperor Kōmyō}} 1336–1348.Titsingh, pp. 294–298. *{{ill|Emperor Sukō|en|Emperor Sukō|WD=Q471888|lt=Emperor Sukō}} 1348–1351.Titsingh, pp. 298–301. *– *{{ill|Emperor Go-Kōgon|en|Emperor Go-Kōgon|WD=Q555465|lt=Emperor Go-Kōgon}} 1352–1371.Titsingh, pp. 302–309. *{{ill|Emperor Go-En'yū|en|Emperor Go-En'yū|WD=Q471799|lt=Emperor Go-En'yū}} 1371–1382.Titsingh, pp. 310–316, 320. *{{ill|Emperor Go-Komatsu|en|Emperor Go-Komatsu|WD=Q123052|lt=Emperor Go-Komatsu}} 1382–1392 (then went on to reign as legitimate emperor 1392–1412)Titsingh, pp. 317–327. The Imperial Court supported by the Ashikaga ''shōguns'' was rivaled by the Southern Court of Go-Daigo and his descendants. This came to be called the Southern Court because its seat was in a location south of its rival. Although the precise location of the emperors' seat did change, it was often identified as simply {{ill|Yoshino, Nara|en|Yoshino, Nara|WD=Q1203731|lt=Yoshino}}. In 1392, {{ill|Emperor Go-Kameyama|en|Emperor Go-Kameyama|WD=Q451864|lt=Emperor Go-Kameyama}} of the Southern Court was defeated and abdicated in favor of Kōgon's great-grandson, {{ill|Emperor Go-Komatsu|en|Emperor Go-Komatsu|WD=Q123052|lt=Emperor Go-Komatsu}}, thus ending the divide. But the Northern Court was under the power of the Ashikaga ''shōguns'' and had little real independence. Partly because of this, since the 19th century, the Emperors of the Southern Imperial Court have been considered the legitimate Emperors of Japan. Moreover, the Southern Court controlled the Japanese imperial regalia. The Northern Court members are officially called {{ill|Pretender|en|Pretender|WD=Q845929|lt=Pretender}}s. One Southern Court descendant, {{ill|Kumazawa Hiromichi|en|Kumazawa Hiromichi|WD=Q1094280|lt=Kumazawa Hiromichi}}, declared himself to be Japan's rightful Emperor in the days after the end of the Pacific War in {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War}}. He claimed that Emperor {{ill|Hirohito|en|Hirohito|WD=Q34479|lt=Hirohito}} was a fraud, arguing that Hirohito's entire line is descended from the Northern Court. Despite this, he was not arrested for {{ill|Lèse-majesté|en|Lèse-majesté|WD=Q1134057|lt=Lèse-majesté}}, even when donning the Imperial Crest. He could and did produce a {{ill|Koseki|en|Koseki|WD=Q2004750|lt=Koseki}} detailing his bloodline back to Go-Daigo in Yoshino, but his claims and rhetoric failed to inspire anything other than sympathy.Dower, John W. (1999). [https://archive.org/details/embracingdefeatj00dowe/page/306 ''Embracing Defeat: Japan in the Wake of World War II'', pp. 306–307.] ==Southern Court emperors== These are the ''Nanchō'' or Southern Court emperors: *{{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo}} 1336–1339.Titsingh, pp. 281–295; Varley, H. Paul. (1980). ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 241–269. *{{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|Emperor Go-Murakami|WD=Q440447|lt=Emperor Go-Murakami}} 1339–1368.Titsingh, pp. 295–308; Varley, pp. 269–270. *{{ill|Emperor Chōkei|en|Emperor Chōkei|WD=Q464039|lt=Emperor Chōkei}} 1368–1383.Titsingh, p. 308; Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=chokei ''The Imperial House of Japan'', p. 158.] *{{ill|Emperor Go-Kameyama|en|Emperor Go-Kameyama|WD=Q451864|lt=Emperor Go-Kameyama}} 1383–1392.Titsingh, p. 320. ==Re-unification Agreement== Go-Kameyama reached an agreement with Go-Komatsu to return to the old alternations on a ten-year plan. However, Go-Komatsu broke this promise, not only ruling for 20 years, but being succeeded by his own son, rather than by one from the former Southern Court. == Notes == {{reflist|2}} ==References == {{refbegin}} * Dower, John W. (1999). ''{{ill|Embracing Defeat|en|Embracing Defeat|WD=Q4185382|lt=Embracing Defeat: Japan in the Wake of World War II}}''. New York: [[:en:W. W. Norton|W. W. Norton]]. {{ISBN|978-0-393-04686-1}}; {{OCLC|39143090}} * Mehl, Margaret. (1997). ''History and the State in Nineteenth-Century Japan''. New York: [[:en:St Martin's Press|St Martin's Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-312-21160-8}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/419870136?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 419870136] * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan''.] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * Thomas, Julia Adeney. (2001). ''Reconfiguring Modernity: Concepts of Nature in Japanese Political Ideology''. Berkeley: {{ill|University of California Press|en|University of California Press|WD=Q27816|lt=University of California Press}}. {{ISBN|978-0-520-22854-2}}; {{OCLC|47916285}} * {{in lang|fr}} {{ill|Isaac Titsingh|en|Isaac Titsingh|WD=Q332779|lt=Titsingh}}, Isaac, ed. (1834). ''[[:en:Nipon o daï itsi ran|Nipon o daï itsi ran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] (written by Hayashi Gahō in 1652). Paris: Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. {{OCLC|251800045}} * Varley, H. Paul, ed. (1980). ''A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns: [[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'' (translated from the 1359 [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]] work). New York: Columbia University Press. {{ISBN|978-0-231-04940-5}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/311157159?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 311157159] {{refend}} {{coord missing|Japan}}]] [[en:Southern Court]] [[ckb:دادگای باشوور (ژاپۆن)]] [[cs:Jižní dvůr]] [[es:Corte del Sur]] [[fa:دربار جنوبی]] [[fr:Cour du Sud]] [[id:Pemerintahan Selatan (Jepang)]] [[it:Corte del Sud]] [[ja:南朝 (日本)]] [[ko:남조 (일본)]] [[pt:Corte do Sul (Japão)]] [[ru:Южный Двор]] [[simple:Southern Court]] [[th:ราชสำนักใต้]] [[tr:Güney Sarayı]] [[uk:Південна династія (Японія)]] [[vi:Nam triều (Nhật Bản)]] [[zh:南朝 (日本)]] {{wikidata link|Q3001082}} [[Category:Former countries in Japanese history]] [[Category:Japanese nobility]] [[Category:Nanboku-chō period|*]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]]

Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Go-Daigo}} | year_leader1 = 1336–1339 | leader2 = {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|Emperor Go-Murakami|WD=Q440447|lt=Go-Murakami}} | year_leader2 = 1339–1368 | leader3 = {{ill|Emperor Chōkei|en|Emperor Chōkei|WD=Q464039|lt=Chōkei}} | year_leader3 = 1368–1383 | leader4 = {{ill|Emperor Go-Kameyama|en|Emperor Go-Kameyama|WD=Q451864|lt=Go-Kameyama}} | year_leader4 = 1383–1392 | religion = [[Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu shūgō]] | common_languages = {{ill|Late Middle Japanese|en|Late Middle Japanese|WD=Q1816184|lt=Late Middle Japanese}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Southern Court'''|南朝|''Nanchō''}} were a set of four emperors ({{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo}} and his line) whose {{ill|Royalist|en|Royalist|WD=Q6505435|lt=claims to sovereignty}} during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period}} spanning from 1336 through 1392 were usurped by the {{ill|Northern Court|en|Northern Court|WD=Q1201819|lt=Northern Court}}. This period ended with the Southern Court definitively losing the war, and they were forced to completely submit sovereignty to the Northern Court. This had the result that, while later Japanese sovereigns were descended from the Northern Court, posterity assigns sole legitimacy during this period to the Southern Court. The Southern descendants are also known as the "junior line" and the {{nihongo|Daikakuji line|大覚寺統|Daikakuji-tō}}, {{ill|Daikaku-ji|en|Daikaku-ji|WD=Q1157585|lt=Daikaku-ji}} being the {{ill|Cloistered rule|en|Cloistered rule|WD=Q865416|lt=cloistered}} home of [[:en:Go-Uda|Go-Uda]], a Southern ruler.{{cite book|last1=Kanai|first1=Madoka|last2=Nitta|first2=Hideharu |last3=Yamagiwa|first3=Joseph Koshimi |title=A Topical History of Japan |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=EzI8AAAAMAAJ&pg=RA1-PA42 |year=1966|publisher=UM Libraries |page=42|id=UOM:39015005373116}} Because it was based in {{ill|Yoshino, Nara|en|Yoshino, Nara|WD=Q1203731|lt=Yoshino, Nara}}, it is also called the {{nihongo|Yoshino court|吉野朝廷|Yoshino chōtei}}.{{cite book|last=Brownlee|first=John S. |title=Japanese Historians and the National Myths |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gLB7XUPTKlsC&pg=PA122|year=2011|publisher=UBC Press|isbn=978-0-7748-4254-9 |page=122|quote= [A 1911 Japanese textbook reads: 'After 1336,] the Yoshino Court was called the Southern Court, and the Kyoto Court was called the Northern Court.'}} ==Nanboku-chō overview== [[File:Nanbokucho-capitals.svg|thumb|The Imperial seats during the ''Nanboku-chō'' period were in relatively close proximity, but geographically distinct. They were conventionally identified as: {{unordered list|Northern capital: {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} |Southern capital: {{ill|Yoshino, Nara|en|Yoshino, Nara|WD=Q1203731|lt=Yoshino}}.}}]] The genesis of the Northern Court go back to {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|en|Emperor Go-Saga|WD=Q349259|lt=Emperor Go-Saga}}, who reigned from 1242 through 1246.Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). ''Annales des empereurs du japon'', pp. 245-247. Go-Saga was succeeded by two of his sons, {{ill|Emperor Go-Fukakusa|en|Emperor Go-Fukakusa|WD=Q349246|lt=Emperor Go-Fukakusa}}Titsingh, pp. 248-255. and {{ill|Emperor Kameyama|en|Emperor Kameyama|WD=Q360972|lt=Emperor Kameyama}}, who took turns on the throne.Titsingh, pp. 255-261. This was because on his death bed in 1272, Go-Saga had insisted that his sons adopt a plan in which future emperors from the two fraternal lines would ascend the throne in alternating succession.Titsingh, p. 261. This plan proved to be unworkable, resulting in rival factions and rival claimants to the throne. ==Northern Court== In 1333, when the Southern {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo}} staged the [[Kenmu Restoration]] and revolted against the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate}}, the ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}'' responded by declaring {{ill|Emperor Kōgon|en|Emperor Kōgon|WD=Q471885|lt=Emperor Kōgon}}, Go-Daigo's [[:en:Second cousin once removed|Second cousin once removed]] and the son of an earlier emperor, {{ill|Emperor Go-Fushimi|en|Emperor Go-Fushimi|WD=Q440454|lt=Emperor Go-Fushimi}} of the ''Jimyōin-tō'', as the new emperor. After the destruction of the Kamakura shogunate in 1333, Kōgon lost his claim, but his brother, {{ill|Emperor Kōmyō|en|Emperor Kōmyō|WD=Q555520|lt=Emperor Kōmyō}}, and two of his sons were supported by the new {{ill|Ashikaga shogunate|en|Ashikaga shogunate|WD=Q1154406|lt=Ashikaga ''shōguns''}} as the rightful claimants to the throne. Kōgon's family thus formed an alternate Imperial Court in Kyoto, which came to be called the Northern Court because its seat was in a location north of its rival. During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}, an Imperial decree dated April 3, 1911 established that the legitimate reigning monarchs of this period were the direct descendants of {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo}} through {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|Emperor Go-Murakami|WD=Q440447|lt=Emperor Go-Murakami}}, whose Southern Court had been established in exile in [[:en:Yoshino District|Yoshino]], near Nara.Thomas, Julia Adeney. (2001). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Re4djF3oaTMC&dq=1911+texbook+controversy&pg=RA1-PA199 ''Reconfiguring modernity: concepts of nature in Japanese political ideology,'' p. 199 n57], citing Mehl, Margaret. (1997). ''History and the State in Nineteenth-Century Japan.'' p. 140-147. The {{ill|Northern Court|en|Northern Court|WD=Q1201819|lt=Northern Court}} established in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} by {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji}} is therefore considered illegitimate. ===Northern Pretenders=== These are the ''Hokuchō'' or Northern Court emperors: *{{ill|Emperor Kōgon|en|Emperor Kōgon|WD=Q471885|lt=Emperor Kōgon}} 1332–1333.Titsingh, pp. 286–289. * – *{{ill|Emperor Kōmyō|en|Emperor Kōmyō|WD=Q555520|lt=Emperor Kōmyō}} 1336–1348.Titsingh, pp. 294–298. *{{ill|Emperor Sukō|en|Emperor Sukō|WD=Q471888|lt=Emperor Sukō}} 1348–1351.Titsingh, pp. 298–301. *– *{{ill|Emperor Go-Kōgon|en|Emperor Go-Kōgon|WD=Q555465|lt=Emperor Go-Kōgon}} 1352–1371.Titsingh, pp. 302–309. *{{ill|Emperor Go-En'yū|en|Emperor Go-En'yū|WD=Q471799|lt=Emperor Go-En'yū}} 1371–1382.Titsingh, pp. 310–316, 320. *{{ill|Emperor Go-Komatsu|en|Emperor Go-Komatsu|WD=Q123052|lt=Emperor Go-Komatsu}} 1382–1392 (then went on to reign as legitimate emperor 1392–1412)Titsingh, pp. 317–327. The Imperial Court supported by the Ashikaga ''shōguns'' was rivaled by the Southern Court of Go-Daigo and his descendants. This came to be called the Southern Court because its seat was in a location south of its rival. Although the precise location of the emperors' seat did change, it was often identified as simply {{ill|Yoshino, Nara|en|Yoshino, Nara|WD=Q1203731|lt=Yoshino}}. In 1392, {{ill|Emperor Go-Kameyama|en|Emperor Go-Kameyama|WD=Q451864|lt=Emperor Go-Kameyama}} of the Southern Court was defeated and abdicated in favor of Kōgon's great-grandson, {{ill|Emperor Go-Komatsu|en|Emperor Go-Komatsu|WD=Q123052|lt=Emperor Go-Komatsu}}, thus ending the divide. But the Northern Court was under the power of the Ashikaga ''shōguns'' and had little real independence. Partly because of this, since the 19th century, the Emperors of the Southern Imperial Court have been considered the legitimate Emperors of Japan. Moreover, the Southern Court controlled the Japanese imperial regalia. The Northern Court members are officially called {{ill|Pretender|en|Pretender|WD=Q845929|lt=Pretender}}s. One Southern Court descendant, {{ill|Kumazawa Hiromichi|en|Kumazawa Hiromichi|WD=Q1094280|lt=Kumazawa Hiromichi}}, declared himself to be Japan's rightful Emperor in the days after the end of the Pacific War in {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War}}. He claimed that Emperor {{ill|Hirohito|en|Hirohito|WD=Q34479|lt=Hirohito}} was a fraud, arguing that Hirohito's entire line is descended from the Northern Court. Despite this, he was not arrested for {{ill|Lèse-majesté|en|Lèse-majesté|WD=Q1134057|lt=Lèse-majesté}}, even when donning the Imperial Crest. He could and did produce a {{ill|Koseki|en|Koseki|WD=Q2004750|lt=Koseki}} detailing his bloodline back to Go-Daigo in Yoshino, but his claims and rhetoric failed to inspire anything other than sympathy.Dower, John W. (1999). [https://archive.org/details/embracingdefeatj00dowe/page/306 ''Embracing Defeat: Japan in the Wake of World War II'', pp. 306–307.] ==Southern Court emperors== These are the ''Nanchō'' or Southern Court emperors: *{{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo}} 1336–1339.Titsingh, pp. 281–295; Varley, H. Paul. (1980). ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 241–269. *{{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|Emperor Go-Murakami|WD=Q440447|lt=Emperor Go-Murakami}} 1339–1368.Titsingh, pp. 295–308; Varley, pp. 269–270. *{{ill|Emperor Chōkei|en|Emperor Chōkei|WD=Q464039|lt=Emperor Chōkei}} 1368–1383.Titsingh, p. 308; Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=chokei ''The Imperial House of Japan'', p. 158.] *{{ill|Emperor Go-Kameyama|en|Emperor Go-Kameyama|WD=Q451864|lt=Emperor Go-Kameyama}} 1383–1392.Titsingh, p. 320. ==Re-unification Agreement== Go-Kameyama reached an agreement with Go-Komatsu to return to the old alternations on a ten-year plan. However, Go-Komatsu broke this promise, not only ruling for 20 years, but being succeeded by his own son, rather than by one from the former Southern Court. == Notes == {{reflist|2}} ==References == {{refbegin}} * Dower, John W. (1999). ''{{ill|Embracing Defeat|en|Embracing Defeat|WD=Q4185382|lt=Embracing Defeat: Japan in the Wake of World War II}}''. New York: [[:en:W. W. Norton|W. W. Norton]]. {{ISBN|978-0-393-04686-1}}; {{OCLC|39143090}} * Mehl, Margaret. (1997). ''History and the State in Nineteenth-Century Japan''. New York: [[:en:St Martin's Press|St Martin's Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-312-21160-8}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/419870136?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 419870136] * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan''.] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * Thomas, Julia Adeney. (2001). ''Reconfiguring Modernity: Concepts of Nature in Japanese Political Ideology''. Berkeley: {{ill|University of California Press|en|University of California Press|WD=Q27816|lt=University of California Press}}. {{ISBN|978-0-520-22854-2}}; {{OCLC|47916285}} * {{in lang|fr}} {{ill|Isaac Titsingh|en|Isaac Titsingh|WD=Q332779|lt=Titsingh}}, Isaac, ed. (1834). ''[[:en:Nipon o daï itsi ran|Nipon o daï itsi ran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] (written by Hayashi Gahō in 1652). Paris: Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. {{OCLC|251800045}} * Varley, H. Paul, ed. (1980). ''A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns: [[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'' (translated from the 1359 [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]] work). New York: Columbia University Press. {{ISBN|978-0-231-04940-5}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/311157159?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 311157159] {{refend}} {{coord missing|Japan}}]] [[en:Southern Court]] [[ckb:دادگای باشوور (ژاپۆن)]] [[cs:Jižní dvůr]] [[es:Corte del Sur]] [[fa:دربار جنوبی]] [[fr:Cour du Sud]] [[id:Pemerintahan Selatan (Jepang)]] [[it:Corte del Sud]] [[ja:南朝 (日本)]] [[ko:남조 (일본)]] [[pt:Corte do Sul (Japão)]] [[ru:Южный Двор]] [[simple:Southern Court]] [[th:ราชสำนักใต้]] [[tr:Güney Sarayı]] [[uk:Південна династія (Японія)]] [[vi:Nam triều (Nhật Bản)]] [[zh:南朝 (日本)]] {{wikidata link|Q3001082}} [[Category:Former countries in Japanese history]] [[Category:Japanese nobility]] [[Category:Nanboku-chō period|*]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]]

to sovereignty}} during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period}} spanning from 1336 through 1392 were usurped by the {{ill|Northern Court|en|Northern Court|WD=Q1201819|lt=Northern Court}}. This period ended with the Southern Court definitively losing the war, and they were forced to completely submit sovereignty to the Northern Court. This had the result that, while later Japanese sovereigns were descended from the Northern Court, posterity assigns sole legitimacy during this period to the Southern Court. The Southern descendants are also known as the "junior line" and the {{nihongo|Daikakuji line|大覚寺統|Daikakuji-tō}}, {{ill|Daikaku-ji|en|Daikaku-ji|WD=Q1157585|lt=Daikaku-ji}} being the {{ill|Cloistered rule|en|Cloistered rule|WD=Q865416|lt=cloistered}} home of [[:en:Go-Uda|Go-Uda]], a Southern ruler.{{cite book|last1=Kanai|first1=Madoka|last2=Nitta|first2=Hideharu |last3=Yamagiwa|first3=Joseph Koshimi |title=A Topical History of Japan |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=EzI8AAAAMAAJ&pg=RA1-PA42 |year=1966|publisher=UM Libraries |page=42|id=UOM:39015005373116}} Because it was based in {{ill|Yoshino, Nara|en|Yoshino, Nara|WD=Q1203731|lt=Yoshino, Nara}}, it is also called the {{nihongo|Yoshino court|吉野朝廷|Yoshino chōtei}}.{{cite book|last=Brownlee|first=John S. |title=Japanese Historians and the National Myths |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gLB7XUPTKlsC&pg=PA122|year=2011|publisher=UBC Press|isbn=978-0-7748-4254-9 |page=122|quote= [A 1911 Japanese textbook reads: 'After 1336,] the Yoshino Court was called the Southern Court, and the Kyoto Court was called the Northern Court.'}} ==Nanboku-chō overview== [[File:Nanbokucho-capitals.svg|thumb|The Imperial seats during the ''Nanboku-chō'' period were in relatively close proximity, but geographically distinct. They were conventionally identified as: {{unordered list|Northern capital: {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} |Southern capital: {{ill|Yoshino, Nara|en|Yoshino, Nara|WD=Q1203731|lt=Yoshino}}.}}]] The genesis of the Northern Court go back to {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|en|Emperor Go-Saga|WD=Q349259|lt=Emperor Go-Saga}}, who reigned from 1242 through 1246.Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). ''Annales des empereurs du japon'', pp. 245-247. Go-Saga was succeeded by two of his sons, {{ill|Emperor Go-Fukakusa|en|Emperor Go-Fukakusa|WD=Q349246|lt=Emperor Go-Fukakusa}}Titsingh, pp. 248-255. and {{ill|Emperor Kameyama|en|Emperor Kameyama|WD=Q360972|lt=Emperor Kameyama}}, who took turns on the throne.Titsingh, pp. 255-261. This was because on his death bed in 1272, Go-Saga had insisted that his sons adopt a plan in which future emperors from the two fraternal lines would ascend the throne in alternating succession.Titsingh, p. 261. This plan proved to be unworkable, resulting in rival factions and rival claimants to the throne. ==Northern Court== In 1333, when the Southern {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo}} staged the [[Kenmu Restoration]] and revolted against the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate}}, the ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}'' responded by declaring {{ill|Emperor Kōgon|en|Emperor Kōgon|WD=Q471885|lt=Emperor Kōgon}}, Go-Daigo's [[:en:Second cousin once removed|Second cousin once removed]] and the son of an earlier emperor, {{ill|Emperor Go-Fushimi|en|Emperor Go-Fushimi|WD=Q440454|lt=Emperor Go-Fushimi}} of the ''Jimyōin-tō'', as the new emperor. After the destruction of the Kamakura shogunate in 1333, Kōgon lost his claim, but his brother, {{ill|Emperor Kōmyō|en|Emperor Kōmyō|WD=Q555520|lt=Emperor Kōmyō}}, and two of his sons were supported by the new {{ill|Ashikaga shogunate|en|Ashikaga shogunate|WD=Q1154406|lt=Ashikaga ''shōguns''}} as the rightful claimants to the throne. Kōgon's family thus formed an alternate Imperial Court in Kyoto, which came to be called the Northern Court because its seat was in a location north of its rival. During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}, an Imperial decree dated April 3, 1911 established that the legitimate reigning monarchs of this period were the direct descendants of {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo}} through {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|Emperor Go-Murakami|WD=Q440447|lt=Emperor Go-Murakami}}, whose Southern Court had been established in exile in [[:en:Yoshino District|Yoshino]], near Nara.Thomas, Julia Adeney. (2001). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Re4djF3oaTMC&dq=1911+texbook+controversy&pg=RA1-PA199 ''Reconfiguring modernity: concepts of nature in Japanese political ideology,'' p. 199 n57], citing Mehl, Margaret. (1997). ''History and the State in Nineteenth-Century Japan.'' p. 140-147. The {{ill|Northern Court|en|Northern Court|WD=Q1201819|lt=Northern Court}} established in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} by {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji}} is therefore considered illegitimate. ===Northern Pretenders=== These are the ''Hokuchō'' or Northern Court emperors: *{{ill|Emperor Kōgon|en|Emperor Kōgon|WD=Q471885|lt=Emperor Kōgon}} 1332–1333.Titsingh, pp. 286–289. * – *{{ill|Emperor Kōmyō|en|Emperor Kōmyō|WD=Q555520|lt=Emperor Kōmyō}} 1336–1348.Titsingh, pp. 294–298. *{{ill|Emperor Sukō|en|Emperor Sukō|WD=Q471888|lt=Emperor Sukō}} 1348–1351.Titsingh, pp. 298–301. *– *{{ill|Emperor Go-Kōgon|en|Emperor Go-Kōgon|WD=Q555465|lt=Emperor Go-Kōgon}} 1352–1371.Titsingh, pp. 302–309. *{{ill|Emperor Go-En'yū|en|Emperor Go-En'yū|WD=Q471799|lt=Emperor Go-En'yū}} 1371–1382.Titsingh, pp. 310–316, 320. *{{ill|Emperor Go-Komatsu|en|Emperor Go-Komatsu|WD=Q123052|lt=Emperor Go-Komatsu}} 1382–1392 (then went on to reign as legitimate emperor 1392–1412)Titsingh, pp. 317–327. The Imperial Court supported by the Ashikaga ''shōguns'' was rivaled by the Southern Court of Go-Daigo and his descendants. This came to be called the Southern Court because its seat was in a location south of its rival. Although the precise location of the emperors' seat did change, it was often identified as simply {{ill|Yoshino, Nara|en|Yoshino, Nara|WD=Q1203731|lt=Yoshino}}. In 1392, {{ill|Emperor Go-Kameyama|en|Emperor Go-Kameyama|WD=Q451864|lt=Emperor Go-Kameyama}} of the Southern Court was defeated and abdicated in favor of Kōgon's great-grandson, {{ill|Emperor Go-Komatsu|en|Emperor Go-Komatsu|WD=Q123052|lt=Emperor Go-Komatsu}}, thus ending the divide. But the Northern Court was under the power of the Ashikaga ''shōguns'' and had little real independence. Partly because of this, since the 19th century, the Emperors of the Southern Imperial Court have been considered the legitimate Emperors of Japan. Moreover, the Southern Court controlled the Japanese imperial regalia. The Northern Court members are officially called {{ill|Pretender|en|Pretender|WD=Q845929|lt=Pretender}}s. One Southern Court descendant, {{ill|Kumazawa Hiromichi|en|Kumazawa Hiromichi|WD=Q1094280|lt=Kumazawa Hiromichi}}, declared himself to be Japan's rightful Emperor in the days after the end of the Pacific War in {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War}}. He claimed that Emperor {{ill|Hirohito|en|Hirohito|WD=Q34479|lt=Hirohito}} was a fraud, arguing that Hirohito's entire line is descended from the Northern Court. Despite this, he was not arrested for {{ill|Lèse-majesté|en|Lèse-majesté|WD=Q1134057|lt=Lèse-majesté}}, even when donning the Imperial Crest. He could and did produce a {{ill|Koseki|en|Koseki|WD=Q2004750|lt=Koseki}} detailing his bloodline back to Go-Daigo in Yoshino, but his claims and rhetoric failed to inspire anything other than sympathy.Dower, John W. (1999). [https://archive.org/details/embracingdefeatj00dowe/page/306 ''Embracing Defeat: Japan in the Wake of World War II'', pp. 306–307.] ==Southern Court emperors== These are the ''Nanchō'' or Southern Court emperors: *{{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo}} 1336–1339.Titsingh, pp. 281–295; Varley, H. Paul. (1980). ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 241–269. *{{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|Emperor Go-Murakami|WD=Q440447|lt=Emperor Go-Murakami}} 1339–1368.Titsingh, pp. 295–308; Varley, pp. 269–270. *{{ill|Emperor Chōkei|en|Emperor Chōkei|WD=Q464039|lt=Emperor Chōkei}} 1368–1383.Titsingh, p. 308; Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=chokei ''The Imperial House of Japan'', p. 158.] *{{ill|Emperor Go-Kameyama|en|Emperor Go-Kameyama|WD=Q451864|lt=Emperor Go-Kameyama}} 1383–1392.Titsingh, p. 320. ==Re-unification Agreement== Go-Kameyama reached an agreement with Go-Komatsu to return to the old alternations on a ten-year plan. However, Go-Komatsu broke this promise, not only ruling for 20 years, but being succeeded by his own son, rather than by one from the former Southern Court. == Notes == {{reflist|2}} ==References == {{refbegin}} * Dower, John W. (1999). ''{{ill|Embracing Defeat|en|Embracing Defeat|WD=Q4185382|lt=Embracing Defeat: Japan in the Wake of World War II}}''. New York: [[:en:W. W. Norton|W. W. Norton]]. {{ISBN|978-0-393-04686-1}}; {{OCLC|39143090}} * Mehl, Margaret. (1997). ''History and the State in Nineteenth-Century Japan''. New York: [[:en:St Martin's Press|St Martin's Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-312-21160-8}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/419870136?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 419870136] * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan''.] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * Thomas, Julia Adeney. (2001). ''Reconfiguring Modernity: Concepts of Nature in Japanese Political Ideology''. Berkeley: {{ill|University of California Press|en|University of California Press|WD=Q27816|lt=University of California Press}}. {{ISBN|978-0-520-22854-2}}; {{OCLC|47916285}} * {{in lang|fr}} {{ill|Isaac Titsingh|en|Isaac Titsingh|WD=Q332779|lt=Titsingh}}, Isaac, ed. (1834). ''[[:en:Nipon o daï itsi ran|Nipon o daï itsi ran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] (written by Hayashi Gahō in 1652). Paris: Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. {{OCLC|251800045}} * Varley, H. Paul, ed. (1980). ''A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns: [[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'' (translated from the 1359 [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]] work). New York: Columbia University Press. {{ISBN|978-0-231-04940-5}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/311157159?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 311157159] {{refend}} {{coord missing|Japan}}]] [[en:Southern Court]] [[ckb:دادگای باشوور (ژاپۆن)]] [[cs:Jižní dvůr]] [[es:Corte del Sur]] [[fa:دربار جنوبی]] [[fr:Cour du Sud]] [[id:Pemerintahan Selatan (Jepang)]] [[it:Corte del Sud]] [[ja:南朝 (日本)]] [[ko:남조 (일본)]] [[pt:Corte do Sul (Japão)]] [[ru:Южный Двор]] [[simple:Southern Court]] [[th:ราชสำนักใต้]] [[tr:Güney Sarayı]] [[uk:Південна династія (Японія)]] [[vi:Nam triều (Nhật Bản)]] [[zh:南朝 (日本)]] {{wikidata link|Q3001082}} [[Category:Former countries in Japanese history]] [[Category:Japanese nobility]] [[Category:Nanboku-chō period|*]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

''Nanchō'' | conventional_long_name = Southern Court | common_name = Southern Court | year_start = 1338 | year_end = 1392 | image_flag = Nishiki no Mihata.svg | flag = List of Japanese flags | flag_type = Military flag | image_coat = Imperial_Seal_of_Japan.svg | symbol_type = {{ill|Seal (emblem)|en|Seal (emblem)|WD=Q162919|lt=Imperial Seal}} | p1 = Kenmu Restoration | flag_p1 = Imperial_Seal_of_Japan.svg | s1 = Imperial House of Japan | flag_s2 = Imperial_Seal_of_Japan.svg | s2 = Later Southern Court | flag_s1 = Imperial_Seal_of_Japan.svg | s3 = Ashikaga shogunate | flag_s3 = Ashikaga_mon.svg | event_start = Fall of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} | date_start = February 23 | date_end = August 11 | event_end = Re-unification of Imperial courts | capital = {{ill|Yoshino, Nara|en|Yoshino, Nara|WD=Q1203731|lt=Yoshino}}, {{ill|Yoshino Province|en|Yoshino Province|WD=Q1371179|lt=Yoshino Province}} | government_type = {{ill|Absolute monarchy|en|Absolute monarchy|WD=Q184558|lt=Absolute monarchy}} | title_leader = {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor}} | leader1 = {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Go-Daigo}} | year_leader1 = 1336–1339 | leader2 = {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|Emperor Go-Murakami|WD=Q440447|lt=Go-Murakami}} | year_leader2 = 1339–1368 | leader3 = {{ill|Emperor Chōkei|en|Emperor Chōkei|WD=Q464039|lt=Chōkei}} | year_leader3 = 1368–1383 | leader4 = {{ill|Emperor Go-Kameyama|en|Emperor Go-Kameyama|WD=Q451864|lt=Go-Kameyama}} | year_leader4 = 1383–1392 | religion = [[Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu shūgō]] | common_languages = {{ill|Late Middle Japanese|en|Late Middle Japanese|WD=Q1816184|lt=Late Middle Japanese}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Southern Court'''|南朝|''Nanchō''}} were a set of four emperors ({{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo}} and his line) whose {{ill|Royalist|en|Royalist|WD=Q6505435|lt=claims to sovereignty}} during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period}} spanning from 1336 through 1392 were usurped by the {{ill|Northern Court|en|Northern Court|WD=Q1201819|lt=Northern Court}}. This period ended with the Southern Court definitively losing the war, and they were forced to completely submit sovereignty to the Northern Court. This had the result that, while later Japanese sovereigns were descended from the Northern Court, posterity assigns sole legitimacy during this period to the Southern Court. The Southern descendants are also known as the "junior line" and the {{nihongo|Daikakuji line|大覚寺統|Daikakuji-tō}}, {{ill|Daikaku-ji|en|Daikaku-ji|WD=Q1157585|lt=Daikaku-ji}} being the {{ill|Cloistered rule|en|Cloistered rule|WD=Q865416|lt=cloistered}} home of [[:en:Go-Uda|Go-Uda]], a Southern ruler.{{cite book|last1=Kanai|first1=Madoka|last2=Nitta|first2=Hideharu |last3=Yamagiwa|first3=Joseph Koshimi |title=A Topical History of Japan |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=EzI8AAAAMAAJ&pg=RA1-PA42 |year=1966|publisher=UM Libraries |page=42|id=UOM:39015005373116}} Because it was based in {{ill|Yoshino, Nara|en|Yoshino, Nara|WD=Q1203731|lt=Yoshino, Nara}}, it is also called the {{nihongo|Yoshino court|吉野朝廷|Yoshino chōtei}}.{{cite book|last=Brownlee|first=John S. |title=Japanese Historians and the National Myths |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gLB7XUPTKlsC&pg=PA122|year=2011|publisher=UBC Press|isbn=978-0-7748-4254-9 |page=122|quote= [A 1911 Japanese textbook reads: 'After 1336,] the Yoshino Court was called the Southern Court, and the Kyoto Court was called the Northern Court.'}} ==Nanboku-chō overview== [[File:Nanbokucho-capitals.svg|thumb|The Imperial seats during the ''Nanboku-chō'' period were in relatively close proximity, but geographically distinct. They were conventionally identified as: {{unordered list|Northern capital: {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} |Southern capital: {{ill|Yoshino, Nara|en|Yoshino, Nara|WD=Q1203731|lt=Yoshino}}.}}]] The genesis of the Northern Court go back to {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|en|Emperor Go-Saga|WD=Q349259|lt=Emperor Go-Saga}}, who reigned from 1242 through 1246.Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). ''Annales des empereurs du japon'', pp. 245-247. Go-Saga was succeeded by two of his sons, {{ill|Emperor Go-Fukakusa|en|Emperor Go-Fukakusa|WD=Q349246|lt=Emperor Go-Fukakusa}}Titsingh, pp. 248-255. and {{ill|Emperor Kameyama|en|Emperor Kameyama|WD=Q360972|lt=Emperor Kameyama}}, who took turns on the throne.Titsingh, pp. 255-261. This was because on his death bed in 1272, Go-Saga had insisted that his sons adopt a plan in which future emperors from the two fraternal lines would ascend the throne in alternating succession.Titsingh, p. 261. This plan proved to be unworkable, resulting in rival factions and rival claimants to the throne. ==Northern Court== In 1333, when the Southern {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo}} staged the [[Kenmu Restoration]] and revolted against the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate}}, the ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}'' responded by declaring {{ill|Emperor Kōgon|en|Emperor Kōgon|WD=Q471885|lt=Emperor Kōgon}}, Go-Daigo's [[:en:Second cousin once removed|Second cousin once removed]] and the son of an earlier emperor, {{ill|Emperor Go-Fushimi|en|Emperor Go-Fushimi|WD=Q440454|lt=Emperor Go-Fushimi}} of the ''Jimyōin-tō'', as the new emperor. After the destruction of the Kamakura shogunate in 1333, Kōgon lost his claim, but his brother, {{ill|Emperor Kōmyō|en|Emperor Kōmyō|WD=Q555520|lt=Emperor Kōmyō}}, and two of his sons were supported by the new {{ill|Ashikaga shogunate|en|Ashikaga shogunate|WD=Q1154406|lt=Ashikaga ''shōguns''}} as the rightful claimants to the throne. Kōgon's family thus formed an alternate Imperial Court in Kyoto, which came to be called the Northern Court because its seat was in a location north of its rival. During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}, an Imperial decree dated April 3, 1911 established that the legitimate reigning monarchs of this period were the direct descendants of {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo}} through {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|Emperor Go-Murakami|WD=Q440447|lt=Emperor Go-Murakami}}, whose Southern Court had been established in exile in [[:en:Yoshino District|Yoshino]], near Nara.Thomas, Julia Adeney. (2001). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Re4djF3oaTMC&dq=1911+texbook+controversy&pg=RA1-PA199 ''Reconfiguring modernity: concepts of nature in Japanese political ideology,'' p. 199 n57], citing Mehl, Margaret. (1997). ''History and the State in Nineteenth-Century Japan.'' p. 140-147. The {{ill|Northern Court|en|Northern Court|WD=Q1201819|lt=Northern Court}} established in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} by {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji}} is therefore considered illegitimate. ===Northern Pretenders=== These are the ''Hokuchō'' or Northern Court emperors: *{{ill|Emperor Kōgon|en|Emperor Kōgon|WD=Q471885|lt=Emperor Kōgon}} 1332–1333.Titsingh, pp. 286–289. * – *{{ill|Emperor Kōmyō|en|Emperor Kōmyō|WD=Q555520|lt=Emperor Kōmyō}} 1336–1348.Titsingh, pp. 294–298. *{{ill|Emperor Sukō|en|Emperor Sukō|WD=Q471888|lt=Emperor Sukō}} 1348–1351.Titsingh, pp. 298–301. *– *{{ill|Emperor Go-Kōgon|en|Emperor Go-Kōgon|WD=Q555465|lt=Emperor Go-Kōgon}} 1352–1371.Titsingh, pp. 302–309. *{{ill|Emperor Go-En'yū|en|Emperor Go-En'yū|WD=Q471799|lt=Emperor Go-En'yū}} 1371–1382.Titsingh, pp. 310–316, 320. *{{ill|Emperor Go-Komatsu|en|Emperor Go-Komatsu|WD=Q123052|lt=Emperor Go-Komatsu}} 1382–1392 (then went on to reign as legitimate emperor 1392–1412)Titsingh, pp. 317–327. The Imperial Court supported by the Ashikaga ''shōguns'' was rivaled by the Southern Court of Go-Daigo and his descendants. This came to be called the Southern Court because its seat was in a location south of its rival. Although the precise location of the emperors' seat did change, it was often identified as simply {{ill|Yoshino, Nara|en|Yoshino, Nara|WD=Q1203731|lt=Yoshino}}. In 1392, {{ill|Emperor Go-Kameyama|en|Emperor Go-Kameyama|WD=Q451864|lt=Emperor Go-Kameyama}} of the Southern Court was defeated and abdicated in favor of Kōgon's great-grandson, {{ill|Emperor Go-Komatsu|en|Emperor Go-Komatsu|WD=Q123052|lt=Emperor Go-Komatsu}}, thus ending the divide. But the Northern Court was under the power of the Ashikaga ''shōguns'' and had little real independence. Partly because of this, since the 19th century, the Emperors of the Southern Imperial Court have been considered the legitimate Emperors of Japan. Moreover, the Southern Court controlled the Japanese imperial regalia. The Northern Court members are officially called {{ill|Pretender|en|Pretender|WD=Q845929|lt=Pretender}}s. One Southern Court descendant, {{ill|Kumazawa Hiromichi|en|Kumazawa Hiromichi|WD=Q1094280|lt=Kumazawa Hiromichi}}, declared himself to be Japan's rightful Emperor in the days after the end of the Pacific War in {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War}}. He claimed that Emperor {{ill|Hirohito|en|Hirohito|WD=Q34479|lt=Hirohito}} was a fraud, arguing that Hirohito's entire line is descended from the Northern Court. Despite this, he was not arrested for {{ill|Lèse-majesté|en|Lèse-majesté|WD=Q1134057|lt=Lèse-majesté}}, even when donning the Imperial Crest. He could and did produce a {{ill|Koseki|en|Koseki|WD=Q2004750|lt=Koseki}} detailing his bloodline back to Go-Daigo in Yoshino, but his claims and rhetoric failed to inspire anything other than sympathy.Dower, John W. (1999). [https://archive.org/details/embracingdefeatj00dowe/page/306 ''Embracing Defeat: Japan in the Wake of World War II'', pp. 306–307.] ==Southern Court emperors== These are the ''Nanchō'' or Southern Court emperors: *{{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo}} 1336–1339.Titsingh, pp. 281–295; Varley, H. Paul. (1980). ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 241–269. *{{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|Emperor Go-Murakami|WD=Q440447|lt=Emperor Go-Murakami}} 1339–1368.Titsingh, pp. 295–308; Varley, pp. 269–270. *{{ill|Emperor Chōkei|en|Emperor Chōkei|WD=Q464039|lt=Emperor Chōkei}} 1368–1383.Titsingh, p. 308; Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=chokei ''The Imperial House of Japan'', p. 158.] *{{ill|Emperor Go-Kameyama|en|Emperor Go-Kameyama|WD=Q451864|lt=Emperor Go-Kameyama}} 1383–1392.Titsingh, p. 320. ==Re-unification Agreement== Go-Kameyama reached an agreement with Go-Komatsu to return to the old alternations on a ten-year plan. However, Go-Komatsu broke this promise, not only ruling for 20 years, but being succeeded by his own son, rather than by one from the former Southern Court. == Notes == {{reflist|2}} ==References == {{refbegin}} * Dower, John W. (1999). ''{{ill|Embracing Defeat|en|Embracing Defeat|WD=Q4185382|lt=Embracing Defeat: Japan in the Wake of World War II}}''. New York: [[:en:W. W. Norton|W. W. Norton]]. {{ISBN|978-0-393-04686-1}}; {{OCLC|39143090}} * Mehl, Margaret. (1997). ''History and the State in Nineteenth-Century Japan''. New York: [[:en:St Martin's Press|St Martin's Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-312-21160-8}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/419870136?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 419870136] * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan''.] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * Thomas, Julia Adeney. (2001). ''Reconfiguring Modernity: Concepts of Nature in Japanese Political Ideology''. Berkeley: {{ill|University of California Press|en|University of California Press|WD=Q27816|lt=University of California Press}}. {{ISBN|978-0-520-22854-2}}; {{OCLC|47916285}} * {{in lang|fr}} {{ill|Isaac Titsingh|en|Isaac Titsingh|WD=Q332779|lt=Titsingh}}, Isaac, ed. (1834). ''[[:en:Nipon o daï itsi ran|Nipon o daï itsi ran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] (written by Hayashi Gahō in 1652). Paris: Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. {{OCLC|251800045}} * Varley, H. Paul, ed. (1980). ''A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns: [[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'' (translated from the 1359 [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]] work). ''Nanchō'' | conventional_long_name = Southern Court | common_name = Southern Court | year_start = 1338 | year_end = 1392 | image_flag = Nishiki no Mihata.svg | flag = List of Japanese flags | flag_type = Military flag | image_coat = Imperial_Seal_of_Japan.svg | symbol_type = {{ill|Seal (emblem)|en|Seal (emblem)|WD=Q162919|lt=Imperial Seal}} | p1 = Kenmu Restoration | flag_p1 = Imperial_Seal_of_Japan.svg | s1 = Imperial House of Japan | flag_s2 = Imperial_Seal_of_Japan.svg | s2 = Later Southern Court | flag_s1 = Imperial_Seal_of_Japan.svg | s3 = Ashikaga shogunate | flag_s3 = Ashikaga_mon.svg | event_start = Fall of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} | date_start = February 23 | date_end = August 11 | event_end = Re-unification of Imperial courts | capital = {{ill|Yoshino, Nara|en|Yoshino, Nara|WD=Q1203731|lt=Yoshino}}, {{ill|Yoshino Province|en|Yoshino Province|WD=Q1371179|lt=Yoshino Province}} | government_type = {{ill|Absolute monarchy|en|Absolute monarchy|WD=Q184558|lt=Absolute monarchy}} | title_leader = {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor}} | leader1 = {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Go-Daigo}} | year_leader1 = 1336–1339 | leader2 = {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|Emperor Go-Murakami|WD=Q440447|lt=Go-Murakami}} | year_leader2 = 1339–1368 | leader3 = {{ill|Emperor Chōkei|en|Emperor Chōkei|WD=Q464039|lt=Chōkei}} | year_leader3 = 1368–1383 | leader4 = {{ill|Emperor Go-Kameyama|en|Emperor Go-Kameyama|WD=Q451864|lt=Go-Kameyama}} | year_leader4 = 1383–1392 | religion = [[Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu shūgō]] | common_languages = {{ill|Late Middle Japanese|en|Late Middle Japanese|WD=Q1816184|lt=Late Middle Japanese}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Southern Court'''|南朝|''Nanchō''}} were a set of four emperors ({{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo}} and his line) whose {{ill|Royalist|en|Royalist|WD=Q6505435|lt=claims to sovereignty}} during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period}} spanning from 1336 through 1392 were usurped by the {{ill|Northern Court|en|Northern Court|WD=Q1201819|lt=Northern Court}}. This period ended with the Southern Court definitively losing the war, and they were forced to completely submit sovereignty to the Northern Court. This had the result that, while later Japanese sovereigns were descended from the Northern Court, posterity assigns sole legitimacy during this period to the Southern Court. The Southern descendants are also known as the "junior line" and the {{nihongo|Daikakuji line|大覚寺統|Daikakuji-tō}}, {{ill|Daikaku-ji|en|Daikaku-ji|WD=Q1157585|lt=Daikaku-ji}} being the {{ill|Cloistered rule|en|Cloistered rule|WD=Q865416|lt=cloistered}} home of [[:en:Go-Uda|Go-Uda]], a Southern ruler.{{cite book|last1=Kanai|first1=Madoka|last2=Nitta|first2=Hideharu |last3=Yamagiwa|first3=Joseph Koshimi |title=A Topical History of Japan |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=EzI8AAAAMAAJ&pg=RA1-PA42 |year=1966|publisher=UM Libraries |page=42|id=UOM:39015005373116}} Because it was based in {{ill|Yoshino, Nara|en|Yoshino, Nara|WD=Q1203731|lt=Yoshino, Nara}}, it is also called the {{nihongo|Yoshino court|吉野朝廷|Yoshino chōtei}}.{{cite book|last=Brownlee|first=John S. |title=Japanese Historians and the National Myths |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gLB7XUPTKlsC&pg=PA122|year=2011|publisher=UBC Press|isbn=978-0-7748-4254-9 |page=122|quote= [A 1911 Japanese textbook reads: 'After 1336,] the Yoshino Court was called the Southern Court, and the Kyoto Court was called the Northern Court.'}} ==Nanboku-chō overview== [[File:Nanbokucho-capitals.svg|thumb|The Imperial seats during the ''Nanboku-chō'' period were in relatively close proximity, but geographically distinct. They were conventionally identified as: {{unordered list|Northern capital: {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} |Southern capital: {{ill|Yoshino, Nara|en|Yoshino, Nara|WD=Q1203731|lt=Yoshino}}.}}]] The genesis of the Northern Court go back to {{ill|Emperor Go-Saga|en|Emperor Go-Saga|WD=Q349259|lt=Emperor Go-Saga}}, who reigned from 1242 through 1246.Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). ''Annales des empereurs du japon'', pp. 245-247. Go-Saga was succeeded by two of his sons, {{ill|Emperor Go-Fukakusa|en|Emperor Go-Fukakusa|WD=Q349246|lt=Emperor Go-Fukakusa}}Titsingh, pp. 248-255. and {{ill|Emperor Kameyama|en|Emperor Kameyama|WD=Q360972|lt=Emperor Kameyama}}, who took turns on the throne.Titsingh, pp. 255-261. This was because on his death bed in 1272, Go-Saga had insisted that his sons adopt a plan in which future emperors from the two fraternal lines would ascend the throne in alternating succession.Titsingh, p. 261. This plan proved to be unworkable, resulting in rival factions and rival claimants to the throne. ==Northern Court== In 1333, when the Southern {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo}} staged the [[Kenmu Restoration]] and revolted against the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate}}, the ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}'' responded by declaring {{ill|Emperor Kōgon|en|Emperor Kōgon|WD=Q471885|lt=Emperor Kōgon}}, Go-Daigo's [[:en:Second cousin once removed|Second cousin once removed]] and the son of an earlier emperor, {{ill|Emperor Go-Fushimi|en|Emperor Go-Fushimi|WD=Q440454|lt=Emperor Go-Fushimi}} of the ''Jimyōin-tō'', as the new emperor. After the destruction of the Kamakura shogunate in 1333, Kōgon lost his claim, but his brother, {{ill|Emperor Kōmyō|en|Emperor Kōmyō|WD=Q555520|lt=Emperor Kōmyō}}, and two of his sons were supported by the new {{ill|Ashikaga shogunate|en|Ashikaga shogunate|WD=Q1154406|lt=Ashikaga ''shōguns''}} as the rightful claimants to the throne. Kōgon's family thus formed an alternate Imperial Court in Kyoto, which came to be called the Northern Court because its seat was in a location north of its rival. During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}, an Imperial decree dated April 3, 1911 established that the legitimate reigning monarchs of this period were the direct descendants of {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo}} through {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|Emperor Go-Murakami|WD=Q440447|lt=Emperor Go-Murakami}}, whose Southern Court had been established in exile in [[:en:Yoshino District|Yoshino]], near Nara.Thomas, Julia Adeney. (2001). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Re4djF3oaTMC&dq=1911+texbook+controversy&pg=RA1-PA199 ''Reconfiguring modernity: concepts of nature in Japanese political ideology,'' p. 199 n57], citing Mehl, Margaret. (1997). ''History and the State in Nineteenth-Century Japan.'' p. 140-147. The {{ill|Northern Court|en|Northern Court|WD=Q1201819|lt=Northern Court}} established in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} by {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji}} is therefore considered illegitimate. ===Northern Pretenders=== These are the ''Hokuchō'' or Northern Court emperors: *{{ill|Emperor Kōgon|en|Emperor Kōgon|WD=Q471885|lt=Emperor Kōgon}} 1332–1333.Titsingh, pp. 286–289. * – *{{ill|Emperor Kōmyō|en|Emperor Kōmyō|WD=Q555520|lt=Emperor Kōmyō}} 1336–1348.Titsingh, pp. 294–298. *{{ill|Emperor Sukō|en|Emperor Sukō|WD=Q471888|lt=Emperor Sukō}} 1348–1351.Titsingh, pp. 298–301. *– *{{ill|Emperor Go-Kōgon|en|Emperor Go-Kōgon|WD=Q555465|lt=Emperor Go-Kōgon}} 1352–1371.Titsingh, pp. 302–309. *{{ill|Emperor Go-En'yū|en|Emperor Go-En'yū|WD=Q471799|lt=Emperor Go-En'yū}} 1371–1382.Titsingh, pp. 310–316, 320. *{{ill|Emperor Go-Komatsu|en|Emperor Go-Komatsu|WD=Q123052|lt=Emperor Go-Komatsu}} 1382–1392 (then went on to reign as legitimate emperor 1392–1412)Titsingh, pp. 317–327. The Imperial Court supported by the Ashikaga ''shōguns'' was rivaled by the Southern Court of Go-Daigo and his descendants. This came to be called the Southern Court because its seat was in a location south of its rival. Although the precise location of the emperors' seat did change, it was often identified as simply {{ill|Yoshino, Nara|en|Yoshino, Nara|WD=Q1203731|lt=Yoshino}}. In 1392, {{ill|Emperor Go-Kameyama|en|Emperor Go-Kameyama|WD=Q451864|lt=Emperor Go-Kameyama}} of the Southern Court was defeated and abdicated in favor of Kōgon's great-grandson, {{ill|Emperor Go-Komatsu|en|Emperor Go-Komatsu|WD=Q123052|lt=Emperor Go-Komatsu}}, thus ending the divide. But the Northern Court was under the power of the Ashikaga ''shōguns'' and had little real independence. Partly because of this, since the 19th century, the Emperors of the Southern Imperial Court have been considered the legitimate Emperors of Japan. Moreover, the Southern Court controlled the Japanese imperial regalia. The Northern Court members are officially called {{ill|Pretender|en|Pretender|WD=Q845929|lt=Pretender}}s. One Southern Court descendant, {{ill|Kumazawa Hiromichi|en|Kumazawa Hiromichi|WD=Q1094280|lt=Kumazawa Hiromichi}}, declared himself to be Japan's rightful Emperor in the days after the end of the Pacific War in {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War}}. He claimed that Emperor {{ill|Hirohito|en|Hirohito|WD=Q34479|lt=Hirohito}} was a fraud, arguing that Hirohito's entire line is descended from the Northern Court. Despite this, he was not arrested for {{ill|Lèse-majesté|en|Lèse-majesté|WD=Q1134057|lt=Lèse-majesté}}, even when donning the Imperial Crest. He could and did produce a {{ill|Koseki|en|Koseki|WD=Q2004750|lt=Koseki}} detailing his bloodline back to Go-Daigo in Yoshino, but his claims and rhetoric failed to inspire anything other than sympathy.Dower, John W. (1999). [https://archive.org/details/embracingdefeatj00dowe/page/306 ''Embracing Defeat: Japan in the Wake of World War II'', pp. 306–307.] ==Southern Court emperors== These are the ''Nanchō'' or Southern Court emperors: *{{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo}} 1336–1339.Titsingh, pp. 281–295; Varley, H. Paul. (1980). ''Jinnō Shōtōki'', pp. 241–269. *{{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|Emperor Go-Murakami|WD=Q440447|lt=Emperor Go-Murakami}} 1339–1368.Titsingh, pp. 295–308; Varley, pp. 269–270. *{{ill|Emperor Chōkei|en|Emperor Chōkei|WD=Q464039|lt=Emperor Chōkei}} 1368–1383.Titsingh, p. 308; Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=chokei ''The Imperial House of Japan'', p. 158.] *{{ill|Emperor Go-Kameyama|en|Emperor Go-Kameyama|WD=Q451864|lt=Emperor Go-Kameyama}} 1383–1392.Titsingh, p. 320. ==Re-unification Agreement== Go-Kameyama reached an agreement with Go-Komatsu to return to the old alternations on a ten-year plan. However, Go-Komatsu broke this promise, not only ruling for 20 years, but being succeeded by his own son, rather than by one from the former Southern Court. == Notes == {{reflist|2}} ==References == {{refbegin}} * Dower, John W. (1999). ''{{ill|Embracing Defeat|en|Embracing Defeat|WD=Q4185382|lt=Embracing Defeat: Japan in the Wake of World War II}}''. New York: [[:en:W. W. Norton|W. W. Norton]]. {{ISBN|978-0-393-04686-1}}; {{OCLC|39143090}} * Mehl, Margaret. (1997). ''History and the State in Nineteenth-Century Japan''. New York: [[:en:St Martin's Press|St Martin's Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-312-21160-8}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/419870136?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 419870136] * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan''.] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * Thomas, Julia Adeney. (2001). ''Reconfiguring Modernity: Concepts of Nature in Japanese Political Ideology''. Berkeley: {{ill|University of California Press|en|University of California Press|WD=Q27816|lt=University of California Press}}. {{ISBN|978-0-520-22854-2}}; {{OCLC|47916285}} * {{in lang|fr}} {{ill|Isaac Titsingh|en|Isaac Titsingh|WD=Q332779|lt=Titsingh}}, Isaac, ed. (1834). ''[[:en:Nipon o daï itsi ran|Nipon o daï itsi ran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] (written by Hayashi Gahō in 1652). Paris: Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. {{OCLC|251800045}} * Varley, H. Paul, ed. (1980). ''A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns: [[Jinnō Shōtōki]]'' (translated from the 1359 [[Kitabatake Chikafusa]] work). New York: Columbia University Press. {{ISBN|978-0-231-04940-5}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/311157159?referer=di&ht=edition OCLC 311157159] {{refend}} {{coord missing|Japan}}]] [[en:Southern Court]] [[ckb:دادگای باشوور (ژاپۆن)]] [[cs:Jižní dvůr]] [[es:Corte del Sur]] [[fa:دربار جنوبی]] [[fr:Cour du Sud]] [[id:Pemerintahan Selatan (Jepang)]] [[it:Corte del Sud]] [[ja:南朝 (日本)]] [[ko:남조 (일본)]] [[pt:Corte do Sul (Japão)]] [[ru:Южный Двор]] [[simple:Southern Court]] [[th:ราชสำนักใต้]] [[tr:Güney Sarayı]] [[uk:Південна династія (Японія)]] [[vi:Nam triều (Nhật Bản)]] [[zh:南朝 (日本)]] {{wikidata link|Q3001082}} [[Category:Former countries in Japanese history]] [[Category:Japanese nobility]] [[Category:Nanboku-chō period|*]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]
